name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 4
text:
Stones looked back at the dark tunnel. "The beast will return," he said.

Greymalkin tensed. "The caravan should know. We need to get back to them."

Stones' hands clenched. "The Waste offers no escape."

Greymalkin hesitated, unsure. "What about the others?"

Stones put a hand on his shoulder. "We find it now, or our people are at risk."

In the silent cave, Greymalkin nodded. They would hunt. Their path was clear, driven by necessity and Stones' determination.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 1
text: Stones and Greymalkin moved carefully along the cliffs. Below, the river reflected a cloudy sky. Every step crunched on loose stones and grass as they searched for the fleeing beast. The wind blew gently as they hunted for signs of the ratboar. Among the rocks and scrub, they stayed alert to the Waste.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 2
text: Stones and Greymalkin checked the oil and prepared the brands. The air was tense. A boar's snort broke the silence. They paused, listening as the sound echoed, confirming the boar was near.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 3
text: The rats were at the edges, making irritating noises. Occasionally, a splash broke the river's surface, ripples hinting at life below. With each step, tension grew, and Stones and Greymalkin prepared for a threat in the mist.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 4
text: Rats emerged from the creature's damaged flesh, falling and scattering. They swarmed, a steady stream over its body like a moving carpet. The air filled with the smell of decay, creating a stark scene of life persisting amidst death.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 5
text: The creature loomed, covered in rat heads with gnashing teeth. A mix of squeals, chittering, and wet chomping noises came from it. Each snap of its jaws made this unsettling sound echo in the chamber. Its breaths were menacing, filling the air with a cycle of fear.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 6
text: The boar charged into the cavern's light, its hoofs clattering on the stone floor. Rats spilled out from under its belly, swarming around. The air buzzed with their movement, signaling the beast's approach as a clear threat.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 7
text: The boar charged with rats swarming around it. Stones swung his weapon but often missed. The noise of steel on stone mixed with roars and squeals. Greymalkin stomped on the rats. The cave echoed with their struggle. Stones' damaged weapon still held up. Instinct and fear pushed them forward into the chaos.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 8
text: Stones swung the hewer with all his strength. The blade cut into the ratboar's hide, but the beast didn't slow down. Each hit met resistance, and the ratboar's squeals echoed in the cavern. The wounds were shallow, barely affecting the creature. Rats poured out and bit at him. Stones' strikes scattered the rats but did little damage. The ratboar pressed forward, unfazed. Stones realized his weapon felt useless.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 9
text: Greymalkin fought with determination. His boots crushed the rats, flattening them. Despite that, they still twitched. His breath was ragged. In a relentless battle against the vermin, sweat dripped from his brow onto the sticky ground, marking each step in his fight to survive.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 10
text: The cavern echoed with the sounds of battle. Squeals and impacts merged into a loud chaos. Greymalkin moved frantically, his feet thudding. Stones hit the ratboar with his hewer, each strike echoing. Blood slicked the ground, making it slippery. The mass of creatures moved energetically, pulling them further into the chaos.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 11
text: The battle continued. Stones swung wildly. Greymalkin slipped on the bloodied stone. The ratboar cried out with the swarm's shrieks. Suddenly, the ratboar retreated into the tunnel. Greymalkin struggled to stand on the wet ground.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 4
text: Stones and Greymalkin stood at the cliff's edge. Below, the broken bridge poles poked out of the water. Memories of their wrecked boat lingered, a reminder of past danger. Shadows lined the remnants, adding unease to the air.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 5
text: The Squealer's call came from the woods. Stones grinned, ready for the hunt. He drew his knife, feeling its solid weight. Adrenaline pumping, he moved with focused intent.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 1
text: Stones and Greymalkin stood at the cliff's edge, their torches lighting the fog over The Restless Waste. They balanced on a narrow ledge where one slip could be fatal. Broken bridge supports jutted from the water. Stones was focused, while Greymalkin dealt with his fear. An unlikely pair—a killer and a scholar, brought together by chance. The ratboar's sounds echoed through Wormapple Wood, unseen but menacing, hidden in the shadows nearby.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 2
text: In the grey daylight, the ratboar appeared. Huge and rat-infested. Its hide was covered in wounds and scabs. Each step sent ripples through its skin. Its mouth, full of snapping rat heads, growled with hunger. A swarm poured out of its body. Its eyes fixed on Stones and Greymalkin, ready to attack.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 3
text:
Greymalkin stood firm, anxiety creeping in. He called out to the beast, "Come here, you swine," trying to provoke it.

Stones' voice was sharp. "Here, pig!" he taunted, wanting a reaction.

The ratboar pawed the ground, hooves ripping into the soil. Muscles tensed, teeth bared; it was ready to charge.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 4
text: The ratboar charged, shaking the ground. Rats poured from its body. Dust rose with each step, and its squeals were loud. Stones held the torch steady, light reflecting off his bald head. Greymalkin looked around, searching for a way out, tension pulling at him.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 5
text: Stones pushed Greymalkin aside as the ratboar charged and fell off the cliff. It hit the bridge supports with a crunch, releasing a swarm of rats.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 6
text: Stones stood at the cliff's edge, lit a burnbottle, and threw it accurately. It hit the beast and erupted into flames. The ratboar struggled as the fire consumed it, its cries echoing briefly before the blaze overtook it and the rats.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 7
text: Silence filled the chasm, interrupted by crackling flames. Greymalkin shook his head, adjusting to the sudden calm. Stones stayed put, steadying himself with a rough rock. The fire's reflection shone in his eyes. He took a breath and spoke, "Can we get back to camp for breakfast? I could use some bacon."

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 1
text:
"I might swim more often in Leper's Branch Lake, if it weren't for the bonefish swimming under it's surface. Damned spikers killed my grandpa, when I threw him in with stones tied around his ankles as a joke."

"Hands off that cup!"

*Wet gurgling sounds.*

"Old Murg sells more or less the same garters as always. Too much the same. He buys his stuff too fresh from the grayve; the worms haven't had the chance to clean the last man's rot from-"

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 2
text: The voices of Middlemoss's most frequented chrypt repeated themselves in the ears of a hunched old man, as he plied a path along the row of columns least-limned in the light of the long fire. "Bonefish... fish... fish..." echoed one. "From the grayve... the grayve... grayve..." came another.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 3
text: The man had heard the voices and their echoes often enough. He passed through the same vault - Middlemoss Last Rest, the town's public house - every day of his adult life. His own tohmb, scarcely more than a carved hollow in the chief crag of the town, could only be reached by passing through The Last Rest. The only way for any traveller to enter Middlemoss proper; Middlemoss, with its repurposed Tohmbs, it's headstones tents, all set under the shadows of high cliffs, with terse brown grass and dead shrub yards; the only passage into this harbor of the living was through the large marble inn chrypt. The old man working his way through it now went entirely unnoticed by the regular partrons. He was the oldest man in town, and its other occupants would no sooner have found his being there out-of-the-ordinary than found the particular whirl on a particular pillar of the marble supports a point of interest. The old man was a part of middle. When he passed - which would be sooner than he might have liked, but not so soon as to be tragic - his ghost would surely keep the regular pilgrimage for many years to come.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 4
text:
Even so, the man took great pains to keep himself inconspicuous. As he skulked, crouching so low to the ground that the hands he was wringing to a so dry as to endanger his sere clothes with their flinty sparking, he mumbled quiet, clipped fragments of sentences:

"Never saw me go. Won't see me come. Hid it nice. No one finds. My best ever treasure. Dug from one hole. Buried in another. Now find a seller. Quiet. No one must find it. My treasure. Buy a better tohmb. Maybe find a mance. Buy a new body."

Thusly punctuated did the pan's silent footsteps carry him from the inn, up the tangled cobblestone roads, to his home's door of barbed iron.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 5
text: As the man opened this barrier of bars with a squeal of metal, stepped through, and squealed it shut behind, he underwent a metamorphosis. Cold comfort though such a portal might have seemed to any ordinary living man, for the old it seemed like a singal of hypnosis. A peace, not unlike contentedness in death, relaxed the muscles on his face. His muttering ceased. He stepped to a candle by a ledge on the wall, lit it, and stared at the reflection of himself in a mirror. He tilted his chin up, then reached for a basin of water to wash some dirt of a grayve off his chin.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 6
text:
So relaxed had the man become that he did not see the deformation of shadow in the corner by the bedslab; not until this deformation had stolen up and shifted behind his shoulder in the reflection. The old man jerked instinctively. He stopped short of turning. He held perfectly still, as he felt the cold, sharp promise of a deep cut, just under the spot of dirt on his chin. In the mirror, the edge of a blade gleamed.

"Don't move," spoke the hooded man who held him, in a deep yet somehow nasally voice. "Don't blink. Don't even breath, except by way of reply. One breath shall stand for yes. Two breaths, for no. Do you understand?"

The man breathed once. Indeed, though the knife had come just slightly off his skin to allow for that breath, the moment he had completed his respiration, it had closed back to his throat - like a door opened only a crack, then slammed shut. To inhale again might amount to suicide.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 7
text:
“I saw where you buried the map," the deep-voiced man went on. "Out of the crevice, up on the mountain. Two rows of headstones rising from the parched heath, like the skin of a mummy, topped with tongue-colored heather fronds waving in the breath of the wind. But I didn't see this: hich grayve did you bury it in? Too dark at night. I don't want to waste my time digging them all. You resurrecter’s will have pulled the wares from any stones under the sky already. Oh dearie me, you're turning purple. Guess I'd better ask a yes-er or a no-er. Now don't lie! Did you bury the map in the westernmost grave in the northmost row of the headstones on the parched heath?”

The old man took two frantic, gasping breaths in.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 8
text: So went the interrogation. The man with the knife asked after each headstone on the parched heath in its turn, sometimes as part of a longer string of words in which he wove in a death threat, sometimes in short form. Then the knife would come away from the old resurrecter's throat, giving him a chance to breath his reply. Each time his gasps came as a pair of twins - no. Then the knife would close once more, that razorous shining edge licking at his wrinkled, soft neck. Fear rattled like a snake through the resurrecter's thoughts, making it difficult to picture each grave marker.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 9
text:
"And what about 'Onion Darling - 37 years, Middlemoss'," Birdsey went on, lifting the knife a fraction. "Three stones from the west on the bottom row?"

The old man drew in one deep rasp, exhaled, then hesitated on the second.

In a flash the knife closed with the throat. In the shadow's reflection on the mirror, teeth flashed in a grin. The shadow tisked. “You aught to be ashamed of yourself. Getting snuck by Killing Birds with Rocks. 'Birdsey' to my friends. My friend Othelmedir’s, he's the linguist. The master mind. And Fogg - well, be glad I’m not Fogg. I’m just the little cook of the crew, though I must give myself credit where credit is due: I do throw a mean stone. You've been had by lowly fodder, you old grayvedigger. ”

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 10
text:
As he spoke, the shadow had unconsciously loosened the razor-edge of the knife from the bared throat. A man of action might have throw his shoulderblades against his soliloquizing antagonist, but the 'old grayvedigger' was by nature a coward. And a fool. Rising to the hooded man's taunt, he said, "Your mushroom food, Birdsey."

"Hmm?" asked Birdsey.

"Map goes to the Foundation. Place for dead. Not living. You'll die."

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 11
text:
Birdsey seemed to ponder this for a moment. He drew his knife away. The old shifted forward and to the side to extricate himself, but a flash of shadow and steel came quicker. The old man felt a flash of pain on his temple. Then the little candle, and the reflection, and consciousness - all vanished.

"Perhaps you're right, he, he," said Birdsey, concluding the thought with two puffs of air that were laughs, the puffs seperated by a distinct space between. Then, "It'd be a shame to cut that old throat now. Firstly, cause you were so obedient - up till that last word. Second, cause you've made a long life on The Mountain of Graves. Ahhh, but Fogg will pull out my fingersnails if I leave you breathing."

So saying, Birdsey cut the old man's breath off, forever.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 12
text:
Some moments after the old man had passed unnoticed through Middlemoss's Last Rest, another shape passed in the opposite direction. While the old man's familiar face might have sparked no interest in the common crowd - had he shown himself - this second shape was a stranger to Middlemoss and its inhabitants. Fortunate for the second shape, then, that it took the same dark corners in its egress as the old man had in his ingress. It passed from Middlemoss, town of Tohmbs in one of The Mountain's many gorges and ravines, and wound a crooked path up to the parched heath - seeking 'friends' and a fresh burial - under the starless black sky in The Time of Dying.

\###

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 1
text: A man with the mien of a redwood tree commenced to kneel to the tiled floor of a long corridor. So proudly stood this man's height over the masonry of the corridor, that it seemed an aeon passed in the span of time it took his left knee join to bend, his right to touch the ground. The two iron-colored eyes set in black skin seemed celestial - like the only pair of stars in an otherwise black Dying sky, making their way to the merely terrestrial stone. In his brought hand, the man cupped a glowing blue ball of thin strands, like a ball of yarn woven from the stuff beneath a fire's coals, and this shed the only light in the dusty passage. When the tall man's right knee finally reached terra-firma, he gently thrust the wisp of light forward along the tiles with his palm. The tiny globe rolled steadily, leaving behind a trail that glowed as it went. The corridor ahead was slowly lighted; shadows danced on the walls from the piles of bones which the ball passed between. Before it reached the end of the darkened passage, the momentum of the ball ceased. It stopped, glowing soft and blue many paces down the passage. The man rose to his feet; eternity passed in the interim.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 2
text:
"I can't figure North from South on this map," said a voice from behind the tall man. Killing Birds with Rocks - Birdsey - stepped into view. Birdsey was a small and wiry man, especially beside the monolith currently occupying the corridor, and especially as he hunched with his sideburns brustling down over an unfurled scroll of leather. "Doesn't look like any catacombs I ever sacked. Othelmedir, are the dead handing out any pointers?"

Othelmedir, the tall man with iron eyes, rubbed a hand through the salt and pepper of his cropped and curled hair. "Not to strangers," he said in answer to Birdsey. "And no siblings or ancestors of mine whisper in the present crowd."

"Waste of time," spoke a third voice, a deep voice, and the body to which that voice surely belonged thundered into the light of the glowing trail. Fogg, the third man, emerged like the ghost of a mammoth. Fogg's skin was alabaster white, sheathing muscles that looked as if they went beyond the scope of natural man. Fogg's clothes were woven with tusks and ribs from a variety of animals (human among them) with each rib or tusk filed down into a serrated shape. Fogg's hair was black, Fogg's eye's were blue. Fogg's two-handed sword swung easily by his hip. Fogg's enormous quadricep, following the direction of his mind as it noticed a brittle skull in the pile of bones nearest the three, crushed the skull to powder.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 3
text:
"Not such a waste," Birdsey countered. He turned so that the glowing trail limned the map more clearly to his eyes. "He, he. If the scratches on this leather usher us to a genuine trove, why, that little bit of grayvediggery was small fare."

Othelmedir raised one sharp eyebrow a fraction of a millimeter, as fog waved his clattering arms in huge circles over the skull he had crushed. The primordial man said, "Waste. Just full-dead here, and dust stirred by marauders. No souls." He glared up at Othelmedir (though Fogg was tall, Othelmedir was yet taller) as if expecting a rebuke.

"As regarding the space over that particular pile of remains, "said Othelmedir, "you are almost certainly accurate, my saber-toothed friend." Othelmedir kept even the smallest hint of sarcasm from his voice and eyes. It would not have served him. Only in his thoughts did he grant himself the pleasure of contempt for Fogg's absurd gesturing.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 4
text: The three men followed the line left by Othelmedir's orb. The trial faded behind them as they moved. Othelmedir bent and picked up his glowing tangle when they came to the intersection where it had stopped. He repeated the maneuver with the next corridor, and the next, and the next. Birdsey carried a lamp, but oil and wood were precious in The Time of Dying; the ball was their chief source of light. Their path carried them into a low-ceilinged vault, lined with huge pillars notched in a hundred alcoves, each alcove containing a skull. The hissing dead-jabber that was common in The Mountain's catacombs - especially the lower catacombs - filled the chamber. Fogg's two-handed sword appeared in his hands as if it had teleported there, and the savage eyed the chamber darkly.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 5
text:
"I hear no words with rotten syllables," said Othelmedir. "No hostile ghost is among the throng around us."

"What are they saying?" asked Birdsey.

"Nothing worth repeating, my stone-throwing associate."

"Slurs? Insults?"

"Most ghosts this dep are too old or mad." Othelmedir shrugged. "Perhaps their language is different. Perhaps, their thinking. I cannot comprehend the words, but I can tell a threat."

"Not a sole shiny disc," Fogg grumbled as he tossed a skull from an alcove and squinted in the vacant space. "No treasures here. It's all been combed."

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 6
text:
"We'll be dragging gold by the sled if the ship's real," said Birdsey. He matched Fogg's displaced head with a stone he threw from his sling - expertly ricocheted off of the inner wall of a pillar-alcove, and knocking the skull loose.

"If."

"The ship in the frozen tomb is spoken of in Middlemoss and in Lower Trestle," said Othelmedir. "By the living and the not. The story is real enough. Finding the old roads drawn in the leather map will be the real body of worms. Finding, and contending with whatever protects the Mailed Flayer's remains."

"Mailed Flayer?" Birdsey ensuing laugh shifted up a nervous octave. "He, He. I never heard about the tohmb's interred."

"A Mail Flayer named Angstlich. Holy and unholy servant of Sang, The Scratcher."

"He, he."

"Stop trembling," said Fogg to Birdsey. "It's just bones ."

"Bones are deadlier than muscle, when they're moving on their own."

"Moving or still, sacred or not, bones can always be crushed." To emphasize his point, Fogg's two-handed sword returned to its sheath, Fogg's one-handed morningstar emerged from its loop, and Fogg's sledgehammer arm crushed another skull in another hollow.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 7
text: Othelmedir marched to the exact middle of four pillars, where his glowing orb had stalled. Fogg's cold blue eyes, Birdsey's narrow ones, and the empty sockets of a score of skulls stared at the tall and thin mancer, underlit in faint sapphire glow by his orb. Othelmedir intoned the same phrase over and over, a language few among the living could understand: "Fehér uj sinonak. Fehér uj sinonak. Fehér uj sinonak."

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 1
text: With Othelmedir's understanding and orb lighting their way, the three walked a short time later into a smaller, round room. The room was an intersection of fifth paths. Four large byways opened into cardinal directions, the three having entered from the east. The fifth way was down. A black, unrailed hole spanned most of the floor in the room, with only a thin ledge of tiles clinging to the wall around it. As Othelmedir leaned over the lip - and he could lean far indeed - the mancer could see a narrow stairway, hardly more than a foot in width, descending well beyond sight.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 2
text: Othelmedir reached out and dropped his orb at the center of this well. It fell like a feather, leaving a midair streak of blue. It was still falling as Othelmedir, then Birdsey, then Fogg set their feet on the narrow descending steps. They started down. As the dropped foot by foot to this lower epoch of the catacombs on The Mountain of Graves, they sensed a change in atmosphere. Any dweller on The Mountain had long ago inundated themselves to the various odors of the various states of decay, from fresh blood, to the first bloated stages of rot, to desiccation. This new odor was not unfamiliar, but certainly uncommon; a smell like the memory of some primordial species of mildew; mildew that had been the apex of the fungal world in an age long past; mildew that ate more than dead matter. The stairs and the walls of the well transitioned from the cold grey of bland stone, to a fuzzy whitish texture of dead moss, to coal-black, covered in soot, and deeply shelved. The stone became more like the stone of a cave than a crypt, jagged and roughly-hewn, and covered in the dust of ancient bones.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 3
text: How long they slid carefully down that well, hugging its walls, would be difficult to determine. Perhaps less than a round of a clock's thinnest hand. Perhaps days. Space and minutes were mutant things in that Time of Dying. Devices for measurement were rare - accurate measurement, rarer - and people told time by the passage of the staring sun on the mountains surface, or by counted footsteps in its guts. The dead-chatter of the upper halls had faded to silence during the descent. Now, there only echoed the footsteps and occasional quips of the three traversers: muted, stillborn echoes, reeling languorously up and down the vertical shaft like a hooked worm on a fishing pole.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 1
text:
"Look now," said Birdsey, his echo slowly moving away below and above him. "Othelmedir, your magic lamp has been only a fleck in the black for long moments, with its string of light reaching behind it. God of this sepulcher be praised, I think it grows in size at last."

We've descended many hundreds of years," said Othelmedir.

"The Foundation?"

"So it would seem." The cant of Othelmedir's words broadened as the three stepped down the last round of the stairs in a more open space. The floor was flat, unmortared stone. The walls showed some working. Huge iron stakes, covered in flaking rust were driven sporadically into the various rock faces, in clusters of eight. What significance these marked had long been lost to history. The stakes represented the only objects of value in the immediate borough of The Mountain's endless catacombs; and even Fogg, a marble sculpture of raw sinew and muscles though he was, could not hope to pull the rusted nails from the hold of the stone.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 2
text:
"We're unlikely to run into looters," Birdsey mused, as Othelmedir lifted his orb and picked a passage to follow. "These cavities might be a thousand years old; the looting of them, a hundred years."

The last word of each of Birdsey's sentences reverberated ahead of them in the silence. "Looters... looters... looters... Years... years... years..."

Fogg said, "Be nice to have a donkey."

"Donkey... donkey... donkey..."

Birdsey cast the pale giant a puzzled glance. "What for?"

"For... for... for..."

"We could bring more of this boat treasure out in a single journey. That was the way of older generations of chryptsackers."

"Journey... journey... journey... Sacker... sacker... sacker..."

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 3
text:
"Othelmedir," said Birdsey, turning back to the mancer. "Will there be wards on this mailed flayer's tohmb?"

"Tohmb... tohmb... tohmb..."

Othelmedir had been walking with his eyes closed, speaking his foreign language quietly, without opening his lips. Now the iron eyes opened, looked back at his comrades, and saw the nerves written on Birdsey's face. "Keep your eyes open for mechanical traps, Birdsey," he said. "I will ensure we cross over no rune or curse."

"Birdsey... birdsey... birdsey... Curse... curse... curse..."

All of these latest echoes - since the three had reached the bottom of the black well - seemed to spend a very long time in dying, as if the trio could follow them as fast as the echoes could move away. As well, these repetitions seemed to take on a tone - an emotion - of their own as they progressed. Though Othelmedir had spoken the words "Birdsey" and "Cursed" with no particular inflection, each successive echo changed the emphasis of the word. "Birdsey", when it finally died, died with note of mockery. "Cursed" faded like the last gasp of spite from the lips of a dying man. Othelmedir's words, calculated to put his anxious ally at ease, were thus undercut.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 4
text: They entered and exited several more length of corridor which contained nothing of note, and in which none spoke. The next moment of intrigue for Birdsey came when they entered a corridor where the walls had been more-finely chiseled. As Othelmedir's ball rolled down the length of this hall, shedding its blue line, it revealed ten perfectly-spaced alcoves, five alcoves on each side. Small holes in the walls were common enough, but these holes were too small and too low to the ground to service as recepticles for the remains of the dead. Othelmedir held a palm high in warning. They stepped cautiously up to a few yards before the first set of niches. Othelmedir bent with torturous slowness to the niche on the left. Birdsey did the same on the right. Each hollow contained a simple pewter plate. The plates were brown, undamaged, and probably shiny, though a layer of dust precluded any reflection.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 5
text:
"I'll be slit," Birdsey exclaimed, his last word echoing slowly away. "Unsacked treasure. He, he. We're getting somewhere."

"Don't touch it," Othelmedir warned as Birdsey reached for the face.

"Cursed? Warded? Will they prevent our crossing?"

"The plates will not stop our passage. Btu see that there are ten niches; ten long-lasting brown plates. In such arrangements, one palte is always cursed. There is no way to know which."

Birdsey's shoulder's sagged. "Guess we'd better keep going," he said. Othelmedir resumed his lengthy stride.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 6
text: Fogg meanwhile had refused to even look at the place. Something in his primitive sensibility had been repulsed by the careful arrangement of porcelain. His instinct had been simply to smash one of them. Hearing Othelmedir's explanation, he simply grunted and fell in line behind the mancer. They hurried past the ten alcoves, feeling watched by the ageless pewter.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 1
text:
No sooner had they reached Othelmedir's orb, however, than the mancer held his palm up oncem ore. He knelt, swiftly this time, and Birdsey could see the excited gleam in a corner of the iron eyes. Othelmedir brushed his palm over the floor.

"Bend and look," he said. The others did so. "See. There is no mortar, but the stone is lightly carved into hexagons. At each corner of each hexagon, three scratches. Innumerable scratches across the floor. We have crossed a threshhold into some burgh committed to Sang. Let us hurry."

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 2
text: Othelmedir continued to push the ball with the price movement along each corridor, but a continual apprehension now lacquered the whites of both his eyes, and as they followed the line, he continually rubbed a hand through his short hair. At last they saw the glow of the rolling ball spread into a wider chamber, and presently reflect off something on the wall. They hurried ofrward. They entered a hexagon room under an arch in the corridor. On the face of the hexagon opposite to them, a set of huge, bronze double-doors gleamed in the blue light. Thousands of scratchmarks raked its surface, marring the brown-yellow sheen like open sores; as if some metal-rending nails had scraped desperately against it. Stepping closer, they found no handles or locks.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 3
text:
"Is this the tomb?" asked Othemedir.

Birdsey unfurled the leather scroll they had stolen. He scanned the markings, then raised his eyes to the cieling as he reflected on the recent turns they had taken. Finally he shrugged, and said, "We have followed the map for some minutes. These two round dots at the edge of the map may have been the plates. It seems like the tohmb. How do we open it?"

Fogg grunted. He stepped up to the door. He did stop and stare at Othelmedir before touching the scarred copper. The latter raised no objection. Fogg slammed a palm on each door and strained. The striations stood out like an azure spiderweb under the pale skin of his arms. He strained silently against the door for several moments, shifted, set his back against the copper and pressed with his mutant legs. The door moved not a mouse's hair. At last, Fogg came away. He stood, caught his breath, and shook his head. "It can't be forced," he said.

"Search for a mechanism," said Othelmedir. "I will ask the dead."

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 4
text:
Birdsey immediately cast his eyes over the six walls of the room. Each of the walls was covered in icons of giant horseflies, carved directly into the stones. The four walls which did not support the bronze doors or the arch by which they'd entered each had a gaint, curling symbol of  a worm embossed on it. Birdsey went to the nearest. He ran his hand over the worm's loops.

“Fehér uj sinonak," Othelmedir chanted softly the same phrase from before. “Fehér uj sinonak élérntés."

Fogg knelt and stirred the dust of the floor with his fingers. It had an altered texture to the dust of the corridors. It felt familiar, in a way that Fogg could not remember. Thin, and fragile, like dry filo flakes."

"Nothing on the reliefs," said Birdsey. He looked up. "The cieling's dark, but it looks smooth."

"Keep looking," said Othelmedir.

Fogg looked up from the dust to the huge motif of the worm beside Birdsey. He squinted, then drew in a hissing breath. "Wormskin," he said.

"What?" asked Othelmedir.

"The dust is shed skin. Like a snake or a worm."

Othelmedir only nodded and closed his eyes again, resuming the call to the dead. Birdsey shifted and said, " He, he. I suppose worms are sacred to all the gods."

"Something is wrong in this room," Fogg went on slowly. He stepped away from the center of the chamber and ran a hand along the surface of another carved worm-panel. "I feel it in the jungle of my bones. I-"

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 5
text: But something suddenly hissed as Fogg set his palm against the worm relief. He jumped back, Othelmedir's eyes popped open, Birdsey spun. The hiss went on, and all three turned to the double bronze doors, from whence it eminated.

name: 6. The Master Appears - Paragraph 1
text:
A perfect black seam opened between the scratches on the bronze. The crack widened. Othelmedir took a pace back, and Fogg's two-handed sword swung from its sheath as the hiss rose to a wail like a banshee. All three put a hand to their faces, both as a ward again the sharp wind, and against the texture-stench of the ancient air. Dust, with the smell of a long-vacant wood cabin, and the chill of stone rubbed raw by a glacier, blasted against their skin.

When the blast of cold air finally faded, the three lowered their hands.

name: 6. The Master Appears - Paragraph 2
text:
The bronze doors yawned before them. Othelmedir bent and picked up his light. The wine-black walls of a tunnel, curving down and to the the left, shone brilliantly in the light of Othelmedir's orb; their surfaces rendered dustless by the breath of the tohmb.

"Fogg," said Othelmedir. "Take the front." The pale giant grunted and stepped toward the tunnel.

But just they were taking their step toward the door which had expelled its hiss, a second hiss - like an echo, but far to present and close - sounded. Something thudded on the floor behind them.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 1
text:
Fogg reacted the swiftest. He had not taken more than a step toward the door; he was nearest to the thumping sound, and halfway round before it had reached the ears of his comrades. The other two reacted more slowly, but not much more.

They all saw that one of the reliefs on the wall had been the source. Specifically, the worm within the relief. This had been one of the two that neither Fogg nor Birdsey had touched. Perhaps they might have recognized that the relief was not a relief - not an embossed, enormous worm set within the carved wall - but an organism. A living (or dead) thing, come to enormous animation as it unfurled on the floor before them. It's underside had been the color of the wall, but the sides and back of the worm were a rust-colored leather, with bony protrusions like the spin of a lizard covering its surface.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 2
text: The creature was enormous. Swift as Fogg and Birdsey and Othelmedir had reacted, yet the creature had already untangled its great mass before any could do more than gape at its size. It's faceless front was thicker than the trunk of a sturdy pine, and rose so high that it brushed the ceiling six feet overhead, and sent curtains of dust and cobwebs floating down around it.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 3
text:
Fogg's coiled muscles launched him, like a triggered jawtrap snapping. The worm reeled back from the swing of Fogg's two-handed sword, but Fogg's two-handed sword clove the air with the speed of a moor cat. The blade slammed the side of the worm; it's upper mass swayed sideways under the weight of the blow; the chamber shuddered as it struck the wall. But the blade did not cut.

Birdsey meanwhile had knocked and whipped a stone into motion with his sling. A whistled punctured the silent heaving of the spined worm as the stone rent the air. It thudded into the stone-like leather of the monster with no more effect than Fogg's sword.

The worm-monster's enormous body whipped out with a speed that seemed impossible for a creature of it's size - it's mass seemed to engulf the room. Fogg brought his sword up to block. The worm slammed against the enormous pale fighter like a collapsing stone wall, knocking him back against the actual stone and pinning him.

Othelmedir had been stunned to torpor by the sudden violence. Now he wagged his head, clearing the fog, and studied the worm as it squeezed against Fogg. Othelmedir had only a small knife for cutting his bread - worthless against this mega-invertebrate - but he did recongize the creature. "It's a leatherwyrm," he called. "Stab it. Don't slash."

Fogg's two-handed sword was pinned with his arm, however, the worms bulk crushing them against the stone. The savage tried to reach his other weapon, but the bulk of the monster covered it waste.

"What it want?!" Birdsey yelled. He whipped another stone to no effect.

Othelmedir replied, "Rust and moss from tohmbs. But it will eat Fogg. It'll crush him and lay it's slime atop his corpse."

Suddenly the worms back-end rose from the ground and swung. Birdsey screamed. He let the stone he had been whirring fall from his sling. He dove sideways, just missing a blow that would have lain him breathless against the floor. He was up in moment, beside Othelmedir, and tugging the towering mancer toward the newly-opened door.

"Fogg's dead," said Birdsey - and indeed, Fogg's pale face was at that moment turning a deep purple as the worm pressed the breath from his lungs. "Inside, and shut the doors."

"No-" Othelmedir began. He felt no special grief for the loss of Fogg (beyond his value as a fighter). He worried only that they were trapping themselves in a tohmb with only one exit. Birdsey was beyond terror, however, and only yanked Othelmedir harder toward the door.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 4
text: Fogg, however, seemed at that moment to have second thoughts about dying. With a savage cry and a surge of corded arms he pressed the worm's bulk off him. Not for long, and not by much. But in the second of time he took a huge breath. Fogg's two-handed sword also shifted in the time. He angled it so that, as the worm pressed in again, it's own force drove it along the razor edge. The creature let out a vibration that might have been pain. It reeled back, a small cut showing on it's body. Fogg neither pressed the attack nor held his place. At once when he was freed he darted for the door, gasping to catch his breath. He heard the worm behind him just as he crossed the threshhold. He spun, bringing his weapon up in a block.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 5
text: Birdsey had pause at the door. He now tried to slam one of the heavy bronze doors. He abandoned the effort and had to fall back, however, as the leatherwyrm threw itself against the door's stone casing. Fogg's two-handed sword came nowhere near to blocking the worm's mass, for the worm came up short of the weapon. Its entire bulk slammed the partially open door and its frame. A crack of stone resounded. A shudder shook the tunnel. "Back!" yelled Othelmedir.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 6
text: The three chryptsackers fell back as rubble and dust crashed in curtains over the shape of the leatherwyrm framed in the doorway. The frame buckled. Thunder like the heart of a storm boomed in their ears as the three stumbled down the curving slop. Othelmedir's light flickered as great plumed of dust blew past and all around them. Then there was one final, terrific crash like a gong, and a loose piece of cieling rock blasted Othelmedir in the shoulder. The light winked out in a instant, and all three were plunged into a cold and storming blackness.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 1
text: The groaning of the stonework faded in starts and stops, like the death-throes of a strong-limbed bull-ox when an axe is driven through it's neck. Amidst the darkness and the dust, men coughed and sputtered. Finally, Birdsey's voice softly spoke: "Who's living still?"

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 2
text:
The reply came as another groan, this time in the deep, scratched voice of Othelmedir, somewhere to Birdsey's left. Then Fogg barked from just beside Birdsey, "I'm alive."

"Othelmedir, are you hurt?"

Another groan sounded, then Othelmedir said hoarsely, "My shoulder's smashed. I'm up though."

"Conjure a globe so-" Fogg began. A fit of coughing dust interrupted him, before he resumed, "So we can take stock."

"I can't- can't focus. The pain, agh."

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 3
text:
Another rumble shook the floor. Then the blackness seemed to fall still for good. Fogg said, "The air feels cool and open before me. Birdsey, help the mancer, then grab the back of my leathers. I'll pick a path."

Birdsey stumbled over some loose rocks to where he heard Othelmedir breathing heavily. He groped until he felt a sharp elbow. He let the tall man set a long-fingered hand around his shoulder, then led the way back to Fogg, guided by a clicking sound the latter was making in imitation of a bat. Birdsey caught hold of the collar of Fogg's leather coat, and with the savage leading they descending along the sloping corridor.

Fogg found the wall soon enough, and after the three had stumbled past the debri of the collapsed door, they felt the travel easy enough, even blinded. Othelmedir's breathing steadied, and he began to walk again unsupported. They all felt the chill in the air. They shivered, and surely their breath misted before each, though they could not see it.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 4
text: As they went blindly on, however, they found that they COULD see the result of each exhalation. Their eyes adjusted to the darkness, but how was that too be in a sepulcher completely without light. They slowly understood that a thin vapor was shedding a soft glow in the tunnel, which continued to curve gradually but inexorably down. As they descended the vapor, which must have been heavier than air, thickened into an even brighter glow; not so bright as the sun, yet brighter than the yellow light of a full harvest-moon, and teal in hue.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 5
text: The tunnel had given the three enough space to walk abreast. Now it widened to five meters form wall-to-wall, then to six, then they saw ahead that it opened out broadly. Othelmedir held up his good arm with a flat palm. They proceeded slowly, as they saw a strange glimmering new texture of the floor where the tunnel terminated. They realized, as they stepped out and into the wide space, that this glimmer was the shine of the teal vapor off of a floor of ice. They had entered a huge, frozen reservoir, deep in the catacombs, and at it's heart was a frozen ship.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 6
text: A long vessel of three masts, tarred and tallowed to watertightness, sat locked in the center of the frozen pool. Blue-white powder urned every board and line of the ship. The double-set of rowlock holes on the side that faced the three were frozen over with a draping curtain of icicles. Two more enormous icicles, oddly symmetrical and perfect, dangled over the ship from the roof of the resevoir, nearly even at their tips with the crow's-nest of the center. The ship seemed to the three more an abstract realization of a ship than a concrete one; a vessel in service to one of the seven dead gods, bearing souls for the deity's own malign ends; so silent did it sit, frosted and still in the middle of the teal fog. The silence was not complete, but crackled low and continually. The air tasted antiseptic.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 7
text:
"Guess this is it, he, he," said Birdsey. He glanced at Othelmedir for confirmation; the mancer's iron eyes shone like steel as they stared across the frozen expanse.

"Seems frozen through," said Fogg, stamping a foot on the ice.

Othelmedir said, "Let us cross."

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 8
text:
Birdsey happened to glance down as they stepped carefully over the frozen pool. He squinted, bent and wiped a hand through the light spackle of frost that covered the surface, then started up and back. "There's dead below," he said.

Fogg and Othelmedir turned and saw where he was looking. Othelmedir unclasped his long cape and swept it about. He uncovered, lying hardly a foot below, locked in near-perfectly transparent crystal, half-a-dozen frozen men. They were entirely composes, without a bit of rot scarring their upturned, pale faces; faces frozen in the last moments of terror and death; lifeless eyes, wide, and seeming to follow the three as they moved.

Othelmedir whispered something, then said, "Their souls are gone, at least."

"Let's get to the ship," said Fogg.

As they progressed through the mist, Birdsey spotted something else. "Look there," he said. "On the far wall, I see another opening. He, he, we are not trapped at least."

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 9
text:
The side of the frozen ship presented a small obstacles when they reached, for the frosted surface was smooth and outward-facing. Fogg overcame this by start up where there were gas in the frosted-over rowholes, then chiseling grooves in the wood and ice with a knife. He reached the gunnels and climbed over. The two heard a quick intake of breath.

"What do you see?" asked Othelmedir.

"Treasure?" asked Birdsey?

In response, Fogg threw a knotted line of rope over the edge. Birdsey climbed easily, and together he and Fogg hauled the mancer, whose shoulder would be long in healing.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 1
text: The three stopped and stared. The deck was barren - covered in the same rime of frost - except for a few pieces of coiled rigging, and except for three thickly-frozen rings around the three masts. The rings were each under a thick but translucent layer of pure ice. Under the ice, hexagonal, golden coins lay piled. Birdsey's eyes especially seemed to reflect the sparkling treasure. Othelmedir, after the sparkle at drawn his first attention, paid closer mind to the masts themselves. They were not sawn and carved pieces of tumber, but birch trees, whole birch trees complete with bark, and with thin, sere sails hanging from their branches. Fogg saw the masts too. He noticed also that, on the deckboards surrounding the masts and their three rings of treasure, scratches had been dug through the wood. The gouges were deep enough that the patina of did not conceal them.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 2
text:
"By The Scratcher's bloody loins," said Birdsey with a whistle, "that's a finer pile than ever my mind imagined. Who'd have thought a trove like this'd still lie completely untouched, with only some rotten worm to guard it? I wonder if there boxes down in the hold? We could pile these golden hexes in, and slide 'em across to the opposite tunnel. Blast me, but this air's colder than a hag's tit."

Othelmedir said, "The frost is a curse or enchantment of some sort."

"Not a bit of it. Five of those golden pieces says there's some crystal machine lying around.

Othelmedir did not answer. He had noticed the door to what would have been the captain's quarters he walked across to it. He tried the knob, but it was frozen stiff, and the door would not open. He glanced back at Fogg.

The door exploded into fragments when Fogg's two-handed sword smote it's face.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 3
text: Within, sitting at a table with his acolytes arranged around him, they beheld the Mailed Flayer. The servant of Sang. All were frozen stiff; their faces pale as ghosts and marked with thousands of small, cat-like scratches; their eyes closed as if in prayer. The acolytes wore black robes with red sashes. The Flayer wore a steel cuirass gilt in silver, and had a three-headed scourge splayed on the table before him. He looked to have been about two-hundred years of age, when the magic of the reservoir stopped his passing years in-perpetuity. More gold, fine tableware of silver and bronze, jeweled weapons, and other valuable treasures were frozen to the surface of the table, or stacked upon the various shelves. Othelmedir's iron eyes locked on one shelf in particular, where he saw stacked half-a-dozen rolled scrolls of leather.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 4
text:
At this scene of the profanely pious frozen before him, Birdsey turned stiff as the frozen boards on which he stood. Othelmedir recognized the superstitious workings in Birdsey's brain, however, and said, "They are true-dead. I would have heard the voice of a servant of Sang the instant we entered the tomb, were his soul stuck here yet."

Birdsey relaxed. "Is there a necromancy to break through ice?" he asked.

"None that I am proficient in."

"Come on, Fogg." Birdsey spun back out of the door and walked to the rail where they had left the knotted rope. "I'm going out onto the lake. I'll look for this freezing-machine; it's got to be in the water. You try to crack one of those ice-rings around the masts."

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 5
text: The pommel of Fogg's two-handed sword let out a ringing note that rang over the ice and resounded within the chamber, as he banged it against one of the ice rings. Birdsey  scampered deftly down the side of the ship. He then began wide arcs upon the ice, sweeping frost, shuddering whenever he met the upturned eyes of a body, searching for the means by which the chamber was kept encased.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 6
text: Othelmedir had remained in the cabin with the frozen Flayer. He drifted toward the rolls of leather on the shelf. The flimsy teal light from the frost-coated window cast his tall shadow over the scrolls as he picked them up and unfurled them. The TINK, TINK, TINK, of Fogg's two-handed sword sounded faintly from without. Some of the leathers Othelmedir looked at were only instructions for rites sacred to The Scratcher. Others were perhaps more useful, having carved upon their surfaces The Words with Rotten Syllables. While his narrow eyes sifted the cracking leather, however, Othelmedir's thoughts hung upon the outer chamber. That the frost was not the result of crystalline machinery, he fully believed. He had read of this kind of enchantment, and Othelmedir now cast his mind back into untitled books for an answer. The cold in this cave was, he understood, brought about by the captured ghost of a terrible winter. A whole terrible year of weather's ghost, he realized, for he suddenly the name of The Blizzard that Flayed the Mountain; and he recalled that the winter of that blizard, recorded centuries and centuries past, had been a year of a terrible Spring blooming, a baking summer; and of course all autumns were dead on The Mountain of Graves. Still, Othelmedir understood that now the captured ghost of that year was waxing in winter, here in this tohmb, now.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 7
text:
Whiler Othelmedir pondered, Fogg's TINK, TINK, TINK, continued to resound dully in the back of his mind. Birdsey had reached the wall of the reservoir, and was cirling toward the second exit with his sweeping pattern.

Othelmedir needed some way to trigger the coming of spring, if he was to thaw the ice and treasure. What could signal a change in season, he wondered? He shivered in the cold; a grisly think to see in a man so lean and tall, too swift, like a hummignbirds flitting wings.

The thought flashed before Othelmedir: water. All was crusted and frozen in this chamber, but a sprinkling of some kind might trigger the change he desired. Othelmedir considered how to bring this about for some minutes. He nearly laughed when he thought of it, but didn't, for he never laughed without purpose. He made as if to spit, but stopped himself. If the environment of the reservoir were as terrible as this, in dead winter, what unpredictable scene would the onset of spring invoke? What storm would the malicious ghost of the year enact?

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 8
text:
The steady TINK, TINK, TINK sounded sharply and more clearly, at that instant, through Othelmedir's pondering. He spun, strode to the door. He looked over to see Fogg still hammering at the ice with his pommel. The position was awkward, the work difficult, and the man was breathing heavily.

"Fogg, stop," said Othelmedir.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 9
text: Fogg stopped and turned to look at the mancer. Before Othelmedir could shout a warning, the pale man raised a pale hand to his long black hair, wiped a translucent bead of sweat from his brow, and flicked the moisture to the ship's deck.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
Lightning flashed, and thunder cracked a signal boom.

The teal mist disgorged a sharp stinging rain.

Within the flash, the ice pool melted.

The water was a roiling sea.

The two stalactites unfroze.

Two metal conductors.

Lightning arced, waves crashed.

Fogg's arms windmilled.

One bolt searched.

Saw Fogg.

Struck.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 2
text: Miraculously, as Fogg stood in place - swaying, smoking, eyes glossy - the direction of his leaning perfectly counteracted the sudden rolling and swaying of the deck beneath him. He remained perfectly upright. Birdsey, meanwhile, had been standing by the second exit when the season shifted. He jumped for one rough-hewn pillars of stone girting the tunnel mouth. Now he screamed, and clung desperately to the stone, as the rain beat his head and shoulders, and the waved hammered against his spine. A howling wind spun through the air, stirring every atom of water into motion. From a scene which had seemed near to absolute zero, so still and entropic had it been, the reservoir had become a place as violent as the heart of any ocean hurricane. The ship was a feather, dancing in a billowing chimney.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 3
text: Othelmedir had tried to catch his balance when the storm struck. He'd grabbed for the rail, but had grabbed unconsciously with his injured, dominant arm. Now one specially heavy swell smashed into the port side of the ship. The deck reversed angles entirely. Othelmedir shouted in pain as his grip on the banister broke. The tall man fell like a tumbling sequoia toward the gunnel and the churning foam beyond. Fogg had regain his sensed by that moment. He saw Othelmedir's fall. He leapt out over the swaying deck. The huge man caught Othelmedir' waist in one arm, just as the latter fell over the rail. Hold Tight to Othelmedir's robe Fogg just managed to encase the wooden lip of the rail in his knuckles. The ship bucked, they slammed against the side. Othelmedir nearly passed out from the impact, but shook the blackening tunnel vision away and grabbed Fogg's drenched collar.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 4
text: There they held, while the rain and waves thrashed. The piled gold had scattered in the impact. Yellow sparks flashed over the sides of the ship, twinkling in the air, or sinking in the water like fallen stars. Othelmedir felt his grip begin to slip. His shoulder throbbed. Another waved slammed the pair against the boars; Fogg gritted his teeth and tightened his grasp on the rail, nearly splintering the wood.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 5
text: Without a osund, the savage muscled warrior heaved with every muscle in his back and  arms. He pulled first his chest up over the trail, the flung Othelmedir back onto the deck. The judder of the impact sent new flashed of pain along the mancer's shoulder. Fogg lifted himself up after, then slid down low and pulled himself and Othelmedir up against the angle of the rail and the deck. He hooked an elbow through a baluster, then a foot. Across the water, by the exit, Birdsey had meanwhile gained a firmer spot on the rock. Together and seperately, all three receded as much as they could into themselves, bunchign their necks and tucking their limbs to be as small as possible, while the ocean-sized mass of the storm railed in the too-small enclosure of the Mailed Flayer's tohmb.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 1
text:
Long were the rain and the waves in their waning. Never did they recede completely, for the ghost of that Year of the Seasons Terrible held to its spiteful condition through Spring as well as Winter. The initial wrath of the weather faded, however, until the rain only poured instead of bit; and until the Wind was only a constant moan, not a mastication of a gaseous jaw; and until the waves were high dunes of whitecapped water that, nevertheless, held a steady rhythm. Fogg rose, arms aching from the strain of holding himself and Othelmedir in place. Othelmedir, despite the throbbing in his whole arm, managed to stain too, supported on the rocking deck by the rail. In the comparative lull a voice could be heard if it shouted; and they heard Birdsey shouting now, "Sang's bloody loins. I thought the water would smash by bones to jelly. You out there, still full-alive?"

"Not slain yet," said Fogg in reply.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 2
text:
"Looks like this tunnel goes up. Any gold left?"

Fogg scanned the deck. "Some scattered pieces."

"Grab them and come across."

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 3
text:
Othelmedir took a deep breath, drawing his mind back from flaring pain in his shoulder. He urealized that at least some bone was probably fractured. They would need to find a skilled leech when they returned to the surface - or a better mancer than he - to correct the injury. If they returned, he mentally corrected himself. He forced his thoughts to gather on the present. "We should check the cabin," he said. "More of that wealth has likely stayed aboard. Birdsey."

"Yes?"

"Is there a boat up your tunnel?"

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 4
text:
There was a pause - Fogg stagged on the churning boat to the captain's quarter, scooping up some scattered hexes of gold as he went. Presently Birdsey called back, "No boat. Those frozen corpses are floating now though. Make a raft of em, maybe? He, he."

"Don't be an imbecile," Othelmedir snapped back. "We'll take some wood off this craft. Fogg?"

Fogg grunted. "That captain's table will suit as a raft. I'll-"

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 5
text: But Fogg fell silent as, from the direction of the masts, they a heard a gouging, rending, wailing sound of scraping.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 1
text:
Fogg and Othelmedir turned in the direction of the masts, from whence the sound had come. Fogg's eyes screwed into anrrowed promised of violence, and he swore without sound: "Hell's spit."

Each of the three birch-tree masts had begun to move. Not 'move' in conjunction with the ship on the water, but of their own accord. Their birch-tree roots, when had benn tapped down and nailed between the deckboard were pulling free, tearing nails out with them. The branches, from which hung the ragged sails, swooped through the air. The two watched as one of the trees swept a free root out across the deck. It gathered up two-score loose hexes of gold, and pulled them back into a small arc around it's old posthole.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 2
text: Othelmedir recognized the false ships' masts in a moment. These were Birchmen. Once there had been a forest on The Mountain of Graves, long before it's graves sprouted. A birch forest, whose limbs grew with green leaves, and whose trunks grew alabaster with beauitful dark spots, not the skeletal, leafless, leperous trees that sprouted fully-grown and crooked and dead from the mountain's soil now. But that ancient forest of many thousand years ago had been sawn down in its time. The spirits of those trees had never forgiven the offense. They haunted their sawn trunks forever after; Birchmen, trees that hated and moved. If Othelmedir's knowledge was right, they were hardy things, though sightless. Carefully and slowly, Othelmedir waved to catch Fogg's attention. He held a finger to his lips.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 3
text: Fogg tracked the sweeping of the aft mast's long roots with his black eyes for a moment longer. The masts began to shuffle across the decking, however, and he saw that he must move or eventually be caught in its path. He began to pick a route to Othelmedir through the scattered spackle of yellow on the deck. The rain poured on, the ship rocked under them. Othelmedir held perfectly still at the rail, watching his companion move back toward him from the door of the cabin. The Birchmen ranged farther about. Fogg stopped at a fallen line of rigging, which spanned the deck, and coiled in a pile at the bow side. He picked up the rop at its end. He continued to sway carefully over the shifting pieces of coin, meanwhile knotting his end of the rope into a loop, by which he meant to tie a harness where they might descend the ship's side in silence. All this time the three Birchmen roved up and away from their original holes in the decking. Though each continued to sweep the scattered gold back into rings, the continuing waves only undid their work.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 4
text: "What's taking?" Birdsey's cry rang out to the ship through the his of rain and sliding coins. One of Birchmen instantly swiveled, the tattered sails on its limbs fluttering in the sudden motion. With alarming speed (any speed was alarming to see in a tree) the things rolled itself to the rail upon its writhing tangle of roots. Othelmedir and Fogg held still as a painting. Othelmedir went into his mind. He found some words of Deadspeech, anbd made them soundlessly with his lips and tongue. Though his voice made no noise, Birdsey - who was standing by the mouth of the tunnel and squinting through the downpour - heard the mancer's ghastly deadvoice in his mind: "Keep utterly silent!" He did so, and waited. On the ship, Fogg and Othelmedir watched as the alerted Birchman gradually moved back toward the pile of gold.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 5
text: Fogg quickly but quietly moved over the remainder of the rain-washed deck, reaching Othelmedir by the rail. He pulled the rope in a loop over his head and shoulder, and pulled some slack in to wrap about the rail. They both leaned out and looked over. The churning water seemed far, far beneath them from the top of the deck. They glanced at one another. Fogg shrugged; there was no sense in waiting. Othelmedir nodded. Fogg went to wrap the rapelling line around on of the balusters.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 6
text: Behind them, unnoticed, one of the Birchmen's roots slithered over the remaining slack in the line. It pulled. Fogg fell the rope move on his chest. He grabbed to pull the loop free, but was a second to late. The knot pulled tight, the line pulled taught, and Fogg was yanked off his feet. The giant marble savage hit the deck with an enormous boom.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
The rain fell as steady static over the boards of the deck of the ship of the Mailed Flayer, while the swells of the reservoir pulsed with rhythm against it's port. Fogg - lying on his back - and Othelmedir at the rail, squinted through the storm at the quivering birch trees. They breath stopped in their lungs - their shoulders bunched - in that moment of the storm which seemed to draw itself out.

The trees released the pressure with a scuttle. All three towering Birchmen hurtled toward Fogg. Fogg's two-handed sword was free of it's sheath in a whistle of movement. He cut the slackened rope attaching them to the tree, he jumped to his feet.

Like scarecrows caught in a tornado the Birchmen seemed, with their tattered sail swaying around them as they hurtled forward. The air felt charged as another lightning bolt dropped from the metal stalgtites - the scene flashed briefly lurid. The nearest tree came up just short of where Fogg stood ready. Then it whipped a root at him.

Fogg made a single-handed cat to meet the tentacle-like appendage, at the same time he used his free arm to bully Othelmedir away further along the railing. Othelmedir followed the momentum of the shove, trying to keep quiet as he hobbled back from the charging trees. At the same time he managed another soundless mental whisper of deadspeech at Fogg: "They're Birchmen. Too strong. Keep away!"

From off by the exit, Birdsey called out, "What's going on out there?"

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 2
text: Fogg's chop, even with a single arm, had severed the whipping root entirely. The tree seemed not to care. It barreled down on him with it's trunk. Fogg took up his weapon in both hands then. While retreating, he began to chop, chop, chop at the trunk and the mass of writhing roots below it. Wood chips fell with the rain. Then Fogg's two-handed sword struck to deep. As he tried to pull it back, he found it wedged in the wood. The Birchman pressed in on him; the other two following close. Fogg released the hilt and stumbled back, the space between himself and the wall of the forecastle growing smaller with each step. He drew his morning, though he doubted he could inflict much damage to the thick old wood of the Birchmen.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 3
text: Othelmedir had managed to escape the notice of the trees. He stood near the cabin door, trying to block out Birdsey's cries and the drum of the rain, trying to focus. How could he affect another season change? How could he force the ghost of that terrible year into Summer? Fogg couldn't fight the waterlogged Birchmen, certainly not on a rocking ship in a storm. But if they oculd dry the trees out, perhaps the mosters could be burned. What had made that summer baking in The Year of Seasons Terrible? An endless sun - that was what it had been. A sun... Othelmedir nearly snapped his finger. He focused, pushed pain and external distraction away. He focused, and recited some careful words of Deadspeech.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 4
text: In the mancer's palm, a yellow yarnball of light swelled.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 5
text: A sharp calp echoed in  a sudden stillness that swept that massive hollow chamber. This was not the clap of thunder, but like the crackle of kindle. In one moment, the atmosphere had seemed more full of water than oxygen, so heavy was the rain. Now the rian ceased with a suddenness that was like a blow to face, and every soiked board of the ship shriveled instantly with a communal creak. Steam rose to the ceiling in a single connected sheet. The air became hot like a sauna. The waves of the reservoir stopped at once, and the water around the ship ran so low that all heard the thunk of the keep striking the stone floor of the massive chamber.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 6
text:
The rising cloud of steam revealed the ship in full, now lighted yellow by the orb in Othelmedir's palm. Across the way, Birdsey finally saw the shifting Birchmen aboard the deck. He cried out in shock and alarm.

The instant the steam had risen, Fogg reacted. Beads of sweat now stood out on hjis forehead as he dropped his one-handed morningstar and lunged for the weapon still lodged in the Birchmen. All three trees were swaying, dizzy they seemed, and looked parched and shrunken in the sudden summer heat. Fogg's two-handed sword came free from the trunk of the nearest tree as Fogg gripped its handle and jerked it loose. He wasted no time in driving it right back into the same spot of the trunk. Splinters flew in great gouts. The tree reeled and groaned. IT seemed to awaken, and tried to scuttle backward. Fogg had thrown himself into a fury though, and he swung back and in, back and in, with huge blows like a grizzly bear. The Birchman cave out a last shriek under the attack - like the tearing wood of a falling tree, but with some otherworldy human pitch to the tone - before the trunk cave out, and the upper half came crashing. The canopy fell and struck the deck with a thud that cracked the withered boards.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 7
text:
"Just jump over!" Birdsey cried from the dark opening of the reservoir exit. "Abandon ship."

"Not without reward," came Fogg's near-breathless reply. The two remaining Birhcmen had recovered, and closed in on the hard-pressed warrior. Fogg's two-handed sword cleaved left and right alternately on the two Birchmen as they came in at both sides. Each blow sent some flakes of birch flying, but each came with less fury than the one before. The warrior was tiring.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 8
text:
Othelmedir heard a low crackle at his back. He glanced behind.

Within the cabin - where the corpses of the Mailed Flayer and his acolytes had fallen from their chairs, and the bodies and furniture lay in dry disarray about the floor - smoke was rising. Somewhere below in the hold, a fire had begin. The ship was burning. Othelmedir realized that the heat he felt was not simply the will of the ghost of a parching summer, but the warmth of fire under his feet. His head swam; concentration on the light orb sapped at his own already-taxed will. As if from far-away, he heard a sharp creaking sound. He glanced lazily sideways.

One of the rigging lines, one attached to a standing Birchman and pulled taught as that monster pressed in on Fogg, had pulled taut. A bright, crowning flame was lickings it way up the length of rope. Othelmedir angled his head slightly sideways, with an almost bemused epxression on his face, as he watched the rigging blacken, fray, and snap.

He watched, for an isntant, the line whip out in his direction. He felt the sharp crack as it struck his temple. Othelmedir felt himself stumbling, and for a moment wondered if the waves had resumed. He wondered for a brief moment if he could use the fire against the Birchmen.

Othelmedir struck the rail and fell over the side. At the same instant his vision went black. His orb had gone out, himself unable to maintain focus to keep it lit, but the ship was aflame now, and shed a yellow glow on the walls of the tohmb. Othelmedir's sudden blindness came about because he had fainted.

\###

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 9
text:
Othelmedir did not remain unconscious for long. He woke to water burning its way out of his mouth from his longs. He felt something jerk his arm - he felt a dull ache from his shoulder. He blinked heated water out of his eyes. He saw the pale dead men who had been frozen in the water floating past. He felt another sharp tug, and understood that Fogg was in the water with him. The warrior had a hand hooked under Othelmedir's shoulder, and was paddling them toward Birdsey.

They crawled out like prehistoric lizards onto the cold tunnel surface. Fog rose, and Birdsey took Othelmedir's other shoulder. Together, they brought the towering mancer all the way up to his feet. Othelmedir swayed in place for a moment. Then he turned, and stared back, watching the burning ship of the Mailed Flayer as it sank into the water. The two Birchmen had caught fire too; they heard the unearhty scream of splitting wood as they burned.

"Left my morningstar," said Fogg.

"I could dive for some coins after the inferno's doused," suggested Birdsey. "Hate to leave with airy pockets."

Othelmedir reached into a fold of his soaked coat. From a long pocket, he pulled out a lengthy scroll of leather. He unfurled it, and turned so that the light of the distant flame shone on the surface, where were written Words with Rotten Syllables.

name: Setup - Paragraph 1
text: All had once been well for the ant princess Christina, until the discovery of Ant Juice. For sixteen years, the ant colony of TipTap had thrived amidst the wild rainforest. Her Mother, queen Suzie Chew, had birthed thousands of eggs, and ruled the colony with a measured foreleg. The ants of TipTap dug a deep complex in the soft rainforest soil. Each ant worked hard to keep the colony bountiful with food: seeds, dead bugs, honeydew.

name: Setup - Paragraph 2
text: Princess Christina had always loved her mother, and TipTap. And the worker ants loved the princess in turn. She spent her days with the workers, cleaning tunnels, caring for larvae. She was humble, yet beautiful, with longs legs, crystalline wings, and eyes that could sell pyramid schemes.

name: Setup - Paragraph 3
text:
TipTap's explorer ants were the first to discover the Ant Juice which was to cause so much trouble. One day, while roaming the dense darkness of the forest, an explorer ant named Dominik had stumbled upon an unusual fruit. The fruit was short, and shaped like a pretzel. It was small, hummed softly, and constantly changed colors, from blue, to pink, to sterile alabaster.

The most exciting aspect of the fruit was its taste. When Dominik stuck his mandible's into it's flesh, he found the juice sweeter than any nectar.

Excited beyond the measure, the explorer rushed back to TipTap. Queen Chew and all her advisors tasted the wonderful juice of the fruit, which they named Ant Juice. All found Ant Juice to be pure ecstasy, better than other vittles. The queen soon called for a mass harvest.

name: Setup - Paragraph 4
text:
For a time, it seemed to Princess Christina that the TipTapian Golden Age had reached a new climacteric. Each day more ants drank the Ant Juice. They ceased to gather the nuts and seeds and golden nectar - nourishing foods which had once filled their bellies. TipTap received word from their scouts that even the other ant colonies - the kingdom of Rockface, and Quickvine, and Money Chatter Stump - were drinking now. All drank deeply of the sweet, sweet Ant Juice.

A change, however, seemed to creep over the ants as they drank. They stopped building new tunnels. They stopped cleaning old ones. From industrious workers, the ants became indolent ner-do-wells. Lazy. Weak. The ants began to drool.

name: Setup - Paragraph 5
text: Princess Christina appealed to the queen: "Something is Rotten in the state of TipTap." Yet Queen Suzie Chew loved Ant Juice more than anyone, and would not listen.

name: Setup - Paragraph 6
text:
The Ant Juice offered few true nutrients, and so some ants starved to death. Yet even then, the ants who did not starve continued to drool with pleasure, for they still had their precious Ant Juice.

It was at this time that one of the queen's royal advisors, Beefoid O'Keith approached the princess. What if they founded a new colony? A colony where ants did not drink Ant Juice?

name: Setup - Paragraph 7
text: The princess bites her antenna; never before had she considered rebellion, yet never had her people known such peril. At last, she agreed.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 1
text: Princess Christina set off into the rainforest, at the head of a long column of ants. Only volunteers followed the princess; those ants who recalled the pride of a clean, well-constructed tunnel, who missed the simple seeds and bugs they had once eaten. The ants travelled far through the wet forest, crossing streams by fallen trees, tiptoeing past sleeping anteaters.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
One ant in-particular made himself invaluable during the long journey. This was Billingham "Billy" Scones, one of TipTap's most experienced explorer's. Billy Scones was an odd ant. Forty-years-old in human years, he gave the impression of wanting people to think him older and wiser than his age allowed. To this end, Billy smoked tobacco continually, through a small wooden pipe, his path through the forest being easily marked by the unbroken line of smoke which wafted behind him over the undergrowth. This habit had been intended to give him the aspect of a much-wiser sixty years; but Billy had overshot the mark, and smoked too much. He looked perhaps something like an eighty-year-old, or more accurately, like a forty-year-old with an addiction.

Nevertheless, Billy Scones was an experienced explorer, and an invaluable guide through the rainforest. Many a spider or hard-shelled snail - many a bog or brake - were skirted under the careful direction of Billy Scones.

Still the outcasts travelled far afield. Not all the ants who stepped out from TipTap's tunnels would set eyes upon their new home.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 3
text:
But at last, after many suns and moons had risen and descended over the canopy above, Billy Scones emerged from the jungle ahead, and said to Princess, "Come at once, ladyship."

"Is there another canyon," asked the princess, wringing her claws.

"No, ladyship. I have seen a mountain, past this mossy log before us."

The princess and her advisors followed Billy Scones around the log. When they rounded its corner, they beheld a great stack of stones, sitting beisde the trunk of a towering brazil nut tree. Creepers and vines served as mortar for the stack. When the ants investigated this stack further, they found the stones had sunken into the jungle floor. A huge network of subsurface tunnels already ran below the stones.

"This is the perfect place for our colony," said the princess. All the ants agreed.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 4
text: By now, the ants were half their original number. They had no rations left from their journey. They knew they would need to work quickly.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 5
text:
Ants are industrious for their size, especially when motivated, and the ants who had followed the princess across the rainforest set to work at once building their new colony. Day-after-day they tunneled and toiled, carving out new caverns for seeds, smoothing and waxing a nursery floor.

Christina was crowned queen of course, on a platform of laurel leaves gathered specially for the occasion. As Queen Christina, she worked harder than she ever had in her entire life, harder than any other ant in the colony. She laid thousands upon thousands of eggs to bolster the colony's numbers, and met hourly with her councilors to organize the workforce. She was forced to give up her lovely translucent wings, for they would not fit in the tunnel, and she grieved privately for many days over their loss.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 6
text:
A rainstorm came and went. The suns rose and set, the paths of the forest grew dark. Tunnel-by-tunnel, Stack-of-Stones was carved into a livable - though not comfortable - subsurface colony.

Through these early days, Queen Christina held firm to her original intent: no Ant Juice. "Stack-of-Stones is different," she said to her assembled ants, in the new colony amphitheater. "Ours shall be a kingdom of willful ants. A kingdom of busy ants. A kingdom of builders, and dreamers, and not daydreamers, as we left in TipTap."

name: Conflict - Paragraph 7
text: Meanwhile, Billy Scones began to mount explorations of the rainforest nearby. Lo-and-behold, they soon found same color-changing, humming, pretzel-shaped fruits from which the Ant Juice could be harvested.
