The fruits were high, high, high in a nearby tree, but they could be reached. The queen forbid all her ants to go there.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 8
text: Now Billy Scones had no more love of Ant Juice than was typical. He liked to be listened-to, however, and once he came to Queen Christian, and said, "My queen, we ants are weary from days of endless toil. Let us bring in some small amounts of juice. It shall raise our peoples' spirits, and attract ants from other colonies."

name: Conflict - Paragraph 9
text: But the queen was firm. Instead of harvesting the juice, she ordered her council to set up a system of reward. The ants who shaped the best tunnels, or found the best nuts to eat, would receive the largest share of breeding time. This was immensely popular.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 10
text:
For some seasons, the queen and her council ran the colony thus. Daily, Stack-of-Stones grew as a colony of skilled, laboring ants.

Time changes neither people nor ants. Soon the novelty of the breeding-time order wore away. The colony ceased to grow. Then one day, Queen Christina's closest advisor, Beefoid O'Keith, came to her. "Your ladyship, he said, "I must tell you, ants are leaving the colony."

"Why?" asked the queen. Her youthful luster and exuberance had been replaced with a ruler's pragmatism.

"They are bored and unhappy."

name: Drive - Paragraph 1
text:
So Queen Christina summoned the advisor-ants into her chamber of begetting.

"We should keep going," said Beefoid O'Keith. "It's okay if ants that refuse to gather seeds or dig tunnels leave our colony. We built our colony for the gatherer's and the diggers."

"Let's expand the flower-garden," suggested Shipsworth, the chief tunnel engineer.

"Or start a mushroom-drive," suggested another.

The queen shook her head. "We've tried all that before. We can't keep losing ants to other colonies."

"Your ladyship," came the sandpaper voice of Billy Scones. "We could gather the nearby Ant Juice."

name: Drive - Paragraph 2
text:
The queen shook her head. "That surely wouldn't make our workers happy. Anyway, the fruit is too far up in the trees."

Billy stroked a mandible. "It is high," he conceded. "But would could cut the fruit stems with our powerful jaws, and drop them to the forest floor. We COULD gather the juice."

name: Drive - Paragraph 3
text:
The queen rubbed the crow's-footed carapace at the corner of her eye. Leadership had worn at her. Those eyes no longer shone, except when she made them to for public address, and the ring in her voice when she spoke to the workers was a forced one.

"My queen," said Beefoid, "reconsider. Bringing Ant Juice into Stack of Stones will be the opening of Pandora's Box. Not ant would compass the reversal of policy if we change our minds."

"Offering Ant Juice will bring adults from other colonies," argued Billy Scones.

Beefoid scoffed. "Offering Ant Juice in Stack-of-Stones will draw in the same ants that drink it in TipTap. They won't eat nectar. They just drool, and want more juice."

"Enough," said Queen Christina. "We will gather a supply of juice."

The gathered council murmured. But they did not argue their queen's command. Queen Christina had led them through the early days; they trusted her.

name: Drive - Paragraph 4
text:
When it was announced in the snailshell amphitheater that Stack-of-Stones would harvest and consume its own Ant Juice, many workers grumbled.

"We were promised a free colony," shouted one old ant.

Another ant with a high voice squealed, "How will we tend the larvae? Or gather leaves? Every ant will be too-busy sipping juice."

Queen Christina, with a politician's deft hand, promised the colony that she heard and 'felt' their concerns, and then she went through with her plans, unaltered. "We're making the juice better," she said. "It won’t be TipTap Juice here. It will be the juice of our wonderful Stack of Stones. Stack Juice. Simply another option in our regular harvest. You have the solemn promise of your queen: the greatest builders and dreamers, in our subsurface corner of the rainforest, under this stack of stones, shall still receive the greatest reward."

name: Drive - Paragraph 5
text: And aside from a few early rumbles, most ants were thrilled when the first drops of sweet, sweet Ant Juice came trickling into the tunnel. A surge of industry swept through the colony: new dining caverns, longer drills for soldiers. The Mineral and Stick Index (MSI) rose 13% in one day. A fresh wave of immigrants arrived when word spread of the Stack's new juice.

name: Drive - Paragraph 6
text:
Gradually, however, the novelty of the juice wore off, as it had in every other colony. The ants still drank their juice, but they stopped their other labors. Other jobs were not needed - the only thing needed was more Ant juice. New colonists stopped coming.

In response, the queen stepped up recruitment. She posted a reward: 25,000 sugar lumps to the ant who could bring the most colonists from the dying warren of TipTap. Some came. Yet few stayed. “Why should we drink Stack Juice?” said they. “Our formula was perfect already. Your juice - if it isn’t inferior - is certainly no better. Your juice is exactly the same as everywhere else.”

name: Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
Seasons passed. Suns rose and set on the colonies of the ants. Once more, the queen found herself within the great chamber of begetting, surrounded by shouting advisors. All were sipping Ant Juice.

"What about ginger and nutmeg," said one advisor.

"Ginger and Nutmeg?" asked another, between slurps of juice.

"We could offer ginger and nutmeg Ant Juice. Gingernut Juice. A new variety."

The ants sipped and pondered. They knew every variety of juice had been tried before, if not in their colony, then in others. But the base was still the same. Plain, sweet Ant Juice. The last ant had starved in TipTap long ago. Rockface's colony was said to populated only by ghost ants now. Inexorably, it seemed, the ant juice ceased to offer any real nourishment; the colony slowly starved.

Queen Christina sat quietly a little apart from the others. She sipped her own drop of juice, through a porcelain straw. She hardly heard the words of her council, was hardly conscious of her own actions. She was thinking about the juice, only the juice, how wonderfully sweet it was, how it seemed to push all tiresome thoughts of governance and industry far from the sanded-down lobes of her brain.

"We should stop gathering Ant Juice altogether," said old Beefoid O'Keith. Even as he said the words, however, he was drowned in a hail of rebuke from the other councillors. Beefoid groaned, and leaned back in his chair.

"I've heard two of our workers were found dead in the trash chamber last week," said Billy Scones. "Starved."

Queen Christina hardly heard his words. She was thinking to herself, things were not yet terrible in Stack-of-Stones. IF they could just figure out a way to bring in more ants, the colony could be back on its feet in no time. The problem, she reflected, was the distance to the fruit. What they needed was more Ant Juice. They needed to make Stack Juice as easily accesible as possible; to make that sweet, blissful, ambrosial, orgiastic syrup of the gods freely available to any ant who would make the journey to stack of stones. They needed... needed...

Queen Christina was suddenly conscious that the chamber of begetting had ceased to echo with the arguing voices of ants. She looked up. All of her councillors were staring at her. Their antennas were raised in looks of shock. Queen Christina blinked at them, she looked down.

From the tip of her left mandible, the queen saw a thin tendril of spittle, making its gravitational exodus from her mouth to the soil below. While sipping away at her drop of Ant Juice, her mind a thousand miles from the meeting, Queen Christina had begun to drool.

name: Setup - Paragraph 1
text:
"Stop. Good job. That's a nice obedient set of mules you've got, friend. Now take those long, black fingers off the reins. Lock the axle. Now set those fingers on the rail in plain view. Keep those hands steady too. My comrade here's a spaz withhis crossbow lever."

"I am only a poor necromancer," said the wagon driver. His voice was deep and resonant.

The other man closed swiftly as the head of his crew - a dozen men. He gestured with a rusted iron speartip, and asked, "What's in that big, chained crate you're hauling?"

"A body."

"Just one?"

"I'm low on stock."

The spokesman with the spear eyed the wagon-driver as he walked up alongside the seat. His men circled around, spanning the bridge, two with loaded crossbows. "Bodies don't sell anyway," said the spokesman. He tapped his teeth with the tip of the spear. "You'll have spell materials. If nothing else, there's these fine cloven animals. Where's the key to that padlock on the crate?"

name: Setup - Paragraph 2
text: Upon his face - a dark face, with a short salt-and-pepper beard, cropped curly hair, and iron-colored eyes - Othelmedir the necromancer held a dour expression, like one grown weary of his Time. Inwardly, his brain moiled. How to escape? He cursed his carelessness; he should have watched the far bank of this lake when he first drove his oxen onto its bridge. A question of theory had distracted him: did the bridge really represented The Road of Graves? The huge paving stones each bore a deceased's fading inscription, just like the stones in any other part of the road. Yet the bridge spanned a wide, cold, pale lake. With no soil beneath the memorializing stones, where were the remains interred?

name: Setup - Paragraph 3
text:
"Down you come," said the lead marauder, and with a TINK of his speartip on the stones he drew Othelmedir's attention.

"I am known in this country," said Othelmedir, "and I've never paid a toll on this bridge before."

"Liar," said the chief evenly. "You've never come this way. At any rate, we're not tollmen."

Othelmedir changed tactics. "Perhaps you know about this bridge?" he asked. "How can it be a part of the Road of Graves? I see the-"

"Pull him down," the chief interrupted. One of the men stepped onto the driver's bench and dragged Othelmedir out. In the bed of the wagon, the chained crate jingled.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
Dragging their victim off his wagon, onto the bridge, the marauders discovered a giant. Othelmedir towered a head above the even the tallest marauder, though his ran thin as a pond reed under a cloak the color of oxblood. The chieftain reached up a long way, undid the clasp of this cloak, and pulled it away. He whistled, initially low at the soft richness of the velvet, then louder as he beheld a gleaming white mass over Othelmedir's left shoulder, like a marble pauldron. "What's this?" asked the chief, tapping the pauldron with his spear.

"Fabricant tissue," said Othelmedir.

"'Poor necromancer' indeed." The chief barked a laugh. Othelmedir's nose crinkled at the man's breath.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
"The box is chained tight," called a bandit from the wagon bed.

Othelmedir placed a fist over his mustache and muttered, as if he were complaining under his breath. One of the bandits behind him saw the steady cadence of the moving lips, however, and quickly barked a warning. He kicked the back of Othelmedir's knee; the necromancer fumbled with his spell, and fell like a redwood.

"Carve his neck," said one man, bending with a knife to put words to action.

The chief of the brigands tisked once. The knife man stopped. The chief said, "Stuff his mouth instead. That'll stop any spells. No need to make a vengeful ghost."

Othelmedir started to thank the chief, keeping his sarcasm to himself, but was interrupted by the cloth they gagged him with. It tasted like the spit of dead men.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 3
text: The man with the knife watched Othelmedir without blinking, as the latter climbed back to his feet. Meanwhile, the others had started combing through the goods Othelmedir kept in the jockey box. Othelmedir saw one man pull out his singing mushroom, see the human mouth on the underside of its cap, and drop it in surprise. They took his firebug lantern, as well as his old steel basinet, his shovel, his banshee-wax, his flints, his cube of moss, and the flour sack he used to to carry bone dust.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 4
text: Othelmedir looked at his singing mushroom where it lay on the bridge. He frowned around his gag. He was thinking: how much of the spell had he completed before the marauder had kicked his knee, interrupting him? He had not begin the motifs of Desire or Nature; had he completed those of Shaping? If so, it was less of a spell, more of an unbounded, open invitation in Deadspeech.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 5
text:
Othelmedir felt a hand clap his shoulder, and heard the marauder chiefs voice: "I have a deal for you. We won't strip you of your shirt, your pants, or your boots. In return, you'll produce the key to that box's padlock."

Othelmedir turned to face the chief. His hands had been tied before him; he raised them and pointed to his gag.

The chief shook his head. "No, my tall friend. You have no need to speak. Don't make yourself difficult either. I said I don't want your avenging ghost on my back - and I meant it - but if you think we'll stop short of-"

name: Conflict - Paragraph 6
text: A sharp gasp - as if the atmosphere had suddenly exhaled through its teeth - interrupted the bandit leader. He and all his men looked out suddenly over the water. Othelmedir had settled his eyes on his mushroom. The fungus's tiny human mouth had started humming a soft song. Now Othelmedir let his eyes drift to lake.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 7
text:
GASP.

GASP. GASP.

Three plumes broke the choppy pale surface of the lake, like the spray of whales. As the water fell in sparkling curtains, three upright cadavers stood upon the surface, joining a fourth. The bodies were mostly skeleton, with only some small pieces of flesh still clinging like tattered sailcloth to their bones. A bloom of ice spread from the toe-philanges of each, giving them surfaces to walk upon.

"Gasps," said one of the marauders, naming the un-dead. As he said it, the nearest creature reached up. From a bag chained to its neck, it plucked a stone. It whipped its arm back and forward. The stone flew and struck the man in the head, fracturing his skull. He dropped. Dead.

name: Drive - Paragraph 1
text:
"Down!" called the chief. "Under the wagon."

The men were already in motion as two more stones whipped like slingshots. One carved a crimson line along a marauder's neck, but only grazed him. Othelmedir had fallen to his belly - he crawled under the wagon.

GASP. GASP. GASP.

They heard more of the rising dead. The mules were drumming the bridge stones and braying like asylum patients, but the wagon's brake held them in place.

GASP. GASP.

They could all heard the steady crackle of forming ice. Three more stones whipped against the wagon, splintering boards and tearing fabric.

"They're closing," said one man who had daired to raise his head above the parapet.

"Back to the shore," said the chief.

The men turned to crawl in the direction. They stopped before they had begin. Two Gasps walked around the wing wall at the end of the bridge, cutting them off.

name: Drive - Paragraph 2
text:
In front, behind, strolling with crackling steps across the freezing lake - the gasping dead closed in. The marauder chief flinched as another stone punctured the wagon cover over his head. Like a wild cornered animal he glanced about with white eyes.

The chief saw Othelmedir, the latter tugging frantically at his gag.

"Kill the necromancer," hissed one of the chief's men. "He conjured them."

name: Drive - Paragraph 3
text:
The marauder chief crawled over to OThelmedir and, with a knife, cut the cloth from his face. Othelmedir spit. "Did you summon them?" the chief demanded.

Another pebble cast from the front of the bridge drew sparks off the flagstones.

"It's your own fault," said Othelmedir sharply. "You cut off my spell."

"Damn your-"

Othelmedir cut off the leader. "One of you, take this key and open the padlock." One of the marauders cursed him. "Do it or we are all corpses."

name: Drive - Paragraph 4
text:
The same marauder cursed again, grabbed the key, and rolled out from under the wagon. A stone zipped past his head as he threw himself at the cage lock.

Below the wagon, Othelmedir murmured steadily.

GASP. GASP.

Another stone zipped in, and the marauder at the wagon staggered. He held his footing though, and managed to jamb the key in the lock and twist.

At almost the same moment, Othelmedir completed his recitation.

The marauders fell silent - the one by the lock retreated. Even the Gasps paused.

The padlocked, chained box rattled.

name: Drive - Paragraph 5
text:
Padlock, chain, and box boards exploded out from the back of the wagon - and a monster emerged.

A thing enormous and massive; a thing of stitched tissues; a thing of three elongated man-arms, and three eyes lying skewed on a disgusting face of cured human skin; a thing that stank of formaldehyde and burned hair. This behemoth stepped out from the crate and down from the wagon in one stride. When its foot struck the ground, the flagstone with the faded name cracked.

The undead had all reached the bridge. Now, they paused.

name: Drive - Paragraph 6
text: "Grot," said Othelmedir, for that was its name. "Kill the Gasps."

name: Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
Boom - boom - boom - boom. Each step of the golem struck the surface of the bridge like a drum, vibrating the stones under the chests of the men hiding beneath the wagon. The Gasps had, by then formed a close circle on the bridge, surrounding the wagon. They launched into a pelting barrage, flinging a circle of hail at the stomping behemoth.

They died - again - in a circle.

name: Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
The marauiders, the chief, and Othelmedir crawled slowly from beneath the wagon as Grot flung himself among the un-dead like a wolf among deer, scattering bones. The marauders watched the violence with a communal look of appreciation. The un-dead, throwing themselves at Grot, had no more mind for the living men. They threw themselves against Othelmedir's horror, only to disintigrate under blows from one of its three fists, or scatter into ribs whenever they met the front-kick of a two-hundred pound leg.

"A fine bit of mancy," said the chief to Othelmedir.

Othelmedir had started replacing his belongings inside the wagon's chest. "He has standing orders for my protection," warned the necromancer.

name: Finale - Paragraph 3
text: Only a dozen or so Gasps had risen from the lake. They could inflict no lasting wound on the reinforced leather exterior of Grot, and he wiped them out to the last. The greatest damage the golem sustained was that one of his eyes was punctured. Othelmedir noticed this as he mounted the wagon, and saw Grot stomping back toward him. He frowned. He would need to find another eye.

name: Finale - Paragraph 4
text:
As the necromancer pulled the axle brake, dusted and donned his thick velvet cloak, and set the skittich mules stamping nervously back on his intended course, he heard a grumble among the marauders behind him. He ignored it. Then he heard the marauder chief say, "A protection fee might go far in healing any ill feeling among the families of my boy who died, stranger."

Othelmedir tugged on the reigns and pulled the brake. He stopped and considered. The golem stamped up to stand beside the driver's bench, awaiting orders. Othelmedir turned to his golem.

name: Finale - Paragraph 5
text:
"Grot, kill those as well. Delicately," he added, as the golem spun and pounded back on the marauders. The chief roared, and ordered his men to fight.

"I was, after all, low on good stock."

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 1
text:
"The thin Silver Ring is known to me. I have seen one, once. Like a tiny circle of pure moonlight."

"You have slain my tyrant? I thank you. Still, this treasure you seek makes for no mealy errand."

"But what do I want with trinkets?"

"All I want is a final cloak of thin white linen. Ha ha ha."

"Go you now to Landmine Forest. Within those woods lies what is left of Wraithcotte. Seek your Silver Ring there."

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 2
text: The remembered words of Othelmedir the Necromancer drop in time with sinking sun, fading like echoes in the mind. An uneasy woodland silence fills the empty space. The wide trunks of Landmine Forest's trees cast chronic shadows at the fringe of the woods. Between the trunks, Landmine Forest waits darkly. The broken stone road scatters to loose stone before the wall of the woods.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 3
text:
*Take a deep breath. You have marched many days, through deeper darks than this. Will you turn back? Now, when the ring your master desires lies somewhere in these woods? You are ready. Examine your equipment.*

**Bitter Steel Blade:**

This scuffed but sharp weapon smell like oil polish and wet earth. It will kill living things, and some that are un-dead.

**Firebug Lantern:**

Buzzes softly, and sheds more light than a candle. It isn't much, but it may repel the giant slugs.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 4
text: Necromancy will not work in the Landmine Forest; the poison within the mines makes the dead sleep. If Othelmedir’s Summoned Golem survived your encounter with Old Twothroats, it perishes once you enter the forest.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 1
text: Night sets thick like syrup between the trunks. As you follow the soft yellow glow of your lantern through the woods, you hear a tender, heartfelt voice. Soon you come upon a clearing at the brink of a forest gorge. Sitting on a boulder at the brink is a skeleton. It wears a blue plague mask with an orange beak, and taps its quill upon a blank parchment.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Late Poet:**

"What is a mirror to a star? A frozen lake, a farther star? This bourgeoisie, he has no star. Ho hee, hee ho, ho hee!" (TODOHyperlink)

"Hast thou come to deride me?"

"They kicked me off the estate. My relations."

"Strike me down! Plunge thy sword into this breast."

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 3
text: You may stab the skeleton through its ribs, at the spot where its heart would be. If you do, it clasps its hands over its chest, stumbles dramatically backward, and plummets down the gorge. You see its white bones scatter upon the rocks before they disappear. If you search the knapsack he left behind, you find a poison landmine.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 4
text:
You come across your first spoor of giant slug. The low grass of the forest floor glistens like dew in a whale-wide trail. Only a moment later, a heavy, wet sliding noise catches your attention. You cannot tell the direction it is coming from.

Your torchbug lamp catches the silhouette of a hollow stump. Thinking quickly, you climb over the crumbling wood and drop inside.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 5
text:
There is a crack in the tree-stump wood. Through it, you watch a shining, black, humongous shape slither over the space where you stood before. It stops and raises its antennae.

The slug slides on: SQUELCH, SQUELCH, SQUELCH.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 1
text:
As night ascends, the moon shines full over the canopy. The forest floor is a calico of shadow and silver. You wander up the steep slope of a long hill. The air seems to fresher as you near the top.

At the summit, you find a broken signpost. The sign, a board half-buried in the grass, reads "Vigyázz!" - 'Caution!' in Deadspeech.

The trees are sparse here, and you can see over the surrounding forest. Your spot a distant mass jutting out of the leaf sea. It is too conical and turreted to be a mountain or a tree. It is the old Wraithcotte Estate; many miles distant by the estimation of your eye.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 2
text: *There are few places safe for the un-dead, within Landmine Forest.*

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 1
text:
After journeying on for several hours and narrowly avoiding the notice of another giant slug, you arrive at a mutation in the undergrowth. The roots of three fig trees have woven together to form a long, writhing wall of roots. A dark tunnel plunges through the wall at the apex of the three.

You lean down and step toward the tunnel, holding your torchbug lantern ahead to see what lies inside.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Song-Singing Fungus:**

This bright red mushroom is sacred to the distended hermits; a holy order of monks devoted to Gallbladder, one of the seven God-necromancers. If you sing a few lines from any song, this long-frilled mushroom will sing the rest. Its clear, resonant voice fills you with peace.

The entrance to the root-cave is too large for the giant slugs to slide through. You have not slept since the previous dawn, and sleep beside the red mushroom sounds wonderful.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 3
text: *Little-do the easy sleeping ground, and heart that's ill at ease, concur.*

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 4
text: Though the fungus's song seemed sweet at first, you soon find yourself sinking into wakeless dreams. You realize that several days have passed since you laid down by the mushroom. Crimson roots have grown over your chest, your legs, your neck, your sword arm; you are pinned to the forest floor.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 5
text:
The only way to escape the Song-Singing Fungus is to use your torchbug lantern. If you opened the lantern door at any point in your journey, the torchbugs have flown off by now; the mushroom will slowly feed upon you, and your bones will sink into the soil of the forest.

If you still have your lantern, use your last free limb to reach out, grab the handle, and hurl it at the red mushroom. The paper walls will burst, and the torchbugs will sting the red mushroom, killing it. Your root prison falls away. The torchbugs scatter like embers from a kicked fire, leaving you to venture on without light.

name: 6. The Master Appears - Paragraph 1
text:
You hear something patrolling. It moves with a chuckling sound, and rends the air with a voice:

"...Who calls at midnight?"

"Who calls at midnight?"

"Who calls at midnight?"

"Who Calls At Midnight?"

"Who Calls At Midnight?"

"WHO CALLS AT MIDNIGHT?"

"WHO CALLS AT MIDNIGHT?"

"WHO CALLS AT MIDNIGHT?"

"Who Calls At Midnight?"

"Who calls at midnight?..."

"Who calls at midnight?......"

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 1
text:
You stifle your breath as you emerge, gasping and sweating from the forest. The ruin of the Wraithcotte Estate rises before you.

Stone outbuildings rise still and grey from the overgrown bluestem grass. The main The main manor seems as if it is made entirely of gabled windows and turrets. Despite its width, the manor has a tall, crooked, slightly swaying aspect; like a hunched old man, unsteady on his feet.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 2
text:
As you cross through the grass, you keep your breath held. Old-guard skeletons stand stock-still in their tabards beside the doors of the outbuilding. You know they will animate if disturbed. The CONSTANTSOUND of the woods is muted here; the air has lost its petrichor smell, replaced by the dying-aristocracy odor of churches and lawhouses.

As you sneak ahead, you see that all the manor windows are broken and boarded from the inside; yet each window bears a jagged skirting of colored, broken glass.

You stop a dozen paces from the recess of main entry, and crouch.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Door with a Face:**

When the voice has passed, you make for the main manor entry.

As you step under the architrave, a thin red light illuminates the door. You see now that it is not pine or any other lumber, but a stretched face, stitched into stonework. It stares at you with blind eyes. Its lips mumble quietly: you have to put your ear an inch from the mouth to hear.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 2
text: The only way to pass The Door with a Face is to answer all four of its riddles. The answers are: 1. A Bouquet, 2. Being Sad, 3. The Mouse Did It, 4. I Offer You My Sense of Smell.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 3
text: The door slides in with a gasp of old air. As you step inside, the rotting floorboards crack.  You plunge into darkness.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 1
text:
**Inside Wraithcotte Estate:**

The dust settles. You are surprised that you can see it on the hard stone beneath you.

The same soft red glow illuminates this deep hall. You realize that the glow is coming from your own body. The red mushroom from Landmine Forest has left glowing spores on the rust of your armor.

Your lungs feel weak and your muscles shake as you rise off the floor. Several of your ribs broke when you landed. You grit your teeth and set off down the rough stone hall.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Sightless Pit:**

Deep and unlit, the halls beneath the Wraithcotte Estate have uncounted floors, floor-below-floor, and there are more passages leading down than up.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 3
text: Fleshless Upright Bones roam these corridors in scheduled circuits. You will need to memorize their movement patterns and sneak past them. Battle is not an option. The sound of fighting will draw the entire swarm - you will be overwhelmed.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 4
text: *'My body is wracked with pain,' you say? Will your suffering cease if you huddle like some brittle scared grandmother, or shed fits of tears?  Your oath swears you to fetch the Silver Ring that is your masters desire. Will you break your promise because of little needling pains? For the sake of a tumble? There are knights who have suffered worse - torture, their eyes poked with heated steel, their limbs wrenched and dislocated - and all the while held a smile. Keep those knights in your mind - and recall that courage empowers us to face the pain, not which is easiest, but which is the most severe.*

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 5
text:
The wine-barrels that line the walls are all hollow and empty. Each barrel has the following icon on its surface:

TODOICON

At first the icon seems harmless. You soon realize, however, that if you stare at any icon for a long time, you feel the acrid taste of sour wine in your throat, and your head starts to swim. Thereafter, you keep your eyes averted.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Hall of Sculptures:**

As you start down this corridor of The Sightless Pit, it's end remains elusive. The walls are drier brown in color, as if the hall had flooded once. After three hundred and thirty three steps, you come across three life-size stone sculptures.

Away-Facer: A male figure encased in smoke; the sculpture turns its head away from you, even as you try to meet its eye.

Frozen Body: Only this old man's head is free, the rest of his body is encased in a block of ice.

The Woman with Backwards Eyes: She sees inside you.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 2
text:
**Hither, Lady of the Well:**

At the end of The Hall of Sculptures you find a room with a well. A wrinkled, two-ton grandmother sits beside the well in an enormous rocking chair. She holds a fishing rod of proportional size over the opening of the well - at the end of the line is a basket the size of a bathtub.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 3
text:
"Young man, help me."

"Something gleams at the bottom of this well, but I can't catch it in my basket."

"I hope its the shell of a turtle, or a shiny toad."

"Those scallywags killed my animals..."

"Will you climb in my basket, and fetch it for me?"

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 4
text:
If you climb into the basket, the giant grandmother will lower you on her pole. At the bottom of the well you find the Reflective Ointment.

Reflective Ointment: a shiny, apple-shaped bottle, filled with an unguent that tastes and looks like metal.

Once you take the bottle and tug twice on the line, you will rise swiftly upward, the mossy stones of the well falling past. Instead of the well room, however, the line pulls the basket all the way to the first floor of the mansion, where you are immediately attacked.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Bell Ringer:**

A clawed foot kick you from the basket. You spring up just in time to meet your foe; a thick skeleton with a bell for a hand, and a cone around its mouth. As it stomps toward you it cries: "Who calls at midnight?!"

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
In battle The Bell Ringers will repeatedly cry and ring his bell. If he is not slain by the 11th ring, you will fall asleep. The tolling bell rings in your dreams as The Bell Ringer crushes you with his clawed feet.

Keep as much space between yourself and the skeleton as possible, and avoid his kicking feet. Strike his bell-ringing hand whenever he breathes in to speak.

If you sever his bell-hand, the skeleton will be stunned. You can then dispatch him with ease.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 1
text:
With The Bell Ringer dead an eerie silence settles over Wraithcotte manor, silence unbroken even by rats. After pausing to catch your breath, you venture on.

Much like the pits, the mansion of the estate is vast and layered, floor-after-floor that seem to be made only of deep corners and dripping chandeliers. Attics surmount attics. Stairs lead to narrower stairs.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 1
text:
Feeling as if eyes follow you wherever you go, you begin exploring the mansion.

In one room you find a circle of twelve beds, their headboards facing away from the walls. The beds are arranged clockwise by their states of decay. The bed on your left as you enter looks freshly-made; to your right lies a contraption of rusted springs and termitewood, like one of The Red King's confession racks.

Several hours later you enter a library. When you examine the books, you find that someone has painstakingly poured a honey over every book. All the pages stick together. The text is all in a language you cannot read.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 2
text:
The mansion is a labyrinth; navigating will be difficult. You can try leaving chalk marks, but something erases them. The only way to track your progress is to leave a tooth (yours or someone else's) at each explored passage. Whoever or whatever erases the chalk marking leaves the teeth alone.

As you explore, you may come across the following items:

**Necromancer's Shovel:** While it can be used to dig earth, this shovel is also enchanted to crack through wood and marble coffins.

**Bowl of Candied Acorns:** Largely unspoiled.

**Caged Whip-poor-will:** How this bird has survived in the mansion, you do not know. If you release it from its cage, it flies away. A few days later it will find you again, drop an emerald in your lap, then leave forever.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 3
text:
**The Chapel:**

After hours in the silent halls, you enter a narrow passage ending in double doors. You push them open, and find yourself in a secluded chapel.

The floor is sticky as you step inside; you have to pull each foot off the cobbled stone with a sound like ripping cotton. A single, unboarded, stained-glass window lights the space. All the pews are broken and filled with slip, like swine troughs. In one trough, a tomato plant has sprouted and withered.

You hear footsteps approaching from behind.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
**Merilka Wraithcotte:**

As she enters the chapel behind you, Merilka wraithcotte stands tall, pale, and narrow as an aspen tree. Herr shining steel armor makes not a sound as she glides like a wraith toward you. Two rapiers smile in her hands.

Beautiful on the outside, but rotten on the inside, an abortion of mortality, Her Royal Corpulency Merilka wraithcotte stands before you. She is the last of her line, having slain the rest of family. Her name means 'Rose of the Sacred Barque'.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
To defeat Merilka, you will need to apply the Reflective Ointment from The Sightless Pit. Doing so will curse you with mirror-like skin. This curse is permanent, and lasts after death, but it is preferable to dying now.

Merilka will try to skewer you with her two rapier, which are thin as needles, and sharp enough to puncture steel. She usually attacks in with a series of seven to nine jabs at your chest and legs, follow by a double-thrust at your eyeballs. When she open her waist at the end of this double-thrust, you have a small window to strike her hip. If you miss your window, you will have to endure another round of her pattern.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 3
text:
The first serious wound you inflict on Marilka will send her into hysterical weeping. She will go berserk. Now her rapiers stab twice as quickly as before. She is slowed by her injury, however, and you are able to keep a little space between you now.

After this relentless flurry, she will need to rest and recover. Use this as a chance to doff your helm and unbuckle your breastplate; they are useless against her attacks anyway. When she blinks away her tears and attacks again, your reflection will startle her; she beholds her own bloodshot, tearstained eyes in her death-pale face. This is your only opportunity; stab her Through the heart with your Bitter Blade.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 4
text: Marilka Wraithcotte falls. Her rapiers disintigrate. With a last sob, she dies. Now, on the chapel floor, a choice lies before you. Taking the Silver Ring from her finger, you may either place it into a pocket, or slip it over your own.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 5
text: *In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 1
text:
Steel and stone wriggle in the dark. A vibration shivers through through a broad, blind surface. The vibration fades. Then again: *zzzzzzzz*. Almost soundless, a faint hum hangs in the cold, black air. It dies, like the lonely song on the final breath of grey old thrush.

A third vibration. A crack light. The surface *scrapes* inward.

The heavy door of steel-gilded rock slides aside under the final smash of a pendulum hammer. Cold daylight opens its eye into the long darkness of a tunnel. Framed in the daylight, your black silhouette stares into the darkness of Mool's Chrypt.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 2
text:
If you carry a firebug lantern on your person, now is the time to whisper to the glowing insects; let the lanterns paper-yellow light slide over the dusted stones ahead of you. If not, strike a torch.

Joy quickens in your stomach at the image of the cracked stone door; the pristine air of the tomb desecrated by the outer atmosphere. You suppress that joy, it is a tumor in your heart.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 3
text:
One by one, you pick up the three pieces of your equipment lying in the shade of a boulder beside the entry.

**Platemail Helmet:** The feather on its crest twists in the wind. Stuffy, and restricts your vision, but may deflect that final killing strike.

**Pinecone Shield:** Decorated with The Blue Lady's pinecone sigil.

**Polished Steel Longsword:** A sharp, shining weapon. Well-maintained.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 4
text:
You swing your sword in an arc before the door; the tunnel will be wide enough for your strikes. You pick up your available source of light with your shield hand. With the darkness before you, you recall the exact orders of Othelmedir:

*"Resurrection is not free, my freshly-lived friend.*

*"Certainly not in your case. Ha ha ha.*

*"Your body is like a cracked teacup. Getting YOU back in yourself was no simple errand.*

*"Now the debt calls. Your Lady cannot pay for your life, and so you must pay for it yourself."*

*"The grave of Mool - a grot, an old species of necromancer - lies three days journey north, along a dry riverbed.*

*"The grot were slain and mummified an age ago. But Mool survived. Within the halls of his chrypt, he dreams of revenge on man.*

*"Go then, you Cracked Cup Knight. The spit of Mool is the currency by which you shall repay me."*

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 5
text: You raise your light, and you raise your sword, and step into darkness that distantly *clangs*.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Upper Halls:**

A tropical-smelling, saddle-colored mist pools in the bone-rich alcoves which line the corridors of Mool's Tomb. Your helmet is filled with the smell, as well as with the sound of your own heavy breathing. You do, however, hear the occasional *clang* from somewhere below.

On first entering the rusted tomb, you will find yourself in the labyrinthine upper passages, and hopelessly lost. In order to proceed down into the tomb proper, keep an eye on the carved architraves over the intersections. Pass only under those which show a fresco of a long jellyfish with its tendrils pointing left - you will soon arrive at a hole in the floor, around which an urn-wide stairway curls.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 2
text:
At the bottom of the steps is a small pool of green water. You snuff your light at once, for there is light shining from a cluster of glow, ruby crystal, sprouting like acne from the stone brick walls. The ruby light reflects off the green water.

You step from the pool - onto a floor of disturbed dust. Almost at once you step into an alcove; something is approaching.

**Gearslave Ape:**

A fabrication of wood, and rusted iron, and glowing translucent crystal shambled over the ancient stones. Simian in shape, it's twitching, loping gait carries it past your hiding space. The red crystal in the ceiling glows like blood on its teeth of foreign bones, and in its glass eyes. It moves to an unseen necromancer's will.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 3
text: You may either follow behind this gearslave ape, or branch off down your own path. If you follow the ape, its leads you deeper into the catacomb. If you choose to find your own way, you will step upon a pressure plate several moments later, triggering a sweeping scythe that severs your jugular.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 4
text:
After slipping past another pair of gearslave apes by skirting around the outer row of a collonaded hall, you find that your unwitting guide has vanished. It is worth your time to explore some of the nearest alcoves. You may find:

**Sunblind Dew:** This thin vial of water contains dew from blades of grass grown in total darkness. Drink it, and your eyes will see without light.

**Sacrificial Cleaver:** A heavy, ornate blade, used to sever the necks of cattle.

**Hourglass:** The sand within this timekeeper has solidified into a single, solid clump of mud. The sand can be replaced. The device is useful in the casting of certain spells.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 5
text:
After wandering for several hours, you arrive before a narrow but enormously tall doorway. You may translate the Deadspeech written on the epithet to the side:

*"In the Sickening Cradle;*

*"Under the Headstone Mountain;*

*"The Thin Man breaths behind a silver mask.*

*"His house shall awaken; hail, Sleeper, Dreamer."*

You pass through the tall doorway.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Chrypt Trench:**

You hold your sabaton back just before stepping over the lip of an invisible ledge, an end in the passage. Before you, a trench stretches. A hundred gearslave apes creep and crawl over the jagged face of the opposite wall. They dart to and from the black holes that mark other passages. Far below you, the faint light of the tomb crystals shine off the surface of a lake.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 2
text:
By observing them without notice for some time, you may distinguish between the distinct types of ape:

**Carver Ape:** These servants climb up and down the walls of the trench, carving out small boreholes, and placing the glowing crystal buds within.

**Stonechewer Ape:** Their purpose is unknown. When they move, it is always accompanied by an enormous scraping, grating sound.

**Yellow Ape:** Covered in wax.

**Heavy Ape:** These gearslaves stand twice the height of a man. You do not wish to draw their attention.

**Milk Ape:** Contructed of pale steel, painted balsa-wood, and glowing white crystal. They shamble about without a sound.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 3
text:
You take a moment to consider the scene.

*Quench those sparks in your chest. Neither now, nor later, is the time for wrath. Better to watch. Perhaps you will learn in which casket is buried Mool and his spit.*

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 1
text:
You watch as a processions of gearslave apes emerges - one ape after the next - from the mouth of one catacomb. The line seems like an endlessly-lolling constructed tongue. The apes jitter in time with each other, their movements like a clockwork ritual dance.

The line moves down a crooked zigzag in the far trench wall. Each ape carries one glowing crystal, each crystal a different color, the colors arranged to form a shifting rainbow. The line passes by other apes on the wall. One ape applies a coat of wax to a new crystal bud. Another is scraping a new recess for bones, using tools of roughly-hammered iron. Some of the wax apes have climbed higher, to the uppermost niches. There they gather bones that are brittle and dry. To these, they apply fresh coats of wax.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 2
text: *They seem harmless, though none have sensed your presence yet. The creations of necromancy are things of secret violence.*

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 3
text:
The lead ape in the swaying procession reaches the bottom of the crooked cliff. The path sinks into the reflective lake. The ape lifts its crystal - a heavy mass of sunflower-colored geode. It reaches its arms back behind its head with a clicking motion, then hurls the stone out and into the lake.

You watch as, beneath the water, other lights rise. Light that are not reflections.

**Glowing Jellyfish:** These creatures are imbued with some sort of necromancy; what that is, you know not. Each geode dropped is "swallowed" by these creatures, these jellyfish in the lake of the trench in Mool's Chrypt.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 4
text:
*Whatever is the purpose of this industry, it should be no concern of yours. Woe for a knight - pious in his heart to the tenets on The Road of Graves - to find himself a robber.*

*But, your Lady cannot pay...*

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 5
text:
A snap breaks your reverie. You swivel your restricted vision across the opposite wall until you catch a speeding shape. One of the gearslave apes - a carver - is falling. The frayed end of its harness line wriggles in the air above it as it plunges down. The ape shatters the reflective surface of the lake. It slips under, its frayed line sliding in after it.

Almost immediately the old tomb gasps with animation. The water comes alive with a new light. A lurid kaleidoscope of colors blooms from the depths. Ten thousand jellyfish rise in in a glowing, angry cloud. The other gearslaves meanwhile have raised an artificial cacophony of voice, so strident that it pains your ears. It is near to the braying of livestock from a barn when a black wolf has stepped inside; frenzied, full of animal soul, but missing animal throats.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 6
text:
**Flee:**

The hooting din of the mechanical apes will soon drive your stitched-in soul insane. You must get away from the trench.

Three paths lie open to you. You may retreat the way you came. You may attempt to scale the jagged underground cliff, seeking a higher or lower passage. The stones are slick though, and it is likely the apes will notice you.

The third path is a narrow stone ledge, running immediately to your right.

As your helmet rings with screaming, you choose the third path, and slide onto the ledge.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 1
text:
Despite the voices rattling in your skull, and the precarious drop to the glowing lake below, you make progress. The gulch narrows, the walls press together. You greased your platemail before entering the chrypt, and have manages to move undetected.

Two score bridges span the two gulch walls ahead you, set at various heights, curves, and grades. They are made of metal plates and suspicious leather. They look thin, but you have few options. Your ledge terminates where one bridge hangs like a slide, leading further down on the opposite wall.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 2
text:
**They Can Speak:**

A ramshackle shape of wood and metal drops suddenly before you. The gearslave ape's fake teeth gleam. It hisses, then speaks in scratchy but distinct Deadspeech, like a wax cylinder voice:

*"A man!*

*"Breath spoils the air.*

*"Steel spoils the light.*

*"Must be remade."*

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 3
text:
A shriek blossoms from the artificial throat. Two claws of scabbed bone fly at your breastplate. You sidestep the clumsy attack with trained instinct, at the same time swinging your Polished Steel Longsword in a whistling arc. The bright blade cuts the monster of rust and wood like a dummy of straw and cloth. It crumples.

The gulch erupts with a renewal of screams. Hundreds of crystal eyes turn and spot you.

You hurl the gearslave - broken and swaying - from the ledge. You rush for the thin bridge of leather and metal. Two other apes drom from above. One grabs your shoulders from behind, the other charges at your face.

You Steel hews a path.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 4
text:
*Boom*.

*Boom.*

*Boom.*

*Boom.*

The gearslaves apes drop into an appalling machine hush as, from an wide opening below, a *booming* shudders through the din.

name: 6. The Master Appears - Paragraph 1
text:
A substance like oil seems to darken the shadows of the wide passage. The *booms* shake the surface of the lake.

It is a moment before you notice another sound. All across the gulch the apes chant; one word in perfect time; one word in perfect harmony; one word whose note leaps across the musical scale, like a church bell toumbling down a circular stair:

Mool. Mool. Mool. Mool.

*Boom.*

Mool. Mool. Mool. Mool.

*Boom.*

Mool. Mool. Mool. Mool.

*Boom.*

Mool. Mool. Mool. Mool.

*Boom.*

Mool. Mool. Mool. Mool.

*Boom.*

Your helmet fills with the reek of burning feathers.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 1
text:
**Surviving the Gulch:**

There is no way for you to escape the gearslave apes. You cannot cut a way out, and cannot look upon Mool. To see the ancient grot necromancer within his tomb is to see a thing beyond living reason. Your eyes shall swim with madness.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 2
text: The only way out is to survive. The only way to survive is to fight your way to the bridge. Three gearslave apes - heavy apes - will drop down between you and the suspended crossing of leather and steel. Charge the first one the moment it lands, and shove it over the side. You must slay the next two with your Steel. Duck their heavy swipes, which can crack rock. Hew fast; Mool approaches. Do not allow yourself to fall from the ledge either - the fanging rock below will break you.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 3
text:
The thin crossing sways dizzily underfoot as you step gingerly onto its surface. Undaunted, you start out over the lake.

You have not taken nine steps when you see that the course is hopeless. A swarm of gearslaves gibbers already at the far end. They swagger onto the bridge, adding their weight. You hear more apes climbing on behind.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 4
text:
*Snap.* The combined weight tears the leather. You plummet, the water spills through your visor. You sink.

The weird, glowing Jellyfish surround and flood eyes.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 1
text:
*"Oh, my Knight. I thought you would be lost to me forever."*

*What is this slimy feeling, between your scarred skin and your steel plate? Wet wool? Why are you soaked?*

*"Othelmedir said your thrice-dead body was like a cracked cup. He said it might no longer hold your soul. But, I knew the necromancer was wrong."*

*The chrypt. The chrypt of Mool. The gearslave apes. The lake. The plunge. Why is there a fog around your thoughts?*

*"Oh, my knight! You are shaking. All will be well, you are alive again."*

*What is this soft thing between your fingers? Tall grass? Focus. Your lady needed you to do a thing in the tomb. What was it?*

*"I will hold your hands."*

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 2
text:
You open your eyes.

**The Heather Vault:**

The Heather Vault waits beneath the higher catacombs in the ancient chrypt of the grot necromancer, Mool. Names for the profusion of tall, magenta shrubs blooming throughout the domed chambers and narrow corridors. The heather grows thickest near the huge crystalline lattices that are its light source; or toward the tubular holes in the ceiling, through which a mirrored, secondhand sunlight limps.

Mool's gearslave ape servants rustle through dense boscage, laboring under the obscure will of the sleepwalking necromancer. They massage salted, electrified water onto the enormous crystals, or chisel still long tunnels for the multiplying bones. Or, they tend the jellyfish; all grot are fascinated by the amorphous, tendriled sealife.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 3
text:
You sit up in the coarse heather. The fronds are pressed down all around where you lay; you find yourself enclosed in a cube of rusted bars. Your throat stings. You cough up a thin mist of the lake water inside your helmet. Suddenly you feel at your hip and all around - you realize that the apes have taken your Steel, and your shield.

Standing, you realize that your cage sits in an overgrown, domed room. You can just see the wooden skulls of the contructs as they move through the heather. There is another matted space not far from you. The gearslave apes have a fire burning beneath an iron pot the size of a bathtub. You see the steam curling over the lip.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 4
text:
The fronds of heather part before your prison. One of the gearslave apes steps upon to the bars.

*"We are the marrow, man. Free marrow of the chrypt.*

*"Man, you are the seed.*

*"Your marrow is in your bones, behind a shell.*

*"We will boil your bones free of your skin."*

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 5
text:
Your prospects are gaunt and clad in tatters. If you remembered to collect your Portal Splinter back in Othelmedir's tower, you can try to use it to pick the padlock on the front of your cage. The lock is rusted, however, and clotted with heather pollen; your chances of picking it are small.

If you scan the area, you are able to spot your Polished Steel Longsword. One of the apes is using it to hack a clearing around the boiling pot. Your Pinecone Shield is nowhere to be seen.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 6
text:
You consider the gearslave standing at your cage.

*Strange truly are the means and ends of necromancy. What purpose can this grot-creature have in stitching souls to wood and metal? This creature before you: is it's soul like yours? A man soul? Is it injured like yours?*

*Enough. That helps nothing.*

*There are too many  of this creature's kind for you to cut a way out, even if you still held your Steel. What then?*

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 1
text:
You spend some minutes examing the ape at your cage; its uncanny stillness and unbreaking, crystalline staring accommodate you in this endeavor.

The gearslave's ape-skull and ape-torso are carved from two pieces of crumbly spruce, in what appears to be a roughyl-feminine shape. The wood sports a live colony of termites. Cobwebs span the spaces between its reticulated fingers and toes, like the webbing of a frog's feet. No breath issues from between this ones shining teeth - human teeth, from a human skill - but the construct produce a general creaking noise as it sways where it stands. There is very little metal to this ape. You do take particular note of this one's tail - it is a single curved rod of iron. It looks solid. It is fixed to the body of the ape by a weak-looking plate, held by nails red with rust.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Boiling Cauldron:**

You see the other gearslave apes building the fire under the pot higher and higher. They carry fronds of heather, cut with your Steel, and press the bundles under the tall tripod of the boiling cauldron. The heather is green, and a choking, acrid smoke fills the space.

This cauldron of cast-iron came to Mool a hundred-hundred years past. It belonged in that long-ago age to The Inn of The Little Sea. Mool burnt the inn to ruins with his necromancy. Only the cauldron survived the fire, and so Mool took it and carved his glyphs of Desire into the bottom. The glyphs have since been buried under the soot of many burning, but they are there still.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 3
text:
To touch the liquid which is not water that lies within the boiling pot is, not just to die in a way that is horrible, but to have the spirit in your cracked body stolen by the ritual of the necromancer. You must find a means of escape.

The simplest way out of your cage to tear the iron tail from the nearby gearslave ape. Using this, you the weak padlock holding the door shut may be pried and snapped. You will need to attract the ape's attention again; it has since turned back to the pot. The best way to antagonize the artificial animal, given its feminine shape, is to bring up The Sisterhood of the Yellow Smile.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 4
text:
The gearslave ape swivels around as you finish speaking. Its face cannot display emotion, but you can hear outrage in its scratching voice.

*"Sisterhood? Why Sisterhood?*

*"This marrow needs no burial.*

*"This marrow serves.*

*"Why Sisterhood? Stupid man, that was old flesh, before Mool!*

*"Stupid. Stupid. Stupid!"*

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 5
text:
Mools Boiling Cauldron errupts with a horrible hissing of the fluid within. This is the screaming of its prior victims. The nearest gearslave apes drop their bundles of heather. They begin hooting and jumping mechanically; like clockwork toys.

The ape standing at your cage turns around and looks at the cauldron.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
**Eleven Strokes with The Old Man Below :**

Lunging, your gauntlets clasp and rip the tail loose. The ape screams like a horsenail against steel. Two score eye crystal spin toward you. You jam the rod beside the lock.

You heave - the lever rod groans. The cage door clinks; ajar. A tonal choir howls. Apes cleave through brush. You emerge. You swing.

*Crack*.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
The door is now open, but the apes have noticed, and swiftly converge upon you. You must act quickly.

Your first job is to retrieve your Steel. Fortunately, the ape cutting the heather is one of the closest ones in the swarm. Using your tail-rod-staff, disarm and clobber the clumsy creature.

Next, you need to escape this boiling-cauldron room. Half a dozen tunnels present black egress to the rest of The Heather Vault. Unfortunately the apes have closed around you, cutting off most paths of escape. You cannot hope to cut through so many foes.

One path, directly behind your cage, offers your best chance. This is The Humming Hall of Andoleg, where the heather grows thickest in the vault. Guarding Andoleg's hall is an ape named Snowy Gore. He's a capable opponent, but you can race past him instead of fighting.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 1
text:
**Places to hide:**

**Ald Medas Shrine:** In this tall chamber of The Heather Vault, you crouch among the splintered piles of ten thousand exhausted magical wands.

**Big Eye's Cave:** Seven ivory eggs encased in amber stud the ceiling.

**Selgon:** This pillared corridor reminds you of an apprentice knight you once escorted through a similar sepulcher.

**Index Chamber:** A snake of yellow light slithers constantly through the air between two pilons in this room. The shifting illumination makes this a surprisingly-good place to hide.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 2
text: With a breath of relief you hear the mechanical thunder of stamping feet roll past your hiding place. It fades like a storm weathered. After several moments have passed you emerge, Steel in hand, and begin to probe the vault.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 1
text:
The heather thins as you squeeze between two slick walls of one corridor, the gap so thin that you must turn your shoulders sidways. There is barely enough space for your breastplate and a single frond's-width of heather to fit between the walls.

Finally you come out of both the thin corridor and the heather simultaneously. You find yourself in a pentagonal room with a waxed dirt floor. Two white crystal tumors hang from the ceiling, lighting the space in sterile alabaster.

**Mool's Sanctum:**

A thousand experiments lay on marble tables in this room, or huddle in one of the five corners, or dangle from cupper chains in the ceiling. Your sense of self-preservation (and your sense of sanity) warns you from lingering long in this space.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 2
text:
If you choose to explore Mool's Sanctum, you find a variety of potentially useful information and objects. The most immediate in a streak of black bile, frozen against the marble edge of one table. This is grot spit - Mool's spit. This is why you came.

There is a small koi-pond full of finger-sized jellyfish. These shed less glow than a firebug, and make no sound at all. If you touch one, you experience a tingle in the hairs of your arm, like a building static charge. Several incomplete gearslave automaton parts lie in scattered parts on the grated shelves. Inert, unshining pieces of crystal form a broken kaleidoscope of muted color near one of the three exits to the room. The air is heavy with the smell of crushed beetles.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 3
text: You find your Pinecone Shield leaning against a lifesize statue of an immortal goddess. The statue is perfectly-molded from waxed copper; you cannot help but wonder how much the assorted treasures of this room would be worth at an Emerald House. Surely the priests of Lady Horsehair would rub their haggling palms at sight of this woman in copper.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 4
text: *The spit is yours; now only escape remains. What horror could have drooled such acrid-stinking bile as this that is in your bottle now? The same monster that stitched the souls into those apes. The same that would have taken your soul and bones. It is not chivalrous, but you cannot quench your hatred of the thing. Polluted necromancer. Hideous grot! It deserves the true death it denies its creations. It's perverse experiments are a heresy upon The Road of Graves. Those jellyfish; whose body are they meant to deform? Horrible experiments. Their destruction would be just repayment to this grot, Mool. Deserved pain. Pain. Pain!*

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 5
text:
Water drips off your Steel. As wrath's red haze gives way to the blue film of contrition, and your eyes settle on the floating pieces of a dizen tiny jellyfish, a distant sound shakes you. From elsewhere in The Heather Vault, you hear a deep, bassy gurgle.

Run.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
Only once in your life have you glimpsed one of the grot; a dead, shriveled, mummified thing, encased in a glass box, in one of the tents of The Coffin Rollers which travel The Road of Graves. It looked like black rope, with frayed ends sticking out in pairs from its sides.

You stop to breathe. The coarse heather fronds rub your visor.

Another alien gurgle reels within your brain.

This living grot has now taken up the chase. Your only chance is to escape.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
The specific path you should follow depends on the door through which you exited Mool's Sanctum.

If you left through the opening flanked by cobbled gallstone pillars, proceed down the left-curving corridor until you arrive at the Laros Vampire Clan's burial chamber.

If you left through the underwater tunnel in the jellyfish pool, follow the tunnel until you reach the second air pocket. You will find a ladder here which leads to an upper level.

If you used the steel crank to open the hidden passage in Mool's sanctum, you will have to fight your way through two gearslave apes. The apes aren't tough, but will allow the necromancer to close the distance. After killing them, you enter The Hall of Diffusion. Make worthy the lost time by grabbing the Cat's Eye Spear lying on the magenta ottoman.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 3
text:
Once you find yourself back in the thick heather, you'll notice something strange about the gearslaves. They still screech for your bones, and will attack you on sight; but their gemstone eyes all shine with a black-grey light, like the final broadcast of an expired star.

One huge gearslave rises to block your path. You cut it down with a swipe that buckles its rusted knee, and an overhead chop that splits its wooden skull. In the succeeding moment, catching your breath, you notice that horrible bass gurgle has drawn closer.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 4
text:
The gurgle resolves into words incongruously rasping and wet at once; like hearing burning fir cones crackle under the surface of a scummy pond.

*"Mortal neoplasm.*

*"Your ingrowth displaces the particles of my vapor.*

*"My white servants will engulf you.*

*"Be inflamed, expire, and absorbed."*

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 5
text:
**The Moonlight Climb:**

After flighted eternity, you stumble under a possible means of escape. Silver moonlight shines like a beam through a slanted ceiling hole.

Climbing into the hole, you realize it develops into a long tunnel; sometimes running horizontal; sometimes angling; sometimes purely vertical along coarse rock. Chains straddle the tunnel, supporting thousands of mirrors, which reflect some far-off moon.

A movement catches your eye. It is only the mirrored reflection of a shadow, somewhere behind you. But the reflection of the shadow of the shape - a shape like a giant toad and a centipede mated - leaves you nauseous. You scramble faster up the rattling chains.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 6
text:
Just as the humpbacked moon begins to widen across the mirrors - seemingly around every corner - you step underneath the next vertical in the tunnel, and are disheartened. Above stands a grate of thin but hard wire; invisible in the reflections, yet all-too-corporeal.

Climb to the wire mesh, and start to saw through it with your Steel. It is difficult, but possible. Cut through at least three tines before trying to squeeze through. Ignore the gurgle and scurry at your back. Keep your hold on the stone. Hack. Chop. Cut.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 7
text:
With a wrench and a screech the wire grate tears from its mooring. You climb free. Something brushes your leg. You scramble.

Rounding a corner of stone, you arrive, finally, at the surface.

You lay on the dewy grass.

You push your visor back, and let the lambent moon dapple your weary eyes.

*In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 1
text:
The cold blue eye of a professional murderer watches, blinkless, from the dappled head of a painting of a horse. The eye is attached to a murderer's body, in a black boarding-house room. It stares through the hole in the horse painting, stares at two people in another room - a knight in shining armor, and his lady of blueblack hair. The lady is speaking, but the eye cannot hear what she says.

There is another killer though (there are several), with his ear at the keyhole.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 2
text:
This is what that other killer hears:

*"My Cracked Cup Knight, I know you have just returned.*

*"Still, I would have us leave under moon and cloak, through the Gate of Green Vines.*

*"The assassins of Clan Wiege will not settle for murdering my loves, destroying my house.*

*"Oh my Knight, let us go. My heart misgives."*

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 3
text:
**Assassins!:**

The instant you hear the crash - the second the thin driftwood door of your private room shudders inward - your shield and sword are within your hands.

Three men in full leather flood into the room. In their hands are long knives. Good weapons for small spaces.

Your Lady shouts and back towards the painting of the horse between the twin beds. You yourself are silent. The spit of a volcano courses through your limbs.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 4
text: To survive, you'll need to withstand the assassins' initial storm of blades. Keep your Pinecone Crest Shield raised as these first three killers stab and hack toward you. When there is an opening, after one of their swords reflects off your pauldron, retaliate with quick jabs of your Polished Steel Longsword.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 5
text:
With two men waxing the floor in their shining blood, the third bull rushes you. You bring your sword to bear, but the impact send the both of you shattering through the chamber's window.

Breath-stolen, you land in the house's inner court. A dead tree casts the skeleton of its shadow over brown grass.

You raise your shield to catch a crossbow quarrel. No time for breath-catching. Rise. You must carve the second wave, half-a-dozen fresh knives in half-a-dozen bloodied hands.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 6
text:
**Razorshell:**

You hear a scream in your lady's voice. You turn your visor up. You see your lady backing along an upper catwalk. Another assassin dogs her with his sword.

Act swiftly, or The Blue Lady will die. Sever the jugular of the remaining killer before you. On his belt you will find a razorshell, this one shaped like a fist-sized conch. Pick it up. Hurl it at the remaining assassin in the couryard, the one with crossbow, level his second bolt at your chest.

As the razorshell evaporates his face into pink mist, run to him. Throw your shield and Steel aside. Dive to the crossbow with a crash of plates. Grab it, turn, and aim at the killer on the catwalk.

If you timed everything perfectly, you will slay this final assassin; just as he raises his blade over your lady's long, blueblack hair.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 1
text:
**Treading the Epitaphs:**

The smoke trails and bright terracotta towers of Ripe - the largest city on the mesa by the Lake of Tomatoes - have dwindled to tiny shapes against the slate-colored sky, as you guide your Blue Lady's palfrey along The Road of Graves. The Gate of Green Vines is a night's distance behind you. The night has passed slowly, and you met few other travelers. The clopping of the palfrey's shoes scored the journey.

Dawn swings in force now. As you round the bend in the next hill, you cast one look back at Ripe.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 2
text:
Your lady has not spoken all through the night. Now her voice comes softly from beneath the velvet of her cloak.

*"Many miles... Still, killing eyes may see far.*

*"Every town on The Road of Graves encloses a Wiege Clan spy.*

*"At least, where it hugs the shore of the vast Lake of Tomatoes.*

*"Oh my husband. My sons. My daughter...*

*"You and I, faithful knight, will not find piece in this country. It's families are not for peace."*

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 3
text:
You consider what your lady has said.

*She's right. Poor woman. Poor Blue Lady. And poor master as well. He's gone, and she has only your service left. Serve well, then.*

*We shall stop at this village ahead; you know she needs rest, ill-filled with dreams though that rest may be. After her rest, however, we must place more graves between ourselves and Ripe.*

*...To what ending?*

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 4
text:
As you reach the next low hill's sun-bleacked summit, a river-ribbon valley runs away below you. A mile further along the road a small burg lies. Four score of homes and barns. Each home is shaped in the common style, like a sloping wedge of cheese. The homes are organized with their points facing each other as well, so that from your distance and height, each pair of homes is like a single hourglass, set on its side and sagging in the middle.

A woman stands at the side of the road, a leather umbrella with holes mottling her white skin in patches of sunlight. She is pretending to count the blue needles on a spruce sprig, but looks up as you approach. She smiles. She wears a bright, short, yellow summer dress. On the inner crook of her left elbow is a tiny tattoo of a red lantern.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 5
text:
**Magpie, the Pleasuress:**

*"New faces, or repeat-comers?*

*"A knight and his mistress, my apologies.*

*"Sir knight, you come to The Cat in the Jar on the eve of seasonal disaster.*

*"When the harridan's moon shines, as it does tonight, the town believes that shining orb will crash down from the heavens.*

*"We pray all through the night to the seven god-necromancers. Pray that our souls will be inducted in their service.*

*"It makes the cats howl and stir."*

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 6
text:
You may allow Magpie the Pleasuress to guide you into town.

There is not much happening at The Cat in the Jar. You may barter a night's-stay in the hay of a nearby barn, and exchange your lady's palfrey for another horse at Brandt's Carriage. At the center of the burg, where a bridge of mossy stones connects The Road of Graves on either side of the river, there is a fountain shaped like a hand. Set a bronze coin in the palm, and you will receive the blessing of Gallbladder.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 7
text: Magpie eventually clasps your gauntlet, kisses it, and bids you and your lady farewell. You wave behind her as she goes; your lady does not.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 2
text:
Making your way around a throng of farmers as they chant around an effigy of the moon, your lady's voice reaches you.

*"We'll keep to ourselves tonight.*

*"A place to sleep, and then we must journey farther.*

*"Any one in this hamlet may one of Weige Family's eyes."*

**The Cat in the Pot:**

Your search for lodging has not proven fruitful. You have been turned away from one homestead after another. Every person you speak with seems more concerned with impending impact of the moon.

The village is haunted with cats, both feral and tame. You'll have to watch where you step; a sabaton on a tail will summon a pride-wide scream of wrath, and no villager will speak with you thereafter.

You may come across a small gulley on the western side of The Cat in the Pot. Here, the town has built several glass-bubble enclosures. In each enclosure is a single cat, each of a different color. If you cross the bridge, naming of the colors as you pass each, you will find a dove's-egg stone on the opposite end.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 3
text:
*The lady seems yet more sorrowful since we entered this place. She does not like that poor prostitute. Surely the lady cannot think...*

*No. She knows your oath to virtue.*

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 4
text:
Just as night approaches, you find a farmer who will lodge in his barn.

Though you intended to stay only one night, a vicious storm of sleet and rain rolls in that very evening. The roads are a goulash of mud. You will be forced to stay for several nights. The first is the most unpleasant; the chanting of the townsfolk and the yowling cats drive sleep far from town.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 1
text:
Despite the poor weather, there is a cheerful atmosphere throughout The Cat in the Pot (after the threat of of this harridan's moon has proven to be yet another false one) which piercing through the drizzle and the frost.

On the second day of your visit, the waifish Magpie brings you and your lady two morsels of rye bread, with some cheese of a black she-goat. It is no fine fayre, but your lady does not seem to mind.

Later, you put your Polished Steel Longsword to good use. In a burrow by the river bridge there lairs a small troll. You lure it out with some of the cheese that Magpie left, cut it down, and boil it's skin for fat. The rest of the town is more welcoming afterwards.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Melted Statue:**

An enormous pile of blue, rock-hard wax sits between the two halfs of an hourglass-house pairing. You and your lady have been told that this was once a wax golem, which defended the town from toll collectors. Clearly it has seen better days.

Magpie left you and your lady a spare unbrella when she brought the bread and cheese. This one sports holes as well, but you hold it over your lady's blueblack hair as you stare at the golem's remains. It gives some protection from the rain.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 3
text:
*"My knight, when I sleep, it is my daughter I dream of most.*

*"She asked us once, for a servant like this golem.*

*"When I see her, her face is white as any ghost's, but there is a shadow under her skin.*

*"It is like I am seeing the inside of an eggshell before it is cracked.*

*"Why should it be her ghost in my dreams? Why not my husband's? My sons'?*

*"...*

*"This Magpie - she is poor, but good also."*

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 4
text:
When you are not attending The Blue Lady, you find that there is a surprising amount of work to be done in The Cat in the Pot.

**Farmer Skein's Scarecrow:** If you wish to make some spare spare copper, Farmer Skein will agree to pay you if you destroy his scarecrow, which has attained sentience. One strike will only make it cackle, so be sure to hack it down with at least two blows.

**The Snake Totem:** Be sure to leave your slash in the wooden totem of the snake, beside the lavender-painted house on the east side of town. Other knight have come before...

**Castiron Inn:** You will eventually learn of a cottage that rents rooms. Colloqually called the Castiron Inn, for the enameled iron pots that decorate its long fireplace mantle.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 5
text:
Initially, the owner of the Castiron Inn is quite reticent.

*"The Inn opens after dark, sir.*

*"The Inn opens after dark, sir.*

*"The Inn opens after dark, sir."*

*"The Inn opens... Oh, your friends of Magpie. Need a bed and roof?"*

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 6
text:
Later, you and your lady and Magpie sit before the fire in the owner's cottage. Your Blue Lady stares at the flame, with a sadly serene light reflected in her noble eye. Magpie coughs, then tell you both that she once dreamed of being a lady herself, in The Court of the Leather Dragon.

Quiet hours slip away.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 1
text: It is not until your third evening at The Cat in the Pot that you realize Magpie's tuberculosis is coming to its end.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 2
text:
