You arrive at the prostitute's "home" just as the sun is burning dusky gold across the crests of the river. A dark, mossy hole in the side of the river wall. You can see that, when the water is high, it flood this small and wild chamber where the poor girl dwells.

Magpie lies within on a mat of wool and straw. The bed provides an hour's-worth of comfort for two, and not a moment more. Lice and mold battle in it for supremacy.

Magpie is pale, and shivers and coughs continually. She hold a cloth that was once white to her mouth as she coughs. The cloth is now grey and crimson.

The air is damp and sickly in your lungs. You try to press you lady back towards the opening, but she brushes past you and kneels next to the dying girl. You follow her example. Magpie's eyes shine as sees the two of you; with that luster which only a dying eye may shed.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 3
text:
*"My greatest wish was once to be an elegant woman. Just as you are, your ladyship.*

*"COUGH COUGH COUGH.*

*"I am not ashamed. My means were ever small.*

*"I only wonder at how my dreams have humbled themselves. Once to be a lady. Now, only to be clean.*

*"COUGH COUGH.*

*"Your ladyship, and you sir, are the first to visit my cave in my years, who have not left at the hours end.*

*"I am afraid. I am promised no formal place upon The Road.*

*"I fear a necromancer will steal my soul.*

*"I hope they don't want the souls of whores.*

*"COUGH COUGH COUGH COUGH."*

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 4
text: Your lady takes one of Magpie's hands in her own before you can stop her. She takes her waterskin as well, and when Magpie pauses in her coughing, holds it to the girls lips.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 5
text:
*You must throw that drinking skin away when your lady is not looking. She is too proud and brave, she should not be in here.*

*This girl will die soon. You have seen this before.*

*She may still have the shield of after-life. But you would need to carry her to a sanctum. And the nearest is fifteen miles, back the way you came, back in Ripe.*

*Swear to do it. You know it is the right thing. So do the right thing.*

*But how shall you protect your lady also? She must stay here, hidden. And you must convince her - proud and brave - to stay...*

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 6
text:
Later, while Magpie sleeps more warmly under your woodgreen woolen cloak, your lady whispers in the dark of the cave.

*"My knight, I implore you. Guide me back to the place we have lately fled from.*

*"You and I must take the girl to the sanctum in Ripe.*

*"You thought the same? Of course, my faithful knight.*

*"But you would leave me behind. No, I will not stay.*

*"I charge you to defend us both.*

*"We will return, and find this girl a stone upon The Road.*

*"Under it, she may lie in peace."*

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 7
text: On the morning of your fifth Day at The Cat in the Pot, Magpie's body lies cold and silent as bones under a glacier.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 1
text:
**Üres Cézék Metelnek Zéllel:**

As you carry Magpie's body across the fields of the common dead - over the bridges of the crisscross ditches - you hear the calling echo of a deadpseech spell.

You spin in the direction of the voice, and spy a man through the frosted mist. In the same moment, you drop the body in your arms, and draw your steel.

Then your lady cries, and you see a thousand shapes stretching in the ditches.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 2
text:
The Wiege Family has found you. They've sent a necromancer named Birchbark to finally end your lady's line. His spell has gone out to all the empty dead in this field, and now a monsoon of moldering cats rises from the quadrated, sogging ditches.

Begin the battle by cutting through the two-dozen cats that rise up from the four ditches surrounding you. Each raised cat takes a single swipe to destroy, but their numbers are many.

With the initial wave hewn, cross the bridge to the next square of field. Work your way toward the necromancer. At each stage you will have to cut through another wave of the dead-cat-flood. Do not let the cats reach your lady. Your shield and armor protect you from their pricking bones, but your lady white skin may be torn to bloody strips.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 3
text:
As you reach the field adjacent to the necromancer, Birchbark cackles like a giraffe. You cannot tell whether the laughter is genuine mirth or madness. The necromancer's face is hidden behind a mask.

**Sorrowful Father Mask:** One of twenty-three alabaster masks, which allow the wearer to assume a false identity. The Sorrowful Father grants leather skin, and a resistance to lesser cuts; but fire will burn the wearer to a crisp.

You hear your lady scream. Glancing back, you see her wresting with a human cadaver - Magpie.

At the same moment the necromancer whips a rope-glaive from underneath his robe. The bridge between you and the necromancer dances with the flying iron glaive.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 4
text: To both defeat Birchbark and save your Blue Lady, you'll have to act in precise order. First, block the two flying thrusts from the rope-glaive with your shield. When the glaive become tangled on the bridge, use the moment to turn and sever Magpie's head. Her body will fall lifeless. Block the next glaive-strike, leap across the ditch, and before Birchbark can utter a spell, cut his throat. Be sure to retrieve the Sorrowful Father Mask.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 1
text:
On the first day when the slate-colored sun shines again, you leave The Cat in the Pot. Using three pieces of blank vellum you barter passage on The Witch Manth's Roadhugging Riverraft.

The mill-paddle of the boat send ripples in the wide river ditch's water. Bullfrogs croak and scatter to the banks. The village has dissapeared behind the hillocks and woods.

Standing at the rail of the barge, you watch the markered flagstones of The Road of Graves slide along beside you.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 2
text:
*There are some who lie under those stones - old stones, for souls that died in eons past - who were less deserving of their place than Magpie, that Pleasuress.*

*Who are you, though to speak of 'deserving'? An old knight in a cracked body.*

*This span of the endless road might be as old as the necromancer-gods. Then again, there will be fresher stones, the farther you go from Ripe.*

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 3
text:
The Blue Lady's voice breaks your lapping-water reverie:

*"You, my knight, were thinking of the girl.*

*"Many are those laid in the common fields. We cannot know where he spirit will go, but it may find its rest.*

*"She said she wished to be a lady.*

*"She was fortunate. She died in ignorance of high station."*

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 4
text:
**The Gate of the Ten-Plates Ghost:**

Many hours pass. Witch Manth guides her raft along its only course. You arrive at a huge arch of stacked, mortared, sharp stones. It shadows both the road, and the two river-ditches to either side.

The gate was built two-hundred and thirty years ago. It was built to ward the Ten-Plates Ghost away from Ripe. The Ghost is dead, but the gate remains.

The Bleu Lady has never come this far from Ripe, you know.

Two guards stand on the road, underneath the arch. One hold a spear, another a sling. Both stare at your raft without blinking. One speaks.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 5
text:
*"You're not traders from Ripe, I know that.*

*"Witch Manth doesn't bring traders on her raft, do you, Witch? I know that.*

*"Oh.*

*"I see you're just a knight and his sorrowful father.*

*"If you're going to the western cities on the shore, make sure you carry a sword. The western cities are dangerous.*

*"I know that."*

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 6
text:
You float under the bridge's cold shadow. You float out the other side.

The land changes little beyond the gate, at least at first. The same trees still cast their shadows over the terse brown grass in the fields. In the distance, The enormous Lake of Tomatoes still shines red under the slate-colored sun. The redness of the lake is reflected in wide the eye of your lady palfrey, where it stands nervously on the bucking raft deck.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 7
text: *In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 1
text:
A rumble shakes the air. It rolls up the jagged hills, and through the bristles of the firs. It ripples across the surface of pools. It scatters the fleecy clouds, like a wolf among sheep.

Sourceless the rumble resounds - like and yet unlike thunder. For, the rumbling carries a tune. It is a Stomach Song, with growling, animal notes. It seems almost as if words should accompany the tune:

*"Come love, come sugar, come morsel.*

*"The supper-sun sets very soon.*

*"Hunger's a terrible malady.*

*"And I'm hungry in the shine of the moon."*

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Blue Lady's Last Look of Home:**

You stand beside your lady upon the Road of Graves. She sits atop a nickering palfrey. The hood of her cloak is pulled down, and a soft wind pulls the strands of her blue-black hair west.

West, towards The Lake of Tomatoes.

In the distance, the great lake that shines ever-red under the slate-colored sun is only the size of an apple. The mesas and forests that hug its shores, built along The Road where it curls around the lake, are distant pinpricks of architecture on the landscape. The humid hay smell that permeates the land is steadily fading from the wool of your clothes.

You hold silence as your lady stares back at the distant red spot.

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 3
text:
*"My eyes cannot look away.*

*"Nor blink. As though some fingers fix my sight on a ruined home.*

*"Winding to infinity behind me are the far places of The Road of Graves.*

*"My Cracked Cup Knight, have you not walked there?*

*"The Whiteeye Waste that lies before us - I have heard that is the origin of nightmares.*

*"Nightmares worse than the warring families.*

*"How can I look toward them?"*

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 4
text:
You consider what your lady has said.

*You can't comfort her. The way of virtue is to always speak truly with your master and mistress, but the truth would frighten her.*

*You have walked beyond The Lake, it is true. That was before your body was cracked. That was when you thought you might find The End of the Road. Once upon a time...*

*You went with a caravan then. The Coffin Rollers. As a troupe, The Waste was a perilous place.*

*Now, there is only you to keep your lady fed, watered, sheltered, safe.*

*Do it, then.*

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 5
text:
You turn to face east at last. You take the pafrey by it's reigns and tread upon the Road of Graves.

The road becomes progressively narrower with each step you take beyond the civilized land. The cobbles stones become hardly more than a single line of epitaphs. The grass on the hills whisper with the voice of the wind.

You pass between six black obelisks. Five stand upright, five times the height of a man. One has toppled on its side. These are the border of your lady's world. No lake men dwell beyond. Many miles lie between you and the next farm or village. What shelter is to be had will be found under canvas, in caves - must be claimed by the bite of your Bitter Steel Longsword.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
The little tin bells woven into the mane of your lady's palfrey tinkle as it follows your guiding hand. It's four-beat walking gait taps a rhythm on the thin stone road like a treadle.

A thin grey dust begins to blow. The valleys stretching to either side dark on a darker hue. At first, you think it is the setting of the sun. Gradually, however, you notice that the palfrey steps more softly on the road. It's shoes are muffled by a gathering dust coat that covers the whole landscape; dust that is thin and fossil-colored when it is in the air, but thickens blackly as it settles. Soon, the palfrey shoes make no sound on the earth.

The tinkling silver bells sound out-of-place in the muted space. Your Blue Lady unties the small tin bells and stuffs them with wool.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Whiteeye Waste:**

Lady Horsehair, sixth of the seven god-necromancers, dictated the creation of The Whiteeye Waste. 

*"A door erected must be,*

*"Between Middlemoss,*

*"And the Red Lake of Gallbladder.*

*"Erected, and shut fast."*

The goddess was not born in a barn. She commanded her dollmakers to weave a great curtain of dust; weave it from the shed winter pelts of animals; a hairy curtain door to separate Gallbladder and his faithful, from the civilized peoples of the middle of The Road.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 3
text:
With the dust and dusk gathering shadows swiftly, your first step should be to find shelter. You will find none on the road. Trudge into the disorder of hills whose shadows squat to your left. Keep your eyes open for a conical silhouette with a flat top.

You will come under the umbra of an enormous tree stump. The wood is dry and black as coal. The stump is wide as a clan-fort in diameter, and pockmarked with a tunnels.

This is The Last of Tree of Old Wormapple. It will be your shelter for the night.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 4
text:
The glow of your firebug lamp gleams against old amber in the wooden walls. The floor is packed dust. There are signs of animal passage.

As you enter this Last Tree of Wormapple, that stomach song - that rumble which, must have been playing out in the air still, somewhere almost below the level of human hearing - fades.

No sooner have you begun to strike camp in the hollow trunk, than you a hear chorus of growls.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 5
text:
**Stump Dugs:**

Out of the adjoining blackness, they prowl. Like dogs, they look in shadow. In light, their hair is that coarse, powdery texture of newly burned hair, their eyes are matte white, and their mouths are full of soft, wriggling teeth.

Retrieve your Steel - you will need it. Set yourself before your lady. Keep your shield raised. The dugs will come to you. They attack repeatedly in groups of two to four. You'll have to hold off at least six waves of dugs. Cutting their forelegs off the easiest way to disable them; their jaws work independent of their bodies.

No matter how many you slay, more dugs will flood in. More than your Steel has edge to cut. Eventually, you will be knocked to the dirt. Only your armor spares you their bite. The pack closes around your lady. She has a knife, but hers is a noble hand. She stands helpless before the wriggling teeth.

name: 3 - Mentor - Paragraph 1
text:
It seems as though both you and your lady shall die within this rotten tree.

Then you hear a shout from one of the tunnels. The pack snarls and turns. Meaty thuds resound in the hollow trunk - the sounds of blades and clubs striking dug flesh. The snarling pack turns back to the opening. You regain your feet, and your Steel, and place yourself again before the lady.

After a furious bout of hacking, you are met by a troupe of wild-looking men and women. They wear full leather coats that fall past their knees, cloth masks, and heavy gloves. Their masks hide their faces, but their eyes are grim.

name: 3 - Mentor - Paragraph 2
text:
No sooner do you raise your voice to thank them than three spring at you. You are taken by surprise. Your arms are pinned to your sides.

One of this band - a man - tells your lady to disrobe. You struggle, helplessly. Your lady does as the man says. Her dress falls. There is something ancient and serene in her movement. You turn your eyes away.

The man grabs your lady and pulls her naked body close.

Then another of their band - a woman with violent grey hair and the eyes of a black widow - steps in and pulls the man away.

name: 3 - Mentor - Paragraph 3
text:
**Vettel, the Captain:**

*"No bites? Then leave her, Swineling.*

*"You two are lucky indeed.*

*"The dugs would have infected you, with the Blight that has no cure.*

*"Trying to cross the waste?*

*"Alone, you'd only feed the Hearse."*

name: 4 - First Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
This woman introduces herself as Vettel, captain of this company of The Walkers of the Waste. It is fortunate that you encountered her party - only The Walkers regularly cross the dust-expanse that covers The Road of Graves, where it runs between The Lake of Tomatoes and Middlemoss.

Vettel offers to take you and your lady across. In exchange, she demands your lady's palfrey. Seeing no other option, you accept.

You spend that night with the walkers, inside the stump that they have secured. They are a grim company, mostly men, and you do not shut your eyes much through the starless night.

The captain herself is hard as frozen stone, with all the pity of a cobra. When you ask her about that stomach song which you heard, she tells you only that it is the song of The Hearse. Then she tells you not to venture out by night.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 1
text:
Over the next twelve days you resign yourself to the habits of The Walkers. Each day Vettel barks the company into motion with the rising sun, even when that sun is hidden in whatever shelter you stop at. Each day you trudge through the desert of greyblack dust. Then time when you stop your walking depends upon the location of your nightly shelter - and you take shelter in a cave or ruin each and every night.

One of the walkers is a man with high cheekbones and a broad shiny forehead, named Wrathmaige. He is the kindest to you and your lady, and will answer many your questions about The Whiteeye Waste. He carries the company astrolabe, by which they navigate the dusty plain from shelter to shelter. For, the Road of Graves has long vanished under the carpet of fuzzy dust.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 2
text:
**Other Walkers:**

**Novel:** A shriveled ochre-colored man. He understands you, but will only speak in his own imaginary language of whistles and coughs.

**Worms for Lashes:** If you retrieved it from Birchbark the Necromance, you can use the Sorrowful Father Mask to gain this ancient woman's trust, though she is fated to be no person's friend. She will give you one copper penny each day at noon, but will spit on your sabatons if you refuse.

**Cemetery Stones:** A jovial, bald, pale, violent killer.

**Greymalkin:** The company scribe. Punctuates each of his sentences with a jerk of his head or fingers.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 3
text:
The Walkers take special care to find a roofed, sturdy shelter every night. You realize that there is more to this than simply finder shelter from the cold and the wind-blown dust. That stomach song has followed you across The Waste.

One night as you sit at the fire in the ruin of an old holdfast, with the yellow glow gleaming off your Pinecone Crest Shield, and your Blue Lady asleep at your side, you ask Wrathmaige what is the source of the stomach song.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 4
text:
The moment the questions leaves your throat, Wrathmaige's smooth forehead wrinkles in rage.

*"That is I think too many questions!*

*"Like ripples at the sand of a pond. Smoothing the shore.*

*"That is just how you are, knight.*

*"Gah! This flame is a crowd."*

Before you can say another word, Wraithmaige storms away to a darker corner of the holdfast.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 5
text:
The following morning the stomach song is gone; it never plays in the day. Long after the sun has risen and you have stepped out into the dust beyond sight, Wrathmaige walks up beside you and your lady. He apologizes.

Wraithmaige explains to you that the song is the call of a creature called a Hearse. It is one of the blighted monsters that roam The Waste. It is far more dangerous than the dugs. The Hearse flies. It is like a dragonfly, says Wrathmaige, with a long tail of a hundred stingers, and shimmering wings covered in a thousand tiny eyes, from which you cannot hide.

Wrathmaige tells you that the Hearse will kill and eat Walkers of The Whiteye Waste if it catches them out at night.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 6
text:
*This Walkers has lost a friend - perhaps many friends - to this monsters. He is afraid.*

*You will try to keep him alive. Don't make a vow of it. You have vows enough. But you will hold your Steel and Pinecone Shield in his defense, if you can.*

name: 6 - Second Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
The next day begins with a stronger wind. Your lady holds her shawl close against the biting dust. You stand with shield raised to shelter her.

As the day progresses, the wind rises until it howls against your steel plate. Captain Vettel scowls.

Along with the wind comes a fog of dust. Soon you cannot see more than a dozen paces ahead.

The atmosphere darkens. The walkers cannot find their nightly shelter.

The rumbling starts. The stomach song rises in pitch, growing so loud that it is like sitting on a vibrating stone; it rattles your ear bones.

It is dark. There is a sudden scream. Then, silence.

When dawn comes, the man named Novel is gone.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 1
text:
The dust storm continues without relenting. It covers the sky the next night. And the next. And the next.

You and The Walkers will have to navigate The Waste without the blinded stars and sun. The stomach song will cease at sunrise, then rumble louder as each night approaches. For every day that you fail to find shelter by sunset, the Hearse will kill and eat exactly one member of your caravan.

On the third night when a single Walker is taken, Cemetery Stones will remark cheerily, "Just room for one inside, sir."

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 2
text:
While The Walkers have no fixed harbor, you may still stumble by accident on several possible places to rest without loss of life:

**The Hive House:** A windowless house of green stone, lying in the cleft of a valley; shaped and structured like a beehive.

**Dry Boardwalk:** An old boardwalk beside a parched old lakebed. You can ask Greymalkin to use his Velvet Mirror on the shadows under the boardwalk. If you do the shadows will reveal themselves to be unquiet ghosts, broken and maddened, from when the lake was full. You may put them to rest by pouring red wine over them, at which time the shadows disappear.

**Old Spot's Place:** A calcified mushroom, standing alone at the top of a dustless brown hill. There is a magic toad underneath, which may cure one company member of a mundane curse, but only if you agree to carry it to fresh water.

**Fossil-Shell:** Currently inhabited by tribal necromancers who command swarms of lethal, undead moths. Use a lamp to distract the moths while you cut down the necromancer, and earn yourself a good night's sleep.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 3
text:
One night, your lady wakes from fitful sleep. Presently she speaks.

*"My Cracked Cup Night, I looked upon the red lake again.*

*"From a tower, whose windows were of leaded glass, and locked.*

*"The earth shook.*

*"I saw it ripple across the lake.*

*"It climbed the tower, and I felt it shaking in my hands."*

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 4
text:
After at least seven days and nights in the storm, the sky will clear. The slate-colored sun shines through the fog of grey-black dust for only a moment. But a moment is all Wrathmaige needs. He takes a reading with his astrolabe. According to it, you are at least half-a-dozen miles north of the Walkers' regular route through the waste.

With the sun already past its zenith, you'll have to hurry. Captain Vettel orders a forced march south. Your linens dampen with sweat under your armor.

The faint outlines of Whiteeye's dust-girt trees and dust-girt hills turn indistinct and dark, as simultaneously the storm clears and the sun sets. In the air all around you, the sound of the flying monster with the singing stomach rolls forth.

One of the lead Walkers - you cannot tell which - points and shouts.

You see an altered texture in the distant darkness ahead. There is a precipice, a cliff, with some indistinct structure protruding out from it's surface.

Captain Vettel barks at the walkers to keep moving.

The song swell suddenly. The Blue Lady grips your gauntlet. There is a scream.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 5
text: In the dark, you cannot see who has been lost. The captain hurries you on. When you reach the precipice, you follow along the edge, until you pass under the shelter of the protruding structure. In the dark, you cannot see the structure either.

name: 8 - Third Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
Dawn reveals the trial. Wrathmaige sidles up next to you, thumbs hooked in the pockets of his leather coat.

*"Much is the distance you will fall if you trip.*

*"Best to hold arms wide, palms flat. See, like this?*

*"Gah. You mad knights with your steel sleeves. You make it harder.*

*"Take comfort in the one thing.*

*"Not many miles are left to reach Middlemoss - after we cross The Waste Bridge."*

name: 8 - Third Transition - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Waste Bridge:**

Between where you stand, and the distant dusted surface of The Waste to the east, a canyon of immense proportion lies. It must be miles wide. You cannot see its end to the north or south.

A bridge spans the gap. It is a single, unsupported curve of whitish rock. You know it cannot be a part of the The Road of Graves - there is no earth within which a body may be set and the bridge stones interred. (Unless the constructors of The Road buried the bodies far below, with stones suspended; the thought is unclean).

The bridge start wide at your embankment, but turns progressively thinner as it stretches over the gap. This is no illusion. Wrathmaige explains that the bridge becomes as narrow as two arching, stone beams. The beams offer just enough surface for a single person to walk on either.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 1
text:
The crossing of The Waste Bridge is precarious and windy. You take care to grind each of your metal boots firmly against the windswept stone. When you reach the middle, where the bridge splits in two thin tracks, the wind fades. The air turns unnaturally still. Your hairs stand all throughout this narrow climax.

You cross the twin splits safely. Your lady crosses as well, he arms stretched the sides, her blueblack hair laying flat against her head.

It is just before he steps up to the twin bands of stone that Wrathmaige is bitten by a Nickel Beetle. The Walkers hisses, and bends down and rubs his foot. By then, the beetle as scurried back to the underside of the bridge.

Wrathmaige successfully crosses - all the walkers- but by the time he reaches the other side, he is already pale and sweating.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 2
text:
**Walkers or Hearse:**

As your walk through the waste proceeds east after the bridge, Wrathmaige's condition deteriorates. His skin turns white as alabaster, with his broad forehead and cheekbones standing out almost crimson, and shining in sweat. Wrathmaige cannot keep up with Captain Vettel's remorseless pace - and the captain won't wait around for dusk.

Here, you may choose one of two paths. You can continue on with The Walkers, leaving Wrathmaige behind. If you do, you will reach Middlemoss in two days. Wrathmaige will be taken by The Hearse that very night.

You can instead choose to remain with Wrathmaige. This of course puts your lady in peril. It is also of little practical value; the Nickel Beetle's poison always kills within ten days.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 3
text:
*The sun has snuck past the noon hour while we stepped over that chasm.*

*If only that Hearse descended while it still shone.*

*If only you could see the monster - then this polished Steel in your hand might cut it before it steals another life.*

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 4
text:
Wrathmaige notices you staring between the sun and your sword. He stops shivering for an instant, and rubs his forehead.

*"Gah, I see your thinking, knight.*

*"I say that there is a way for us to summon The Hearse.*

*"We must loudly sing it's belly song ourselves.*

*"Hungry calls to hungry.*

*"I tell you to leave me, but I see your lady will stay, and so shall you.*

*"You are friends."*

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 5
text:
You stand alone with your lady - Wrathmaige breathing heavily on the dust beside you - amidst a field of jagged, crumbling wooden stakes atop a round hill. It must have once been a birch wood.

Your lady's palfrey is gone; The Walker captain, Vettel, took it. Your lady has kept the horse's small silver bells though. Now she jingles them one-by-one, playing their varied notes in a familiar tune.

*"Come love, come sugar, come morsel.*

*"The supper-sun sets very soon."*

In the waning daytime sky, a grumble seems to reply:

*"Hunger's a terrible malady.*

*"And I'm hungry in the shine of the moon."*

A shadow slithers up the round hill. The bell she is holding falls from your blue lady's fingers. She dives between a pair of stakes. You keep your visor on the sky; your knuckles whiten on the hilt of your Steel.

Buzzing it sinks. Softly it lands. Its body is a long, two-ton tumor of chitin; like the gnarled undergrowth of an old forest. It's face is eyeless, all the eyes are upon its humming wings.

name: 10 - Fourth Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Hearse:**

The Hearse is as Wrathmaige described, like a giant dragonfly with a hundred stinging tails. Despite its bulk, however, its gossamer wings propel it swiftly over the earth.

When the battle begins The Hearse will try to return to the sky. To keep it grounded, keep humming the tune which your lady struck upon the bells.

Even on the ground, The Hearse is fast. It will charge at you repeatedly like a bull - don't bother trying to put distance between you. The Hearse will repeatedly launch its thousand-stingers up and over a wing, and send them plunging at you.

To survive, use your Pinecone Crest Shield to block its attacks. If a stinger gets through your shield, your armor might deflect it, but there's no guarantee. While it's retracting the stingers, you'll have a moment where you can swipe at them with your sword.

When you've cut about half it's tail stinger's away, the Hearse will bury you under it's bulk. Get your sword up, and wedge it between the Hearse's belly and the dusty earth. This is your only chance. If you fail, the Hearse will crush you under its mass. If you succeed, it will be impaled.

...

name: 10 - Fourth Transition - Paragraph 2
text: The Hearse tries to rise, then flops sideways. It hums - a last refrain of the stomach song - then slowly fades. The air is silent but for your heavy breathing.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Middle of the Road:**

Tap. Tap. Tap. Tap.

You steps create a staccato and rhythmic tapping sound as you step along The Road of Graves. The air is still windless and silent about you. You feel as if you are stepping down the aisle of a towering stone cathedral, so loud do your footsteps seem.

As you put The Whiteeye Waste and its grey dust farther behind with each step, the greens and blues and brown's of the landscape and the sky return. The transition from huelessness to color is almost like the shift in bleary eyes fresh from sleep.

Finally, you emerge out of a deep gulley, onto a promontory where you behold the land ahead.

Old growth forests cling to the slopes of short, solitary mountain peaks, like a kind of creeping green mold. Thick and pale mists obscure the valleys and divets. You see the the cobalt veins of many rivers running over the scene. And through it all, a widely-cut trail wends. The Road of Graves. In this land, it is one tangles, crisscrossing squiggle. You stand upon one end of it - you cannot see where the other emerges.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
Wrathmaige sighs as he leans upon your paudron.

*"Though many times I have seen The Middle of the Road, yet it is always freshly shining in my eyes.*

*"You see those two white peaks? Closer together than most?*

*"There is middlemoss.*

*"The Heart of an endless road, so I have heard mens' mouths say.*

*"I will not see it again. Though my thanks I give, your rescue was for nothing."*

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 3
text:
Wrathmaige doubles over in a fit of coughs. The poison is killing him, slowly but surely.

You hear a rumble that shakes the air. You step away from the feeble Walker - he falls - and draw your sword.

Wrathmaige only laughs, however, where he lies on the ground. A moment later you understand; rain plinks atop your steel helmet.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 4
text:
Your lady has said little since The Walker's left. Now, she speaks again.

*"These are sleep-mists that gild the forests here.*

*"I have never seen their like upon The Lake. There, the mists are red.*

*"From white mists, ghosts emerge.*

*"I have seen my daughter's face in such a mist.*

*"Never, though, have I seen the face of our enemies."*

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 5
text: Wrathmaige says that there is a hotel only a few miles further on the road. He recommends you make a quick journey to its door; the rain is picking up. Wrathmaige thinks you may even meet the other Walkers of Whiteye there.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 6
text:
*You will keep your lady safe, here, as you did back home. She does not dream of enemies, now that we have put the lake far behind. Perhaps the old enemies are behind as well. Yet, like that Hearse, this world is full of things that would harm her.*

*Harm her, if you did not stop them with your shield and your Steel.*

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 7
text: In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.

name: Setup - Paragraph 1
text:
*"I stepped out from a garden of tall roses, where the thorns cut my skin.*

*"I walked, my knight, for I know not how long.*

*"Suns rolled like a spinning wheel.*

*"But when I reached where the path stopped, I found a different garden.*

*"It WAS different.*

*"Yet though the petals were pink not red, the thorns were already pranked with my blood."*

name: Setup - Paragraph 2
text:
The Lady's words seem muffled as she descends the creaking wooden stairs, into the common room of Cobweb's Hotel. It is as if she spoke through a heavy blanket of wool. This is expected, given the space; the hotel and it's owner seem aptly-named.

You lead your lady through a network of fallen beams and translucent spidersilk curtains. Your Polished Steel Longsword would make short work of the threaded foliage, but you keep it sheathed. The owner, Cobweb, has threatened to throw you out if you break a single strand.

Cobweb watches you as you take your lady's hand, guiding her under an angled piece of oak festooned in iron nails. You hear the flint-skinned man's whistling breathing quite clearly. It is a sound small enough to pass through the webbing which entraps heavier voices.

Your rooms were not spacious, but you and lady carry have few posessions. You slept soundly. Your lady settles herself herself now in a plush, slightly-motheaten archair before the hearth, a space mercifully free of cobwebs. You settle yourself into station behind her.

name: Setup - Paragraph 3
text:
*Her words give vent to the hurt that is in her heart. Poor woman. You would say something to comfort her, but what comfort can you give to one who has lost her home? Her children? Her husband your lord?*

*She sees no hope in these new miles of The Road of Graves. She sees the same feuds and assassins that brought her house to ruin.*

*Is she right? Or is this a different country from The Lake? The Middle of the Road... You walked here, once. That was as a boy. Time and your cracked body have let leak all your memory of this place.*

name: Setup - Paragraph 4
text:
Three others are already seated in the cushioned chairs which circle the hearth of Cobweb's Hotel. The low blue flames behind the grate lend a glacial sheen to each of their faces.

**R, the Killer:** You have met him. Though he has never turned that Horned Morningstar against you or your lady, yet you do not like this man.

**Wrathmaige:** Your newest and only friend. One of The Walkers, bitten during your journey across The Whiteeye Waste. His broad forehead in beads of sweat, and he shivers despite the warmth of the hearth.

**The Mothy Widow:** She says nothing, only stares at the blue veins on the backs of her white hands.

name: Setup - Paragraph 5
text:
A cup from the bar may offer your lady some small comfort. If you navigate your way through the pale veils and the slanting forest of fallen beams, you can order wine at the bar from Cobweb.

Cobweb's flint-colored skin makes his temper undecipherable. His voice when he speaks is like hearing several people talking at once, and your are only able to distinguish snippets of any given line.

To order any beverage from cobweb, you'll have to piece together the exact name of a drink. This is no easy task - Cobweb lists several kinds of wine, but each has an elaborate, sentence-long name, and the names are broken up by his irregular speech pattern. Pick out as many of his words as you can. When he stops speaking, you may ask for one combination. If you give a combination which isn't an actual beverage at the hotel, you'll have to listen to the entire many-voiced, disjointed list again.

The actual drink names are:

- 'My dazzled sight he oft deceives a brother of the dancing leaves.'
- 'Hope and Despair meet in the porch of Death.'
- 'The night that follow'd such a morn had worn a deeper shade.'
- 'Forests may fall, but not the dusk they shield.'

name: Setup - Paragraph 6
text:
No matter which drink you bring to your lady - even if you return with no wine at all - R, the Killer will laugh like a bag full of dry leaves.

*"By my jiggling lungs.*

*"This knight has never lain so much as a finger on a woman's skin.*

*"Have you, Steelclad?*

*"Your laced lady doesn't want wine. She thinks she wants death.*

*"Pity. She aught to rub with some of The Road's weird pleasures."*

name: Setup - Paragraph 7
text:
Like the mothy window, your lady says nothing. Unlike her, your lady stares not at her hands, but directly at R, the Killer.

R takes out a long pipe (with someone else's initials) from a front fold of his padded battle-coat. He places the stem to his thin lips. From another pocket, he takes a pinch of some black leaf, and stuff it in the bowl. There is all of a sudden a lit match between the index and thumb of his left hand, like a miniscule reflection of the flame in the hearth, burning bright blue. He places this to bowl. He inhales. He exhales blue smoke.

name: Setup - Paragraph 8
text:
*"What? You want me to speak?*

*"You want a taste of the Weird. The Pleasant.*

*"Just a cold tongue-tips worth.*

*"Let me fill my belly with this smoke once more.*

*"Ah. Warm and scratchy.*

*"Lean in close then. Mines a doe story. Skittish. I'll need a fur-soft voice."*

name: Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
**THE STORY OF R, THE KILLER:**

You remember her.

Nilghiri.

Now *she* was a someone to fill a pipe's worth of time.

One day in Autumn, you walk along one of those thin dirt tails of the road, and enter the two-score burg of Emily's Gold. The old and young of the village quiver at your coming. Each of the men finds some tool-stick tipped in iron.

When they discover that you kill for gold, however, they lower their weapons.

They have work for a good killer.

An uncontrolled dead thing haunts a wooden castle, which sits in a glen a day's march from the village. The villager's do not trust this undead to stay in its home. They have seen it through the trees when they have lingered out past the slate-colored sunset.

They have a priest-necromancer, who thinks he might exorcize the castle's unliving host. But, he will not go alone. Not without protection. They ask you to use your Horned Morningstar to protect the priest. The villagers will pay you, they say, in thirteen bronze arm-rings which they dug out of an old warrior's grave.

You accept.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
Hours before dawn you have the sparse comfort of a bug-rich bed of straw. Their necromancer-priest travels beside, through the thick oaks and spruce that girt the village.

Your travelling companion is a short, squinting man of somewhere between thirty and eighty. His long window's-peak - like a stretched, fuzzy, grey finger pointing down his forehead toward his nose - seems to be a key component of his spellcasting. Throughout the journey, you catch him stroking it between the index and thumb of his left hand, while muttering under his breath in deadspeech.

You reach the wooden castle before dusk. Its walls are, in fact, nothing more than innumerable oak trees side-by-side. They are grown so closely together that cannot see so much as a crack between the trunks. Their lowest branches are twenty feet above the ground and short, like the branches of redwoods. Beyond the wall you see a chapel and a keep, each made of still-higher trees with woven-together limbs. The castle continually creaks and groans.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 3
text:
Your companions voice rises out of muttered deadspeech, into plain language.

*"Nippy nippy nippy. You're the rough? Of course you are.*

*"You're coarse and quiet.*

*"Name? Wait! Don't say it. Better if my mind's clean.*

*"Find the castle door. Or a castle window.*

*"Lead lead lead us in.*

*"I'll put any restless ones to sleep with a lullaby."*

name: Conflict - Paragraph 4
text:
You find the front door of Castle Celadust with little bother. It is a recessed tunnel, packed with the interlaced roots of two trees. A sign before the tunnel says: 'This castle's doors with never open'.

The sign is accurate. You won't be able to get through the main entrance. If you search around the exterior though, you'll find a hole in the trunks on the southern portion of the wall. Termites have bored through the wood. You break away the infested, spongy wall to widen the opening, allowing you to pass through the exterior wall.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 5
text:
Once inside Castle Celadust, you can start your search in The Mother's Chapel, the barracks, or the main keep. You may encounter Vigorous Blood or Skinrights in any of these places.

No matter where you start, you'll end up descending into the dungeons. You'll have to fight the Rolling Walrus Bones that occupies this floor. From a distance, the Bones will launch beams of Walrus Fire, which you'll have to dodge; in melee, the Bones will engulf you and tear you to pieces; keep your distance... Dodge the fire beams, and use a thrown hammer or other blunt object to slowly chip away at the skeletal tumbleweed. If the Bones gets in close, you can still destroy it by getting in one good swing with your Horned Morningstar.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 6
text:
In the blink of an eye her breath on your neck. Nilghiri. She manifests out of portal in the stone on your right, which was not there when last you looked. As you spin, raising your morningstar, her eye catches your eye.

You hear the necromancer-priest stammer and shout. But, his voice is muffled.

Nilghiri's eyes are like two enormous plates of faceted onyx. The sere, loose, decaying gauze which wraps the rest of her body is nothing to you. Her hair is uncannily vital - lusterous gold curtains peeking through her wraps - and her lips are full and red. It is those bottomless onyx pits, however, which cause your morningstar to dip, and your jaw to hang slack.

You do not hear the priest's body when it strikes the floor.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 7
text:
*"Invader.*

*"Not full of hubris, though. Not as was that false voice of the necromancer.*

*"More handsome, as well.*

*"Sink into my eyes. Breath deeply the music of my voice.*

*"You've slain my guardians. Now, be one yourself."*

name: Conflict - Paragraph 8
text:
You're service to Nilghiri the mummy is largely spent patrolling the rootmade floors and wooden walls that form her castle of trees. You kill rabbits and foxes that slip through the wall with a stone slingshot, and keep the population of rats down by setting out piles of yellow powder. You tend the gardens. You bring cold water up from a deep well.

Sleep is an old habit now - you no longer *need* sleep. Your nights are spent standing over the limestone sarcophagus of your mistress. She leaves the stone lid open. While the moon and stars wheel overhead, you stare at her body. She is beautiful, though cannot see her skin.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 9
text:
*When did you break from the hypnosis? When did your service change from forced, to willing?*

*She was lovely to look upon, and never a cruel woman. Not in death.*

*You remember her eyes, even to this day. The season have taken her voice, her hair, her red lips. Everything. But not her eyes. You still have those.*

*Your pipe is burning low. There isn't much smoke left to tickle your lungs. Come to the end while your throat's still warm.*

name: Conflict - Paragraph 10
text:
How many days - how many years - pass for you in Celadust, you do not know. It cannot be more than half a decade, when it is over, your face will have few new wrinkles.

While you remain in the castle you'll have to fight other mercenaries hired by the village. Most of these are young blades - you can either scare them off, or kill them.

After four to seven lesser fights, you'll face a fellow professional.

**King Ikno:**

Before you can defeat King Ikno you'll have to kill his two henchmen. The brambles will slow down the warriors, so stick to the tangled rooms of the castle. Once you've got them thoroughly caught up in the roots and vines, close quickly. Open their skin with your knife. (Your Morningstar needs too much room for this kind of blood work).

After his henchmen are dead, King Ikno will attack you. His greataxe is better at cleaving through the tangled plants, but Nilghiri will arrive at that exact moment. With her stare instilling a cold into his body, Ikno's hands will turn numb. Eventually he'll drop his axe. Give this 'king' the same end you gave to his subjects.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 11
text:
Each dawn as she wakes, you see that those gemstone eyes seem softer. Smoother. After some time, they look less like gems, more like the wet globes of two ink-filled jellyfish.

One day, you catch the mummy as she sings The Elegy of the Equinox. The song allows her to see in the cold when the stars shine over her linen covering. She catches you watching her, your face reflecting in the corner of her obsidian eye. She sings on. Her voice rings like theramin in your ears.

A few evenings later, you and Nilghiri sit beside one another on a bough, in the high canopy of the tallest tower of Celadust Castle. You watch the owls swoop for mice against the dark sky.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 12
text:
*"A too-willing servant you have been.*

*"You have no resentment in your face when you guard my casque after dark.*

*"No resentment, but worse.*

*"To feel warmth within my breast again - that would cast me down to ash as no blade yet has.*

*"I release you from my spell. Leave this castle, my handsome invader."*

name: Conflict - Paragraph 13
text:
The spell was broken already. You stay.

The next dawn as she awakes and finds you in her wooded grand hall, Nilghiri threatens to melt you with her magic. You drop your Horned Morningstar with a hollow thunk. You hold your arms with fingers splayed, and invite her to destroy you.

That evening, you lay down. Together.

name: Conflict - Paragraph 14
text:
*Never did one sleep more soundly in a grave as you slept, in that grave of Nilghiri's, on that night.*

*Imagine that a seed falls from its ash tree in autumn. Before it can find the soil, a man takes it, and places it in a wooden box. All through the winter it lays in the box, while the cold snows fall. Then, when Spring brings its birds and rain, the man buries the seed in the soil at last. To return finally to that warm earth for which it was destined from birth - that is how you felt on that night. It was a warmth you never knew before.*

name: Conflict - Paragraph 15
text:
In the morning you wake up next to a pile of ash-covered linen. Nilghiri is gone.

When the hunters arrive later that afternoon, you pretend that you fought and exorcized the undead monster haunting Celadust Castle. They take you at your word and offer to guide you back to the village.

Before you go, you want to collect a few of the valuable items which the mummy collected over the years:

**Ferald Picked Farie:** A tiny farie suspended in lime-colored brine in a jar. Dead, yet there is some magic left in its body.

**Glass Merigold:** Orange.

**Coldsoil:** This pot of special soil can keep undead worms alive... Dead? Alive-dead?

name: Conflict - Paragraph 16
text:
You return to the village. You are paid. You leave.

The castle made of trees has begun to rot in the succeeding years. It is a ruin now.

And the village? Two summers ago, you heard that a priest of The The Scratcher came, and sewed a deadly miasma over the crops. Those few villagers who survived left for other dwellings.

You moved on. You spent your thirteen bronze arm-rings. You made more coin. That is true Life upon the Road of Graves.

\#

name: Drive - Paragraph 1
text:
R, the killer, exhales the last of the smoke that is in his lungs. He says no more.

In the chair nearest to the fire Wrathmaige stirs. The pallid shivering Walker breathes deep with a shudder.

*"This man said about a seed buried in warm earth.*

*"His seed was caught in Early Spring Frost, I am thinking.*

*"But some seeds live to be tall, tall trees.*

*"COUGH COUGH COUGH.*

*"Wonderful things are on this Road.*

*"The Mystery in a handful of dust. The songs of whip-poor-wills. The things that live in old woods."*

\#

name: Drive - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Story of Wrathmaige:**

A bootlace shaped loop, of the knobby-skinned root, of a spruce that uphold the sky, in The Old Soulwood - catches your foot. You stifle a shout as you fall. You land on your shoulder at the top of a wooden slope. Your fall finally ends when you have rolled to the bottom.

Your body, battered by the trunks and turf, stings, yet you stifle a cry. You catch your wind in silent gasps - the smell of moss and rain and old forest earth fills your lungs. You listen. There are no calls of birds. No other fearful animal howl. The only noise is a single cricket that seems to follow you through the woods, repeating continuously a high, squeaking, rusty-wheel chirp.

Your companions are dead, and you've lost your way back to The Road of Graves. The Old Soulwood's pillar-evergreen roll up and down the shaded earth in all direction.

name: Drive - Paragraph 3
text:
You listen longer, but there is still only the cricket. Having caught your breath you swallow and wet your throat.

*"Anybody is there? Help. Please.*

*"I have seen a gigantic monster. A lynx, but as big as a bear, and wide like a salamander.*

*"A Periwinkle Cat."*

*"Help me to be not lost. I promise I'll serve you."*

name: Drive - Paragraph 4
text:
**The Periwinkle Forest Cats:**

The Periwinkle Forest Cats are the ancient defenders of The Old Soulwood. Any traveler who strays from The Road, where it passes through the woods, may catch the slit yellow eye of a cat. Periwinkle Forest Cats always kill an adult man if they catch him wandering off the cobbled stone epitaphs. They sometimes allow children or women to pass unscathed.

It is said by certain among the oldest ghosts that these cats are the spirits of the Bouldsatr, the giants who planted the first trees of The Old Soulwood. While their bodies were hunted to extinction, the giants refused to die. They possessed the bodies of the native wild cats, to become the forest's eternal guards.

name: Drive - Paragraph 5
text:
**Strange Light:**

The semitranslucent light emerges from the branches above you like a line of fuchsia-colored drool. This wisp's glow is so soft that you only notice it when it has coagulated to a ball inches from your ear, and sheds a faint warmth against your cheek.

You can jump up draw your spear, but you'll scare the wisp away. The stalking cats will probably track you down before you find your own way out.

Instead, keep your body movements slow and your breathing steady. You should hold out your hand slowly to the glowing ball, as if to a horse. Don't mind the tingling sensation - that is just the wisp tasting your emotions.

If you have any Speech Cheese, eat it now; you'll be able to understand and speak in the tinny voice of the wisp. By speaking tenderly to it, you can convince the wisp to reveal secret underground entrances to the five major temples in Middlemoss.

name: Drive - Paragraph 6
text:
*You think - COUGH COUGH COUGH - think the glow light is not a usually friendly one. Some trees will leak off the souls they've ensnared, like sap, to lure others. When you taste the sap, it will shrink you, until you are small enough for the tree's digestion.*

*COUGH COUGH COUGH.*

*But you think that the stalking Periwinkle will rip your bones apart if you stay lost. And the night is darkening the canopy. Following a stranger-light: may be worse than following the cuckoo in the woods. But, may be good.*

*What else can you do? Follow - COUGH - follow the mushrooms?*

name: Drive - Paragraph 7
text:
Through the branches there rises the terrible, titanic war cry of the cat. It is a deep yowl. The sound is to the call of a normal lynx as the bellow of a bull is to the squeal of a piglet.

You fall into step behind the fushcia ball as it floats over the roots. Unpathed tracts of The Old Soulwood roll under your feet.

At one point, you spy four children in deer masks. They dance and skip around a bush of bright-red strawberries. As soon as they notice you, they evaporate like dew in the sun.

**The Eye of the Woods:**

A glade opens up to the sky. Spanning it, you see a wide pond of eggshell-colored water.

This is the blind eye of The Old Soulwood. It's water is waist-high across its bed; except that there is one hole just large enough for a man to fall into, which leads to an ocean in another place.

name: Drive - Paragraph 8
text:
*"Be pleased not to lead me in there, light orb.*

*"I'm afraid of drowning.*

*"I want my bones to be buried on The Road.*

*"Not lost.*

*"Take me out, and I will find you a blue potion to make your color more purple."*

name: Drive - Paragraph 9
text:
Another *Yowl*. Like thunder.

You follow the wisp into the pool.

As you cross, you produce no ripples in the water. There are several faces which you might see in the eggshell surface.

**Old Necromancer:** You stare down at a face of folds, wrinkles upon wrinkles. The white eyebrows stretch to each cheek until they interweave with the white hair, into the white beard.

**Bluechid:** This pudgy baby's face is blue - as if starved of air - but the mouth is open in a smile.

**Inn Hound's Head:** The tired dog stared at you with drooping eyes.

When you reach the other side, you turn and glance back.

Across the water, between two trees, a hulking feline shadow creeps.

name: Drive - Paragraph 10
text:
*COUGH COUGH.*

*No. You won't settle back into the chair cushion. You will tell these other guests - COUGH COUGH COUGH - tell these guests how you escaped that old soul-filled forest.*

*COUGH COUGH COUGH.*

*The Blue Lady should attend her ear. This story has a good end.*

name: Drive - Paragraph 11
text:
When you turn back, the fuchsia glow is fading into the hollows tunnel of a tree. With the Periwinkle Forest cat already stepping out into the water, you'll want to follow the wisp.

The hollow is guarded by a pair of scorpion stoats. You need to kill them in order to pass farther in. Their long bodies are covered in thick plates of chitin, but their heads and tails are weak.

Once you've killed the guardians, you'll find the wisp has vanished. You'll have to find it. You can be sure your path through the winding hollows is right by only walking down tunnels that run with the rings of the tree. If you go through more than three layers at once, you've taken a wrong turn, and the cat will catch up.

After you've climbed a hundred steps, you'll step out onto a branch overlooking-

name: Drive - Paragraph 12
text:
*COUGH COUGH COUGH COUGH.*

*Nooo. Hunted by the cat is not how the story end - COUGH COUGH - end...*

*End...*

\#

name: Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
The sweat has ceased to bead upon the long, pale forehead of Wrathmaige. His reflect glassily the blue fire under the grate.

It is The Aged Widow's voice that breaks the silence.

*"Sir knight, ladyship - are you pleased with that end?*

*"No?*

*"The sun is rising. And my dying bones have far to travel.*

*"Still, I will give you a better story to take through The Middle of the Road."*

name: Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Story of The Aged Widow:**

You and your husband have stepped across the Athidal in Middlemoss - over the sulfurous gorge that splits this cemetery of the living - and entered the town's House of Maze. You've come to beg a mystery from The Master of the Puzzle Mask.

After you've ascended the Stairs of Falling Echoes and given a gift of pure water to one of the attendants, you and your husband will be led to separate rooms. The Master enters your room after you. He gives you your mystery: 'two men were seen marching onto the eastern end of the dry river wharf yesterday, yet only a little blonde girl came out of the western side.'

When you meet your husband again in the guest's anteroom, already you feel that something is different. There is something incorrect in the shape of his body.

You cast your eyes down, however, and with the man, depart.

name: Finale - Paragraph 3
text:
There is little value in the mystery you received in The House of Maze. You'll have to find some other way to earn black bread for your supper.

**Tailor's Tester:** Yarnstitchweave the Tailor wants someone to try on his latest graveclothes, checking them for lice, or mold, or skin disease.

**Squeaker:** The town butcher needs more squeakers; men who go into the woods and trap the giant rats. He'll pay five copper chits for a female, one bronze for a male.

**Leechee:** The college of Leeches is in need of good, strong blood. If you sign a contract in leather with any of the Leech's, they will cover you in the animal for which their profession is named, and draw a few pints. You'll make no money, but they will offer you supper, and you can take some home.

**Pet Cemetery:** The keeper of the town petting zoo needs someone to look after his four species of animal: goats, pigs, chickens, and his special praying mantis.

name: Finale - Paragraph 4
text:
That night over supper, your husband's words are wrong.

*"Aged Wife, how young your skin is tonight.*

*"What? Why do you stare at me so?*

*"A husband should remind his wife of her beauty"*

name: Finale - Paragraph 5
text:
That evening, as you are unbuttoning your husband's shoulder-cloak for him, your hand touches his collar. The bone feels wrong. You ask the man if the cloak was enough to keep him warm today, when he went out on the moor over town. You're husband looks at your eyes directly. He says he didn't go on the moor.

It is not until the following morning that you know for certain the man on your straw mattress is not the one you wed.

Every morning, your husband gives you a rhyme dependent on the circumstances of the day. "Warm rain on the windowpane," on a drizzly summer morning. "My toes have gone numb and the birds are all mum, and your dress is the color of gum," if he sees you wearing your pink wool shirt and it is snowing.

This morning, he says nothing.

name: Finale - Paragraph 6
text:
You wrack your brain, and after several hours you remember one likely imposter.

**Doppelganger Skeletons:**

These immortal masters-of-disguise are the everlasting remains of cunning necromancers.  They cannot endure outside of flesh. When their body grows old, their skeleton rips its way out. They find a new host, tear the host's bones out by the spine, then climb into the vacant body. Several of these undead servants are said to work in The House of Maze.

name: Finale - Paragraph 7
text:
You know of some possibly ways to test for a fake skeleton in your husband's body. Any inside-seeing statue of a woman would be able to look through his skin as through a window. You might also purchase an enchanted bloodstone - they turn pale and shiver when a body near them is made from multiple people.

Both such tests are beyond your means.

*You know that the man in your home is no longer your husband. You are certain.*

*Will you live with the changed man?*

name: Finale - Paragraph 8
text:
Later that day, you tell the doppelganger skeleton disguised as your husband that tonight is the night when you must deliver black bread and tomatoes, to the harridan which lives out on The Wounded Hills.

The man does not protest.

You feel some remorse, but little fear, later that evening as you cross the moonlit moor. You yourself took a dram of Lemon Cider in secret before you left home. You are protected.

The black heather rustles. It is not the wind.

Your husband screams as the greyskinned dead emerge. Spittle gleams in streams from the fanged teeth and long tongues.

Ghouls.

You shudder as the dead swarm in around. Yet they run past you. The doppelganger turns and goes to run, but the ghouls are on all his sides. You drop your bread and tomatoes. Higher screams. There is a ripping noise.

You turn away. You hurry back the way you came. You cannot see the pulp and gore spattering the heather behind you, but you hear that leathery tearing, and the cracking of the false bones.

name: Finale - Paragraph 9
text:
*You never went back in the day. You never checked the body.*

*But you know you were right. That man was not your husband, even if it was his flesh. Even if- if it was his soul.*

*Which bones are yours?*

*Which, a stranger's?*

name: Finale - Paragraph 10
text:
The Aged Widow falls silent.

R, the killer smokes another pipe.

You stand stock-still behind your Blue Lady.

The living - and one dead man - stare into the fire at Cobweb's Hotel.

name: Finale - Paragraph 11
text: *In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 1
text:
A bright white light crackles sideways through a cataract of wind. You only see the blush shadow of the light, it's blush against the landscape. The dark epitaphs of The Road, and the hills surrounding, brighten to grey for an instant. Then, darkness again.

It is difficult to hear over the howling of the wind in your helmet; but, you feel The Blue Lady grasp your left gauntlet, and attend her shout.

*"Cracked Cup Knight, I heard a voice ahead.*

*"On for a moment, in a lull after that crackling bolt.*

*"The voice was singing.*

*"Someone waits ahead of us on The Road of Graves."*

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 2
text:
Another sound like a thunking butcher's axe comes from your right. You turn your visor.

You see the indistinct outline of your fellow-traveler: R, the Killer. He stands partially bent, his Horned Morningstar extended, having just crushed a hurtling tumbleweed.

R jerks his Morningstar loose; he staggers.

*"How's your precious lady hearing anything over howling?*

*"Maybe she's sharper these days. He he.*

*"Lets hope she's right.*

*"This storm'll blast us to bits soon."*

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 3
text:
**Bent Squall:**

The bent squall is an apparition of driving jaguar winds, flowing streams of black cloud, and sideways lightning. They are local to this region of The Road of Graves. They killer quicker than blizzards, faster than floods, more swiftly even than some fatal poisons. 

You know that your Lady is in peril. You lead her swiftly, plunging ahead. R the Killer follows.

The shapes which gradually condense through the black cloud-streams - solidifying on both sides of the road - are familiar to your eye. Wagons, large as houses.

You quickly lead your lady into the windscreen which the nearest wagon offers. As you step under the tall, crooked, leaning shadow of canvas and wood, the howling ceases. Your Lady's cloak relaxes around her shoulders. Beyond the twin lines of wagons along The Road, the clouds of the storm are like cave walls.

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 4
text:
Freaks peek from the canvas wagon flaps. Pale freaks. Freaks with too many teeth, too few eyes. One freak face all of warts drags a bent freak body out of the flaps.

*"Friends of Madams. Friends of Misters. Friends of pigs and goats.*

*"A host of friends, we.*

*"The Coffin Rollers, we; for we roll over the coffins of The Road.*

*"Enter our wagons.*

*"Let your eyes be stricken with show."*

name: 1. Title Card - Paragraph 5
text:
You can either accept the freak's offer, or decide to press on through the storm.

If you choose to press on, R the Killer will part ways with you. All the wagons will shoot forward in a rush, and you'll be left in the storm. You'll discover that there is no other shelter to be had. Eventually, a side flying bolt will strike you down.

If you stay, the warty Roller will give you a tour.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Coffin Rollers:**

The Coffin Rollers are groups of tramps, outcasts, and rejected freaks, who roam up and down the endless Road of Graves. They earn money by putting on circuses and pageants in every town they visit. At least, that's what the Rollers say...

The varied vagrants that travel with the caravan come in all shapes and sizes, colors and talents. Some are simple dwarves. Some are necromancers. Some are sane.

Each caravan has one Master. To identify themselves, Rollers carry a wooden coin bearing their master's symbol. When you ask your tour guide, he shows you a piece of birch. It looks as if it has been broken into three pieces, and sewn back together with hair.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 2
text:
As you march down the wagon lines, several features attract your eye.

**Legbones Man:** One freak sits on the stairs of his house-sized wagon. His upper half is hidden behind the flaps of the entrance. Of his lower body, you see two skinny legs clad in loose red silk. Each leg is crooked acutely, pointing up at the tunnel of clouds. You guess the limbs to be nine feet long.

**The Hypnotist:** A woman with a warty, wide, froglike face stares at you from the round window of a wagon. Her eyes spin in their sockets. You feel the hair stand on your neck.

**Carousel of Bones:** The source of the singing which your lady heard is made clear when you pass a flat-topped wagon. Upon it, there is a carousel of horses shaped from cat bones. A girl the size of a cat spins the carousel with a crank, while singing through the numbers one through twelve to the tune of the calliope.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 3
text:
*What is the purpose of these scores of Wax Golems that you see? Why do they guard the wagons? Where did these Rollers acquire such an army?*

*These Rollers are not to be trusted. Vaguely only do you remember when you followed on the road with them. That was long ago. They were avaricious, though, and without scruples.*

*Your Lady must have her rest. Yet, you would leave as soon as the storm passes.*

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 4
text:
R the Killer's voice breaks your contemplation.

*"Where's our guide vanished to?*

*"Cursed bones. That hypnotist woman distracted me.*

*"And look at them, the freaks.*

*"They're cutting us off."*

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Paragraph 5
text:
The Coffin Rollers have indeed formed a deformed wall behind you, and more gather ahead.

You'll need to fight through at least the two closest rollers: two furred men with glass-embedded staves. They hit like battering rams, but their attacks are slow. Let R handle one with his Morningstar. Keep out of the other's range, and whittle him down with your Bitter Steel Blade.

By the time you've killed the hirsute brothers, only one route remains; a wagon-building with the flaps drawn wide.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 1
text:
**Silk Maze:**

You enter the largest wagon in the caravan (on the inside, anyway.) Silks abound, hanging from a ceiling hidden high in blackness, down to the creaking wooden floor. The silks are of a thousand muted colors: Cream, Mauve, Artichoke, Top-of-straw-hat, Old-dog-mouth. The wagon smell faintly decayed, as if one mouse's death had been provision for the vast space.

There is one long, loud, descending hiss as you plumb the first curtain. All the silks flutter. You feel your lady's arm grasp at your armor. R growls and slashes a curtain. The sound is not repeated, though from somewhere deeper in the forest of curtains, you hear hushed voices.

A cry from outside reminds you that you are prey.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Paragraph 2
text:
Each time you pass through one of the Silk Maze's curtains, it takes you to a random room in the maze. Most rooms are empty. R will grab any of the colorful clothing, but don't let him spend long looting. The freaks are still searching for you.

Each room you enter has a twenty percent chance of being occupied by at least two Coffin Rollers. There is an additional five percent chance you'll stumble into a Wax Golem.

**Wax Golem:** A nine-foot, animated, hostile statue made of wax. There is something pale encased in it.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 1
text:
After exploring the maze for some time, you'll stumble randomly into a silken room of eight sides, eight exits. One of the Wax Golems waits within. You and R will have to take the Golem out first. You fight well with the killer, but that is not a comforting thought.

While you slay the golem, the room is filled with screaming. It is not until broken chunks of yellow wax litter the floor that you identify the source.

**Bonnie and Butter:**

Two small, round, red-haired girls sit on a purple ottoman, between twocarved wooden totems, in the middle of the room. They are twins; identical from the pitch of their screams to the translucent of their afterbirth-thin skin. They seem to be about twelve years of age.

Many seconds after the golem is slain, the twins stop screaming. Their mouths remain open for a moment, heaving in unison. Every breath seems to jiggle through their entire bodies - as if, despite their size, each inhalation whirled in them like a hurricane.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 2
text:
When Bonnie and Butter speak, it is in turns. Their voices are so similar that it is like hearing one person.

"These three are dressed too oddly for Rollers."

"They're dressed too oddly for guests either."

"Guests too."

"Guests too what?"

"I mean they're dressed too oddly for guests too."

"That's what I said, except I didn't say too twice because it's a bit much."

"Why does it matter how many times I say too?"

"Because it's common, stupid."

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 3
text:
You keep your Polished Steel between these two girls and your lady. The weapon gleams. It reflects a the candle burning atop one of the totems. It reflects the four eyes of the twins, two sets, two eyes on either side of the blade, so similar that they appear mirrored.

Bonnie and Butter seem to pay the weapon no mind. Their reflected and their physical eyes dart between the eight silken exits.

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 4
text:
"Sometimes we smuggle."

"Not us personally."

"I didn't say us personally, I meant the Rollers smuggle for The House of Maze."

"We hate mazes."

"Hate them a lot."

"Especially this maze."

"Yes, because Patchwork lives in this maze."

"But mainly because its hard to get out of, and we don't like things that are hard to get out of, we like like things that are hard to get into."

"Like clothes and locked boxes."

"But we can't get into this room."

"Because we're already inside of the maze."

"And you can't get in something when you're already there."

"And that is why we hate mazes."

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 5
text:
R the Killer snarls.

"Cut the babble.

"Cupknight, I hear clunking feet.

"That Man Made of Metal we saw; he's coming.

"My Morningstar won't break him.

"Lets kill these two and go."

name: 4. False Hopes - Paragraph 6
text:
You can either stop R's descending blow before rushing from the room with your Lady, or let the Killer kill the twin. If you spare the twins, their voices give hysterical pursuit.

"You'd never kill us anyway."

"Maybe they could once."

"I mean never forever."

"Patchwork would bring us back."

"He'd bring us back and make us shine his buttons and polish his nails."

"He is the first master of dying."

"The Thin Man."

"Patchwork."

"Thinman. Patchwork. Thinman. Patchwork! Thinman! PATCHWORK!"

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 1
text:
The Maze of Silk feels endless. Freaks, chasing endlessly. Your poor Blue Lady running, gasping, endlessly fatigued. R's endless curses.

You do not know how many minutes, hours, days have passed. There is no food or water, but your stomach never rumbles, your tongue is never parched. Only your lungs burn, and your skin under your armor dampens with sweat.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 2
text:
Beyond the same rooms with bright clothes and stacked furnishing, or the rooms stocked with Waiting Coffin Rollers and Wax Golems, a few of the silken spaces you may enter are noteworthy.

**Beetroot Picklery:** Two hundred gnarled roots float in an obfuscating green fluid, upon three long racks of metal shelves. It is hard to tell, but it seems as if the roots turn insude their jars, following you as you pass.

**Shaggy Franklin:** A black goat with long, tangled hair paces in wide circles in this room. If you search in its pelt, you can find a nugget of Speech Cheese.

**Theater:** A small box of a room, with the silks hanging so close to each other that your pauldrons rub again your Lady and R's shoulders. On one side, there is a stage for puppets.

**The Dwarf Compressor:** A hole in the shape of a normal-sized man opens on one side of the copper machine - the shape is the same on the other side, but two feet in height.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Paragraph 3
text: After plunging through curtains for what seems like eternity, you enter a special room.

name: 6. The Master Appears - Paragraph 1
text:
**Room Three One Seven:**

A curtain shining black beads obscures half of this room.

Something breaths on the other side. The breathing is many-faceted, as if it were several somethings. But the sounds all come from a point to small for more than one living thing.

At the first breath, you see a curtain rustle.

Another breath. Another curtain flutters. As the breathing continues, all around the silken hangings writhe. Like feet under a gibbet. Even the shining beads dance, clattering.

Behind the beads you hear a creak. Something rises from a chair as ancient as the cracked desert earth.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 1
text:
A howl of foes explodes through the dancing curtain. Your hand is on the hilt of Polished Steel Longsword in a fraction of a blink. The blade shines free.

The freaks pour from all sides, with not one congruity between them. One is taller than the golems, twelve feet at least, but hunched into such a U of spine that he skitters like a catarpillar on his palms and feet. Your blade cuts his face as he scuttles toward your lady. Another gives you pause; a Big Brother. He is about your height, twice your weight, shaved, and with muscles so striated that his skin seems covered in veiny cobwebs.

Your lady shouts as the mass closes on you. She steps backward toward the black curtain. She stops. From the other side, you both hear the breathing swell nearer.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Paragraph 2
text:
There is no escape from The Coffin Rollers. Your best option is to slay a few. You can kill either a large number of the frailer ones, or fell a few of the strong. The Strongman himself is too hardy for your blade to cut him before they take you, but it might be a good idea to kill the toad-woman-hypnotist, or the dungbeetle man. Both will make later battles more difficult.

Swinging, blocking, you nevertheless are buried under a grabble of mutant bodies.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 1
text:
Something warm, wet, and crimson slides over your eye, beneath your helmet. Your body burns with a thousand bruises.

The Coffin Rollers drag you from The Silk Maze. Your metal shoes leave gouging trails in the boards. As your fog clears, you wheel your visor from side to side. You do not see your lady. They have taken her.

You are pulled by two ruddy men with manacle fingers down the long rows of wagons. Your armor scrapes on The Road's epitaph stones. Your left poleyn mars one stone, so that 'Janus: 1919 to -' is left without his death date.

The crowd drags you for exactly five-hundred paces. On the stoop of the wag they stop at, you meet another of their unusual tribe.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Contortionist:**

The man on the wooden steps sits with his elbows and knees all in a knot before him; you can't tell which are elbows, which are knees; there seem to be more than four joints total. Through this appendicular seal of Melchizedek, a head of snowy hair and bear peeks. It smiles with a mouth of white teeth.

"Brothers and sisters, beasts and slaves; what a test you've brought me.

"I've never wrapped around a body like *him* before.

"Bones, covered in flesh, covered in tin.

"Hmmmm. What an onion.

"Come in, won't you? I've some drinks that will relax your nerves."

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 3
text:
**The Chemical Show:**

This wagon is oppsite of The Maze. On the outside, the 'Chemical Show' sign above the canvas flaps appeared atomic against the towering, crooked, shadow-casting mass of the giant wagon. Within, it is a modestly-sized room, not much bigger than the bed of a regular wagon.

Two long beams, lousy with nails, span the ceiling. A dozen wired skeletons are stacked across the beams. Smaller sets, cats and dogs, hang from the nails.

One huge steel vat bubbles against the far wall as you are shoved inside. It fills the room with a daffodil musk; it is full of hot, scented wax. There are also an assortment of skins, jars, bowls, and beakers, which rattled like a percussion section as you are shoved into a sturdy wooden chair.

Your hands and legs are bound to the chair. You see your sword and shield, but they are set away from you, beside the entrance flap.

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 4
text:
*You must find some way out of these leather straps. You must find your Blue Lady and flee.*

*Your lady - will they hypnotize her? Will take her hair, or age her into a harridan? She could bear it, in her nobility; you could not.*

*Will they lay her at the foot of their hideous master? Surely it was him who you heard, breathing behind that curtain of black beads.*

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 5
text:
A piece of paper catches your eye. It is partially buried under the corner of the wagon's catskin rug. It looks like a poem, but you can only see the last stanza.

"The frame breaks loose from the stifling spruce

"To the clack of timber chimes

"And the delicate moon like a silver balloon

"Will smile in its own good time.
