"With the speed of the unburdened bones, he leaps,

"That bard with skeleton free,

"And the plummeting jumps descend rolling lumps

"From the hills to the crested sea."

name: 8. Introduction - Paragraph 6
text:
While you remain fixed in the chair, The Contortionist unravels a few of the links in his limbs. He starts pulling a bottle of glazed fir needles, a pouch of knuckle dust, and two slivers of moose antler. He setting them out on a tiny stone-topped workspace, beside the bubbling vat.

By following the actions of the contortion, you can learn the recipe for Violet Jelly.

**Violet Jelly:** An alchemical unguent, a stable among necromancers. Melts tissue, but leaves bones intact.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 1
text:
Thudding footsteps herlad the entrance of another into the wagon. A moment later, you watch The Strongmen squeeze through the flap. Wrapped in his spiderwebbed arms, he holds R, the squirming Killer. The latter is lashed to the chair beside you.

"Undo these leather straps you stinking freaks.

"Undo them, so I can show your brains the open air.

"I'll crack and puncture your skulls.

"Ah. So they've captured you too, cup knight?

"Rotten bastards.

"Hey, tangled man, don't touch that! Don't touch my precious star."

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 2
text:
While you can reach neither your own Polished Steel, nor any other sharp tool, your armor plates themselves can cut. The elbow couters are rusted and sawtoothed.

Trying to cut your own bonds produces nothing but squealing and scraping. This will draw The Contortionist's twisting eye. It is best to let him see your failure in this case; otherwise, he summon additional Rollers as guards. He'll watch you struggle for a moment, then chuckle along his tangled limbs. He'll return to his alchemy.

While you can't free yourself, by twisting your elbow you can rub the armor again R the Killer's restraints.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 3
text:
Once the Contortionist finishes composing his Violet Jelly, he will start upon another brew. A metal-melting acid. While he pulls bottles from the shelf, his head spin's like an owl's. You sit back just in time. The Contortionist stares at R.

"Your fingers are lovely.

"Your beauty is... very unusual.

"Would you like to be a golem?

"No? I could do you right now? Don't rusting agent for you.

"But, best not to get my tasks tangled. The knight first."

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 4
text:
From a distant part of the caravan, you hear that spinning calliope of the bone carousel. Something is beginning.

A moment later, the mismatched voices of many Coffin Rollers strike up words to the song.

"On the winding roadway went a stomping, dancing show.

"All their feet upon the stones did wake the dead below.

"Grouching dead; they spit and cursed and clutched their grave goods tight.

"When Rollers roll across the road they do stir up a fright!

"In the coffins, beneath the Rollers.

"In the coffins, below.

"In the coffins, beneath the rollers,

"We find gemstones and good bones.

"Lovely, grouchy dead ones,

"Soon we'll have you clean.

"In the black coffins, beneath the Rollers,

"There's an army for our queen."

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Paragraph 5
text:
You'll have to work fast. If you don't, your lady will perish.

When The Contortionist spins his ancient head back to the alchemy table, twist your elbow back into position. Shift your shoulder forward and backward to rub your elbow over the strap binding R's left wrist to the chair. The leather will start fraying, and R will strain against his bond - until, with the sound of ripping muscle, the killer tears his hand free.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
**Eleven Strokes with The Old Man Below :**

With three plucks, R tugs free his three other binds. The Contortionist spins, screams, and charged. R rises, and grabs the tangled mass. He flings the roller like a snake. He fetches his Horned Star. Bunched limbs smack the hot vat. Wax spills, the freak burns. R closes in. Raises Star. Cudgels. Kills.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Paragraph 2
text:
R the Killer stops striking Contortionist as soon as the latter stop moving. By then, the former ball of long limbs is an indistinct mass of pulpy tissue; like a twine ball soaked in mud.

With the immediate enemy slain, R scans the room. From one of the hanging nails, he retrieves a sharpened cat skeleton. He uses this to cut your binding. You should retrieve your Steel and Pinecone Crest Shield before leaving.

Outside the wagon, you'll have to fight two rollers who were standing nearby. These are Whistle and Spur, and their kicks can break your bones, even through armor. R can take one of them. Focus on either Whistle or Spur. Block a few kicks with your shield. Eventually, your foe will shatter a toe bone. From there, your steels makes quick pieces of the enemy.

Don't spend long slaying these two. You can already hear the hooting of reinforcements. Kill fast, and run.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Paragraph 1
text:
\#

After breaking line of sight with the gawps, you and R scramble up the steps of a covered wagon. The flap slides shut behind you. The wagon appears to belong to the Rollers' hairdresser.  Various scissors and scalpels and razors lie on a long velvet table.

You hold your breath. You hear the rumble of the approaching gawps. Their footsteps rattle the blades on the table.

A moment later, their rabble fades.

You hear horses nickering on the wagon's other side. R suggests you take a pair and leave.

*You will NOT leave your Lady to these gawps. You won't leave her behind.*

*But can you fight them without this Killer?*

*Appeal to his vengeance. He has that temper. He'll want their master dead.*

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 1
text:
As you creep along the outer side of the wagons, R whispers.

"The Master of these gawps; what is he?

"My bet's an alienated homunculous.

"Hardy quarry.

"MY own lovely Star won't puncture incorporeals.

"It's better for killing freaks.

"Your sword's mundane too.

"Got any aces? Any-"

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 2
text:
R's whispers are cut short by a hiss like air through the nose of a bull. You both glance sharply back the way you've come.

A shadow cast by lantern light grows around the corner of a wagon.

R grabs your pauldron and pulls you into the underbrush beside the wagons. You try to step quietly, but the branches scrape against your armor.

As you huddle under the yellowing leaves of the foliage, the shadow slides into view. A moment later, you see the gawp. This one is a horrorshow. Half man, half gila-monster, half-a-ton heavy. It's footsteps shake the earth as it comes into view.

The gawp stops. It raises its head like a boulder on a crane. It points its slitted nose to the stars. It sniffs.

The monster hisses, then turns around and thunders away.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 3
text:
Though time is against you, you stop in the brush for a moment while waiting for the monster to gain some distance. You take stock with R of your magical gear.

**Sorrowful Father Mask:** Though it has served you on occasion before, you doubt that this magical disguise will grant any advantage against the Rollers.

**Silver Ring:** R carries this masterfully lambent ring. He assures you it won't help in this battle. He does not explain its qualities.

**Jugflower:** You find this pressed, pitcher-shaped flower blossom in a roll of leather in the bottom of your pack. You quickly rewrap it; when exposed to air, a plucked Jugflower instantly starts to disintegrate. As it disintegrates, a piercing ring fills the ears of anyone nearby, deafening them.

**Pure Bread:** You only have three crackers of this revitalizing bread. You were saving them for your lady. Now, you chew a piece, and grudgingly pass one to R. Any weariness in your arms, or soreness from your rough handling, disappears.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 4
text:
Don't bother returning to The Silk Maze or The Chemical Show. You won't find your lady in either of those wagons.

As you sneak along the caravan, keep your eye out for a wagon with a semitranslucent, glass-like cover. You'll know you're close when you see three wagons in a row loaded with buckets of varicolored lead paint.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Paragraph 5
text:
**Yellowrock Way:**

The flap of this wagon reveals an impossibly-long corridor with a glass roof. It stretches and shrinks into a distant pinhole.

The moment you step inside you know you are close. You hear those many-chorded inhalations and exhalations.

As you walk along the starlit span of yellow stone, the breathing swells in volume. It seems that you should see this master by now, for the way lies entirely open ahead.

Then, you stand before him.

**Patchwork:**

The black-clad, lank, pale figure before you seems more stitches than skin. His body is a network of different flesh. His eyes gleam with the madness of a thousand personalities.

The Strongman Roller stands before this master, striated arms crossed. The strangled-and-resurrected twins flank him. Your Lady - sleeping, hypnotized - sways behind.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
If you open your dried Jugflower and let it dissolve during the battle, you'll have a much easier time beating Patchwork. He attacks in choral screams.

If you don't use the flower or already spent its power, draw your Polished Steel Longsword, raise your Pinecone Crest shield, and brace yourself. You're in for a fight.

The fight begins with The Strongman's charge. Step sideways and let R draw the veiny man's wrath.

You yourself should turn your attention to the undead twins. Patchwork will immediately scream after The Strongman charges. The scream staggers you, and places the twins' bodies under his control. You'll have to block or dodge their biting teeth and fingernails. Don't them them both close in on you at once; they'll grapple you to the ground and pry your plates off with their deadly strangle. Try to focus one down at a time. Your Steel *will* wear away at their deathless flesh.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
With the second deaths of Bonnie and Butter, the battle enters its second stage.

Patchwork will leap away, hurtling a hundred paces down the endless hall.

You'll see the man with the stitched flesh of many people pull a two-foot-long ceramic smoke pipe from the front of his coat. He stuffs it with a bale of smoking weed, lights it, breaths in, and exhales.

That exhaled cloud does not spread into the endless hall. It immediately sinks into and around patchwork. It forms a veil. The smoke ripples, and in a moment you see not the disfigured man of skins, but a shiny, black, elfin, necromancer god. Patchwork appears as an idealization of The Thin Man himself.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 3
text:
Leave R to deal with the Strongman. Start running. You'll want to close as much distance while Patchwork transforms as possible.

Patchwork is a master of fume necromancy, in which he waves the pipe through long arcs. He'll chop through these streaks with the flat of his hand, creating distinct wedges of vapor.

Meanwhile, his many voice each release distinct staccato laughs. They form a disjointed symphony.

At each laugh, one of the waveblades of smoke soars at you. You can block them with your Pinecone Crest shield, but they will burn your arm through the wood. Don't let them strike your armor; you'll be cooked. It's better to cut them apart as they come, but you have to get the timing right.

When you finally reach Patchwork, cleave.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 4
text:
Patchwork's smoky veil evaporates. He suddenly sucks in all his breath. The air vanishes from the hall; you gasp and choke.

Patchwork swells; his stitches stretch and split.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 5
text:
This is the last stage, and the hardest. Patchwork's skin is like the leather of a stone in his swollen state. You can't cut or puncture any part of him. Your only advantage is in speed. Duck or sidestep his complacent punches. Don't try to parry them; a single grazing hit will shatter your skeleton.

For every three swing patchwork will kick out with his left foot. This attack is faster and hard to dodge. It's also your only chance of victory. You see one loose seam in the bottom of his soleless boot; smoke streams from it.

Wait for a few kicks to learn his timing. Then, with a perfect strike, plunge your sword into the bottom of patchwork's raised foot, and rip your Steel free.

name: 13. The Finale - Paragraph 6
text:
Patchwork screams in a thousand tongues. Then he falls. Writhing on the ground, his voices fade out one by one.

The hallway compresses. You find yourself beside R and the lady. The Strongman is dead. Your lady awake, and alive.

You have never fought so great a foe. You hope you never shall again.

*In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 1
text:
The endless Road of Graves rolls before you and your Blue Lady. An unwound cobblestone yarn. The epitaphs on each stone stand out with oddly sharp texture. Each name is bold and legible. Even the worn ones.

You blink.

Something snaps.

Within your chest a bright and painful flower takes root. Like dandelions of fire it spreads in dots through your body. A groan escapes.

You see your Lady touch your pauldron, but feel nothing through the fire under your skin. Every sense - sound, touch, taste, sight, smell - hurts. The air burns like snorted water down your throat. The stir of the wind scrapes like an infection in your ears.

Even your sense of time burns. You cannot count the seconds, yet each one seems like a doctor's hammer tapping directly on the tissue of your brain.

Another snap. Pain vanished.

\#

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 2
text:
**An Age Before The Road:**

The stones are new. The Road is not one of graves, but an ordinary road of mundane cobbles.

Five-thousand years ago, the demonic sorcerer kings burned and salted each others' kingdoms, ruling people that were half-demons. Terror and blood were the orders of the day.

\#

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 3
text:
*Is this hallucination? Self-hypnotism?*

The first demon-slave will attack alone. From the sinking sun at the end of the road it first widens and stretches0 into a black silhouette. You'll see it raise its face to the air, and sniff with a nose in the shape of a swine's.

Luckily you still have your Polished Steel Longsword. Draw it, parry the first blow from the demon-slave's crooked axe. Capitalize on your off-balance foe. Carve a red opening in the demon from shoulder to groin; like a pea pod.

You'll have to fend off multiple waves of such demons. They come in groups of up to four. Some come from the road, some from the wilds. With each one you slay the sun will sink lower and lower. The Road will shrink.

Somewhere, back in your own time, your life shrinks as well.

You'll have to slay twenty-seven demons in order to progress. They will keep coming after the twenty-seventh falls, so as soon as you hit that number, begin praying to the seven necromancer-gods for deliverance.

One will answer.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Paragraph 4
text:
A third snap. With this snap of your soul the squealing demon-slaves vanish. In their place you'll find a thrashed and flayed giant.

The Red King.

You'll now have to fight effigy of the deathless lord of vengeance. He stands ten feet tall, a scarred and naked man-thing, face contorting into such a scowl as could only by caste in wax or plaster. He wields a blood-polished sword.

In an instant the effigy will raise his sword, howl, crouched, and spring.

You have one chance. Time your cut. You will know if you succeed by the feel of your Steel biting into skin.

\#

The effigy vanishes in red mist. The sun sinks. The road grows dark. Your senses turn fully black and blind.

\#

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 1
text:
Your Blue Lady's voice begins softly, but echoes stronger with each word.

"Breath easier, my Cracked Cup Knight. Can you smell that? It is tea, I found mint and rose hips not far. Be easy. You are well. You will be well.

"Here, I will hold your hands.

"That is good, my knight. Move a little. You are lying again a large oak. I pulled you off The Road.

"I dared not remove your helmet, I know your vows. Do you feel that tree's rugged skin?"

name: Diablery Scene - Paragraph 1
text:
Letter from Lionel Pinnick to his wife, Adelaide "Addie" Pinnick.

April 5, 1911

My dear, everything appears to be arranged for you to join me here in St. Petersberg. After the crossing, and a gala carriage to Paris, you shall board the Orient Express, until it reaches Strassburg. A few connecting rails until Königsberg. Finally, by steamer to your waiting husband. You need not chafe your hands with the usual anxiety, for I have taken care of every detail, down to show of the fontaines lumineuses on the evening of the 8th. Were it within my power, I'd have placed a 3-day reservation of clear weather!

name: Diablery Scene - Paragraph 2
text: I daresay I am about to be green with the envy of your miniature grand tour. Having come by water all the way from London, I missed the the Alps, The Rhine, Feldberg and bristling Black Forest beard. Services are practically modern here in Petersberg. Where one of our familiar desserts, or some usual domestic service is unavailable, the adaptation has been an easy one. Certainly this is not Afrika. I myself am thoroughly healthy now within our own embassy. Among both foreign and domestic ministers, I expect that we islanders are alone in that respect. If health is the harmonious workings of all organs, the Russian giant is sick indeed. The most alarming symptom is the gulf between the rural folk and- But I must not digress here into an essay on the shortcoming of the state. Doubtless you shall have your share when you arrive.

name: Diablery Scene - Paragraph 3
text:
Has Penny settled down since I left? You must allow the girl her passions. Let her have that blue velvet dress she asked after, or attend that reception at count So-and-So's while you are in Munich. Time enough will she have for spartan living here; a little freedom on the journey can do no lasting harm.

But I must close this letter - Sazunov and Buchanan will hardly forgive my lateness in the first week of service. Wishing you a safe and quick journey.

Your husband,

Lionel

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 10, 1911

What runs on an even number of wheels, on an odd number stands perfectly still, and walks befuddled upon two legs in a crisis?

I think it must be the German rail industry.

Our train had put three hours of distanced between itself and Strasbourg when Penny and I felt a terrific shudder through our seats. It shook one of our cases down from overhead and burst it upon the floor (fortunate that neither she nor I were in it's path, though her own cranium is possibly thick enough to have withstood the impact). Thereafter the train screeched and trembled to a full stop. Stopped it has remained, and although the quilled pines on the hills are a stupendous aspect on our right, one would prefer that they were yet sliding gradually out of vision, hill-after-hill disappearing behind. As it is, the distant forest moves no more than if it were so much green paint over the train window.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 2
text: Penny is delighted by the accident. She has already introduced herself to Duc d'Aoste in the adjacent compartment, and every other body in our car, no doubt. Now she has suggested we have our luggage ported to one of villages nearby, and go by carriage. I said we certainly would not, which has ignited in her a deep sulk. I see those beautiful blue eyes sulking busily out of the window even as write, under those rich brown tresses. There, she has caught me staring, and it has only upset her the more. Thank god she received her father's looks, though I wish she had not cut her hair - my friend Lady Urry was quite right when she described it as being, "Rather short." God grant me patience with this girl. Where she gets her inquisitive nature from, I do not know. I was never half-so-wily, and Lionel has the cunning of a yak.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 3
text: It has been almost a month since I last saw Lionel. I must admit, I'm still uncertain of his appointment. Certainly Lionel has all the necessary qualification of one of his majesty's foreign ministers, and he is gregarious enough. I have that the Russian court is nothing but plots and poisons, however, and Lionel can hardly keep a straight face. Oh how I wish that he were here now. His German is so much better than my own. He has always been better with people; anyway, I'm certain these rail workers would attend him as a military man, not to mention his position. They certain did not give much thought when I offered to get behind and push. (And I suppose I cannot blame them there). Enough. What good can come of tears? Lionel is not here now, and I must be patient.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 4
text: So, now I am patiently sitting in our compartment. I am patiently looking at those still, high hills. The sky is clear now, but I think it is going to rain. I am most certain not keeping an eye out for the engineer at his work, and definitely not listening to the tick, tick, tick of the clock above the door.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 5
text: Post Script: It appears Penny's wish shall be at least partially fulfilled. The conductor has just told us that it will be at least twenty-fours hours before the necessary replacement parts will arrive. We must, it seems, rely on local hospitality for one night at least.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 1
text:
Letter from Senior German Rail Engineer Victor Grob, to the station at Karlsruhe.

April 10, 1911

Dear brothers of the German rail industry, this humble Kaiser's servants asks that you send a motorcar with new coupling rod and boiler down Baden line. (See the order which accompanies this note). He begs your haste on behalf of his passengers.

One busy harpy is especially bad. Not a bad frau to *look at* - an unusual beauty. Thin as the last wheat stalk standing in a November field. Too much white of her eyes showing, too large a forehead, flat blond-grey hair. One of those "high-collar, long black sleeve, British" types. Her whole affect is like something at once delicate and sharp; like a knife made of glass. And, brothers, the voice is matched to cutting.

Anyway. Recommended her to The Green Man in Sankt Sechs. I have been there myself, it is a good inn. They serve good tongue. (Which is a fine thing, when it isn't a woman's; ha, ha, ha!)

\-Sr. Engineer Grob

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 10, 1911 (later that afternoon)

Fate deals a fair hand it seems - though never does she issue the good and the bad all at once, and rarely do the cards seem to come in the order which is easiest upon the heart.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 2
text:
Penny and I rode in the back of a lumberer's wagon from our stopped train to the nearest village, which is called St. Six, an odd name. The ride was no more than three miles, but though our driver seemed to know the tracts well, the uneven ground was perfectly suited to shake my teeth within their sockets. Our suitcases fell from the wagon more than once, and I am afraid that one of Penny's best evening gown is ruined with mud. She does not seem to mind. I am thankful at least that it was the middle of the day as we came through the woods. Between the boughs was shaded even at that high hour (no wonder thou art called Black, Forest!). I should not wish to make such a journey after dark.

Coming into St. Six a little after noon, we found the town to be perhaps a few hundred people. They are mostly mining and lumber families I would guess. At the dubious recommendation of one Engineer Grob, we stopped at the door of a public house; the sign out front had written in German "The Green Man" above a caricature of some goblin or other. We stepped in, and my apprehension told me at once that this would be no comfortable accommodation. The common room was packed with German men. No doubt they were celebrating some festival or other, the room was a a babel of voices, with at least one trio or quartet lending their baritones to the words of a volkslieder. As Penny and I pressed our way toward the keeper of the Green Man - a perfectly clean, swarthy man himself, albeit with a great dishevel of fuzz over his lips - we could hear some distant *boom, boom, boom* sound, which must have been some mining or lumber machinery.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 3
text:
I must have looked like some grim-faced Valkyrie, bracing for an unpleasant evening, jaw clenched. I had Penny's wrist in my hand; I let her go only when she informed me that I was crushing her bones.

It was at that moment, however, when he stumbled into the only representative of our sex inside the place, a stout woman of about my own age. I tried to apologize, but she would hear nothing of it. She introduced herself as Frau Helena Kessel. Between a smatter of German, French, and English on both sides we could communicate tolerably well (competing vocal crowd withstanding). Frau Kessel told me a little about the town; that all in St. Six got on well with their neighbors; that their chief industries were coal and timber. She said that she was a teacher who boarded at the only schoolhouse in the town, but that now closed. She must have sensed my discomfort, for she eventually offered to lodge Penny and I at her schoolhouse for our stay. My senses were so scattered by the presence of the crowd that I think the show of refusal I made was a poor one. I was in any event convinced.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 4
text: So here I write now, inside a screen-enclosed porch, at the old schoolhouse on the edge of town. We have just set our luggage down. It is a two-story affair, with a specious dining hall serving as the moon schooling room. That *boom, boom, boom* of idustry is audible still, but distant. I do not like how close the house sits to those black and quiet hills. I have heard that child-eating wolves haunt the fabled German wood - doubtless it is the stuff of fairy tales.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 10, 1911 (evening)

I have taken a page out Penny's book, and spent the remainder of the day alongside my daughter exploring what there was to explore in the village. Frau Kessel performed the generous service of a tour guide. (That is always the way in the small holdings of France and Germany, I think; find any goodly, older Samaritan who is happy in their home, and they will be only to happy to show you its every heathered field and trickling creek).

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 2
text: One wide dirt road curves in a downward arc from the northeastern corner of the village to its southeastern limit, and it is along this arc that most of the homes are situated. A river skirts the southern and eastern sides of the town. The schoolhouse is at the southeast end. The nearest building to it is an archaic river-powered sawmill which has not been in operation for over a year, the workers having all switched to a steam mill on the north side of town. A few pasturages for goats and sheep fill out the spaces between the red brick homes. Curiously, there is a small, polygonal, Napoleonic era fort, just a hundred paces beyond the east side of town. It is a pale grey stone, with five ruined towers. I am told that older generations kept it as a museum of relics from the Napoleonic wars. The forest has since reclaimed most of it, and only the tumbling tops of the towers are visible now through the creeping woods. There is also a post office. I have already spoken with the clerk and sent a message off to Lionel.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 3
text:
After our tour we returned and dined with Frau Kessel in the schoolhouse. She produced a stew of potatoes, flour, pork, and some seasonings in a huge black pot over the kitchen fireplace. I found it sufficient, though I daresay Penny was less than impressed with the spartan fayre. Her excitement at this unplanned excursion has worn away on seeing that this is just a small rural village with not a lot to do.

I am not as easy about our 'fortunate' accommodations as when Frau Kessel first approached us. For one thing, our hostess seems to be a teller of tall tales. I asked her about the woods behind the house over supper, whereupon she told me that the most dangerous thing in the forest was a great satin panther. "A panther?" I asked. "In the Black Forest?" Whereupon our hostess made answer, "He's a stranger here, like yourselves." Her story did alleviate my silly concerns, which Frau  Kessel noticed and laughed at. She pointed to a rifle above the fireplace, which she said would do for any wicked thing should it trouble us during our stay. (I hope she does not intend for me to handle the firearm in such a circumstance. Lionel has shown me how to use one of course, but am hardly a capable marksman under the coolest circumstances.)

Another thing which gives me pause is the... atmosphere of Frau Kessel's schoolhouse. On the sill of a wide bay window beside the foyer, there is a crow perched which is *tap, tap, tapping* the glass. In the schoolroom, I see that there is a dustless space on an otherwise dusty wall, in the shape of a cross. Frau Kessel has told me that the cross fell and broke, and that she has sent to a carpenter for a new one. More than this, there is a certain 'feel' to Frau Kessel's home. I do not know how to put it into words - it is like that texture in the air one notices in a basement that has previously flooded and mildewed, like the place is clean and dry now, but was once covered in slime. Probably it is nerves and paranoia - the frau herself has been nothing but kind. Still, I shall be glad to leave St. Six tomorrow morning.

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 1
text:
Telegram from Lionel Pinnick

April 10, 1911

Addie, eard of breakdown, and that all are safe. Jelous of your opportunity; Rural Fatherland Living. Bring Webele and some morello cheries here with you. Don't take part in too much diablery.

Please respond when able.

Your husband,

Lionel

name: Scene 1 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 11, 1911

Today has been a very generous one. Generous how? Well, it has given me the opportunity to practice that constant appeal of my dear Lionel: "Have Patience."

name: Scene 1 - Paragraph 2
text:
Ill dreams of the woods beyond the window disturbed much of my sleep. I awoke with my skin hot with irritation. How quickly that heat cooled to goosepimples when I found that Penny had gone. I called out, first to my daughter, then to Frau Kettel, and received no answer from either. Why Penny's absence should have bothered me so is unaccountable; she always rises before me when we are at home. Nevertheless I began a frantic search of the schoolhouse. In the basement I found a varnish of dust covering old chairs, a wood stove, a broken loom, crockery - baubles and preserved jams and dried herbs galore, but no beloved offspring. Around the back I found a coop and a nearby chopping table. Two cocks had been tied about their feet, and hung from pegs in the roof to let them drain. The air was bitter for April in that German mountain town, and their blood had frozen over the grass. I turned to the forest. I think my brains must have been really scattered, for another unaccountable impulse came over me to rush into the hills. Why should I find Penny out in the woods? And attired in only my nightdress.

Well, I had already taken the first steps toward those trunks when I heard a cry from the front of the house: "Mother? Are you awake?"

name: Scene 1 - Paragraph 3
text: I finally took ahold of some sense. Before I went to see Penny, I ran back to the room we were given and changed into proper attire. When I finally went to see the girl, I found her cutting a black rye loaf in the kitchen, while Frau Kettel was cracking eggs by the fire. They had been to town to fetch some eggs for breakfast from The Green Man. I told Addie that I had been worried when I woke and found that she was gone. She told me they had only left a little while ago I did my best to "have patience", and didn't press the issue. I asked if there was any news, but Penny says she and Frau Kettel could hardly understand one another (she has neglected her German I'm afraid), and that they only shared a few words of local customs and traditions. It is odd: I saw a glance and a smile from Frau Kettel as my daughter said this. I get the impression that the frau understands more of our English than she lets on. I am afraid to ask Addie not to talk so much with the woman, however, since it will only encourage the girl.

name: Scene 1 - Paragraph 4
text:
I followed up the morning excitement by making myself rather busy. I went with Penny back into the village. I asked for news at the post office myself, but the worker had nothing to share. I was able to convince one of men outside to drive us back in a wagon to the broken down train. When we got there (after a ride just as unpleasant as the one into town) I found the chief engineer and asked him when we expected to be rolling again. He pretended to misunderstand me for some while, but eventually admitted that the parts had not even arrived yet. How many hours shall we have to wait? He did not know. Some of the other passengers are making little campsites in and around the cars. Others have ordered carriages and wagons back to Baden, or forward onto Karlsruhe. Only a few are staying in town.

"Have patience." "Have patience." "Have patience..."

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 1
text:
Excerpt from the Karlsruhe Sanntagpost

April 11, 1911

Sankt Sechs. Police continue to investigate damage to livestock in and around the Buhl area.  A report comes in today from Herr Mathias Wahrmann, a coal miner who keeps a small pasture and twenty head of goat in Sankt Sechs. Herr Wahrmann reported that, on going out to feed his flock on the morning of the 11th, several did not come to his call. Walking out to where the pasture lies near the woods at the back of his property, he discovered that a section of the fence boards had been broken inward. Around the breach lay the bodies of three of Herr Wahrmann's goats. Their stomachs had been torn open, and their innards and the corn which they had been digesting lay in a mess across the field. Police have been to investigate the scene. One officer of experience has suggested that the injuries look as if they were inflicted by the claws of a great cat. There are no great cats native to the area. This report matches three similar incidents in the nearby town of Oos, Steinbach, and Baden. A general warning has been issued. Herr Wahrmann reports that his favorite animal, an old buck named Wolfgang, is still missing.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 11, 1911 (Evening)

Today has passed all in a single rush, only one start and only one stop. I feel as if I have not rested since we left England.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 2
text:
After making myself a bit of a nuisance with the engineer for a spell, Penny and I returned by the rattling wagon way to the village. We made an effort to distract ourselves. The local man who drove the wagon offered to take us out to the coal facilities north of town. The ride was only a mile, on blessedly smooth road. As we drew closer the constant *Boom, Boom, Boom* of machinery rang louder and louder. It seemed to shake the branches of the trees. We rounded a bend in the woods and came rather abruptly upon the scene. A tall and craggy hillside had been scraped clean of forest, with only a few old stumps making a stumble of face. In that face, two surprisingly tiny holes descended into perfect darkness. I cannot believe that men would crawl into such a place for the purest, brightest gold, never mind ordinary coal. A man stood near one of the entrances. A matte onyx dust covered every inch of both the man and the surrounding - we did not step down from the wagon. The source of the booming sound - so loud that I feel it still vibrating in my knuckles, undiluted then by screen of the woods - was a great silo, some kind of refinery. Steam belched continually from a huge rusted pipe which ran up the side of this silo. Some lever or piston must have been continuously hammering within the silo, for at each boom the whole steel case seemed to shudder.

The man who had been standing by the caves noticed us. I told him: "We came to see your mine."

"Yes," he replied.

I asked him, "Can you tell us what that building is doing? Why does it make such sound?"

He answered, "When the coal is drawn out of the ground, it must be crushed and washed before it burns in a stove and engine."

I believe they have the crushing part of coal refinery down to a science - it is the washing that could do with some innovation.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 3
text:
After, we returned to The Green Man. The landlord, Herr Boeckmann, I found to be very pleasant company. There were fewer patrons today, and Herr Boeckmann and Frau Boeckmann made for Penny and I an excellent lunch of some local style of dumpling, filled with minced lamb, onion, and spinach. I asked the Frau for the recipe - I am sure Lionel will like them. I also asked Herr Boeckmann about lodging at the inn, just in the event we have to stay another evening in St. Six.

"I am very sorry madam," he said. "There is only one room I have, which is above my son's house down the road. All the upstairs is full."

I said, "Is your son uncomfortable around others, Herr?"

"Uncomfortable? Ha ha! But he is working in chemical in Heidelberg. The house will be empty, lady, and you and your daughter will find it very dull."

And so our lodging has been arranged quite satisfactorily. As Penny and I were leaving The Green Man we met several of the train engineers who were entering the establishment. I took the opportunity to ask again about the state of repairs. I am afraid that my temper ran away.

"Then how long shall your passengers wait upon your fancy?" I finally shouted.\
"The engine just will not go without working parts, woman," the lead engineer, Herr Grob, shouted back, before stepping inside.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 4
text:
Now I write from the same screened schoolhouse porch. We have returned just to collect our luggage. Penny is packing now, a little sulkily. It seems we shall stay one day more in St. Six.

Post script: Will that malignant crow never cease tapping?!

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 13, 1911

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 2
text: Stuck like a mouse in a pot. Penny and I are both stuck still in this little village. The replacement parts for our train have not even arrived. I can only imagine that this is costing the rail company a tidy sum. I am too humiliated by my outburst at the chief engineer to ask him when he thinks the train will be fixed. He does not know, I am sure, and even the train engineers look a little sullen as they sit outside of The Green Man.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 3
text:
No, no more arguments with the German tradesmen for this English woman: she has decided that her is a better opponent to spar with. Penny and I have had another one of our falling outs today. I have tried to be patient with her, I know that she has not much to do in this village. But she has been rising early, and going about on her own anyway. Spending her time with Frau Helena Kettel. What does that "schoolteacher" do, with no school to run? And what can a sixteen-year-old girl and middle-aged woman who hardly share a common language have in common? I asked Penny to stay in my sight until we had left the village from now on.

"Mother," she answered, "please don't be such a dry stick."

She knows how I dislike to be called that. "My dear, we shall be leaving soon. You must simply-"

She interrupted me, "Why is it that whenever we travel, you stiffen against anyone or anything exciting?"

"I do not stiffen!" I said, and so walked down from any higher ground. Thus was battle joined. Finally I told her that she was to stay away from Frau Kettel. Penny cried and she left the room. I hate her tears, but she only cries if she means to do as I say. My mind is easy at least that she will not go again to the schoolteacher.

I should have hired a wagon or carriage to take us onto Karlsruhe yesterday; I will do so this afternoon.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 1
text:
Telegram from Lionel Pinnick

April 13, 1911

Addie, have not heard news since train stopped. Is everything okay? Please send news.

Your husband,

Lionel

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 13, 1911 (Morning)

We are making ready to depart this afternoon. Yesterday I made arrangements with the Herr Waldow who so generously carted us to and from the train. (Incidentally, I think he could make himself into a proper gentleman - and far less intimidating on first acquaintance - if he fixed the loose threads about his shirt, and shaved that hair off his upper lip; but perhaps this is the German peasant custom.) The Herr has agreed to drive us all the way to Karlsruhe after lunch. We should arrive before nightfall. I feel simply awful for having not posted a word in reply to Lionel's original telegram. I will send him word the moment we reach the station in Karlsruhe.

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 3
text: Penny returned almost after sundown the previous evening. (Something sinister is in the pink sun touching down behind a rugged land of black trees). I told her of the arrangements I made with Herr Waldow, and she nodded. She has been quiet since, and I think it best to let what I said to her sink in. She is a bright thinker, and I am sure she will understand. Still, this silence between us hurts my heart. I love her.

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 4
text:
This morning I woke to find Penny gone again. I walked over to The Green Man again. There I found only Frau Boeckmann, the landlords wife. I asked her what she knew about Frau Kettel.

"She has been schoolmistress here for seven years," said Frau Boeckmann.

I said, "She must have been a good teacher indeed. She has taught all the students so well that there are none left for her to teach."

The landlady laughed. "Just so! They go to their farms and their father's trades in April."

"The Frau is very kind with lost strangers."

"They all are."

"Who is all?" I asked.

"All those schoolmistresses. The Frau Kettel is a part of a-"

I did not understand the word she used. "Coterie?" I asked.

"Yes. They are all schoolteachers. They eat here sometimes, or she goes to Buhl or Rastatt. Wherever they are from. Mostly they are older than Frau Kettel, but some are still young."

I later asked her where I could find a carpenter in town. The good woman pointed me to a single-story home on the opposite side of the road. He was a large and surly man, not one given to idle conversation. His answer was plain enough however when I asked this carpenter, Herr Karloff, whether he had been commissioned to make a new cross for the schoolhouse. Even did I not comprehend a word of German, still the cant of his reply would have made the answer clear.

"Nein."

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 5
text:
...

Penny just returned to our rooms at the house of the of the innkeeper's son. "Were you with that schoolteacher?" I asked her.

She looked at me, but she looked more surprised than angry. "No, mother," she said. "I have done just as you said."

There was something surprising in her meek reply. "Has she tried to see you?"

"She- she invited me to a Sabbath."

"A Sabbath? We will be far from here by Sunday."

She shook her head. "It's a certain kind of... black magic ritual."

My daughter will remain in my sight until we have arrived in St. Petersburg. I cannot wait to leave this village.

name: Diablery Scene - Paragraph 1
text:
Diary of Addie Pinnick

April 13, 1911 (Afternoon)

My poor darling girl - what is happening to her?

Penny was staring out of the window at the back of our room. It faces that old and ruined fort, screened by the hills and woods. I was seated in a chair in the corner reading. She had not spoken since I told her not to leave my sight, she seemed dazed. All at once I heard a *thump*, and looked up to see my sunshine lying on the floor. She had fainted. She was stirring and sitting up. I sprang from the chair at once and helped her up. I brought her over to the bed. I asked her what was wrong, and she told me that she had felt a pressure, a kind of pounding in her head. She thought it might be that *boom, boom, boom* of the coal machinery. I told her to lie down and sleep, and she was willing. She is sleeping now and breathing more easily. I don't think this any show; her cheeks are bright prink, and her fingers are too cold under the blankets.

name: Diablery Scene - Paragraph 2
text:
There is a druggist in the village. I would not leave my daughter's side, even for that, but I must also speak with Herr Waldow and ask that he brings his wagon over to the house. He must be ready and waiting for us now. Our bags are packed. I will run over to the druggist and purchase some aspirin or tonic for Penny. Then quickly over to Herr Waldow, and I will ride straight back here with him. Then we will leave this town. My daughter is sleeping soundly, and I refuse to be gone longer than five minutes.

Please God, let my happiness in life be kept safe.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 1
text:
Letter from Leopold Boeckmann to his son in Heidelberg

April 13, 1911

My son, I hope that all is well with you. You are doing a good thing. The gendarmerie are very good. You are serving the duke well.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 2
text: The chickens are all very healthy, except that three are dead this Spring. I think it is the damp. They lay more eggs than I need. I sell a dozen to the grocer from Rastatt, and still I have too many eggs. I feed the extras to the cats. The Old Mum had six Kittens at the end of march, and they were very soft, but we have too many cats already, and so your mother has put them in a sack and thrown them in the river, and the Old Mum is now quite sad. I myself am also quite sad, but your mother is of course very right to do this.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 3
text: My son, I write to ask for your help with an Englishwoman. Her name is Addie, and she has been staying in the unused quarters of your house. I know you will not mind. Anyway, she came over to my inn today in tears and told me that her daughter is nowhere to be found. She was very upset. I myself went over to the house and could not find the daughter, who is about your age except ten years younger. She asked me where were the police in our duchy, and I knew she meant the gendarmerie, and so you see now why I am writing.

name: Scene 2 - Paragraph 4
text:
My son, I am sure you will know who is right to talk to. I wish for you to help this Englishwoman. She is a bossy but nice lady.

Please forgive your father's bad letters.

\-Leopold Boeckmann

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 1
text:
Note from Addie Pinnick

April 13, 1911

My name is Adelaide Pinnick. I'm writing this note at one of the desks in the classroom of the schoolhouse at Halbzwölf. The hour is past six - the sun is nearly gone.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 2
text: I arrived in this village with my daughter, Penny Pinnick, two days ago, on the 11th of April,. Our train had broken down. After stopping to stay in town, we were offered lodging by the mistress of this school, Frau Helena Kettel. Her manner stuck out to me as malicious, and we stayed with her only one night before finding rooms from the landlord of The Green Man. She has watched my daughter too closely since that time.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 3
text: Today, when I stepped away from our quarters for a moment to arrange our departure from the village, my daughter disappeared. I know that Frau Kettel has taken her. I immediately tried to get in touch with nearest German police. I believe the landlord of The Green Man, Herr Boeckmann, means to do that. But there are none in town now, and my daughter is in danger. I know that the Frau took her. I feel it. There is an old Napoleonic fort near the town - I can see its ruined pale turrets through the trees, like a white hill among the black woods. Somehow, I feel it as well that she has taken my daughter there. I think she means to induct my Penny into some ungodly cult.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 4
text: I have retrieved a rifle which the Frau kept over the schoolhouse kitchen fireplace. In the basement I found ammunition, as well as a set of boots and an acetylene lamp. I have stolen all three. I am going to go into the woods, over to that fort, and get my daughter back. The witch will not have her.

name: Scene 3 - Paragraph 5
text:
I leave this note behind in the event that I do not return, so that it may be found by any authorities who come in search of us. I accept all responsibility for the theft of Frau Kettel's property and- and for anything else. Neither the British empire, nor my daughter, bear any responsibility.

Signed,

\- Adelaide Pinnick

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 1
text:
Excerpt from a Witch's Sabbath.

Date unknown.

Author unknown.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 2
text:
...Having made assembly in some deserted place,

Anointed now in the living paints,

Naked, thy soul stripped of its cover,

Step Forth! ye daughter, ye slaves.

Come, thou who is to mock the soutane,

Come between the twain bonfires,

Lead the bleating, horned offering,

That no shadow shall conceal it from HIM.

Ye slaves that are witness, give voice to thy lamentations.

Come, ye slave child that is the ewer;

Ye slave mother that is the life;

Wash thy skins in yellow,

Sit ye each by HIS flames.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 3
text:
And now ye slaves that are witness,

Make thy spring rounds.

Dance, Sing, Wail!

In thy throats make music - in thy flesh, art - for HIM.

HE cometh upon Walpurgisnacht,

Under moon and star.

Heap high upon thy bonfires the fir cones,

Let the air crack and sparkle,

Crack and sparkle,

As thou deeply breathe the burning seed.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 4
text:
Slaves, hast thou demeaned thy woman's flesh?

Behold! HE approacheth.

Master of Shapes is HE.

The Beast is HE.

Great is HE.

HE cometh unto thy ritual in Shape of the Great Beast.

Step, ye false soutane, from thy bleating, horning offering,

Let HIM have thy offering before the flames.

Exult, all ye slaves, in this blood that has sated HIS lips,

As, running from the veins, it polluteth the loam.

name: Scene 4 - Paragraph 5
text:
The hour is nigh.

Let thee, slave mother that is the life,

Contorteth thy limbs into Melchizedek's seal,

For thou art the twisted slave of HIM.

From thy lips in the center of the seal,

Let thy old spirit cometh forth;

Let thee, slave child that is the ewer,

Take this old spirit into thy new flesh.

...

Slaves! Scream as is written:

MANY: "Hail!... Hail!... Hail!... Hail!... Hail!... Hail!... Hail!... Hail!..."

ONE: "Hail!...."

MANY: "Hail!..."

ONE: "Hail!...."

ONE: "Hail!...."

WHISPERS: "Hail..."

ONE: "Hail...."

WHISPERS: "Hail... Hail... Hail...... Hail.......... Hail......."

....

MANY-VOICES-MOVE-AS-ONE:

"LU-"

"CI-"

"FER."

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 1
text:
Report of Wachtmeister Lutz Schiller, Grand Ducal Baden Gendarmerie.

April 16, 1911.

Having received word from one Victor Grob, Rail Engineer for the Baden Rail Industry, regarding the disappearance of two passengers from his train, I arrived with three of my gendarmes in the nearby village of Halbzwölf, it being the place where they were last sighted. The missing passengers are two English women, a mother and daughter.

Upon arriving at the village we were directed to contact one, Herr Leopold Boeckmann, landlord of The Green Man, a local boarding house and inn. Following up on information he rendered, we next went to the local house and school of a Frau Helena Kettel. There, we found a letter from the missing mother, a copy of which is enclosed herewith. The letter makes apparent why our next destination was an old Napoleonic fort near town. Herr Boeckmann agreed to accompany me and my three gendarmes into the ruin.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 2
text: We arrived through the woods to the faded wall of the fort. Circling the perimeter, we found one of the outer doors propped open. Tracks showed signs of passage to and from, both animal and human. We followed the trail inside. The halls were overgrown with brush, and dark, but we carried lamps. We soon came across a most gruesome scene. A large, black hunting cat lay on its side in the middle of the hall. We approached cautiously, but found it to be in a state of rigor-mortis. The area around it was spattered with blood - it must have thrashed for some minutes. We inspected the body, and agreed that it had died of a large gunshot wound, just between its neck and left shoulder. As the panther is not native to The Black Forest, possibly this was an animal imported and kept in some private zoo, which had escaped. Inquiries are being made.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 3
text: The human tracks - booted and barefoot - went on in the dust past the animal. We followed them. Presently we entered a large circular room, which must have sat near the center of the fort. The ceiling had collapsed in long ago; brickwork lay across a floor of mud and short weeds, and one large pine tree had sprouted up through the opening. We found the ashes from two large fires on either side of the room. There were many footprints about the space, captured in the packed mud and pine needles.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 4
text: Two dead woman lay near to one of the ash piles. One woman was approximately sixty years of age. She was naked, except for green and blue paint along her arms and face. She had suffered a gunshot to her stomach, which is the likely cause of death. Herr Boeckmann, our local man, could not identify the woman. The other body was younger, perhaps forty. She had been shot in the face, just between her left eye and nose. She was naked as well, painted the same. Herr Boeckmann was able to identify her as Frau Helena Kettel, the local school teacher. The shell casing found at the scene match three we found by the panther, likely a Mauser.

name: Scene 5 - Paragraph 5
text: A trail of blood and footprints led through a another door into this room. We followed this trail through the fort, and out of another propped door at the back. The trail led into the hills. Beneath the boughs of the trees we found another dead woman. Also naked and painted, also about sixty. She appears to have died from blunt trauma, possibly beaten with the stock of a rifle. Footprints, at least one booted set, lead further into the woods. The trail goes cold in the trees, where the soil is more dry. Dogs have been sent for, and inquiries have been dispatched to the nearby communities to identify the deceased. The missing English women are, at present, assumed to be alive. New information will be reported as it is brought to light.

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 1
text:
Letter from Lionel Pinnick, British Minister in St. Petersburg, to his friend.

June 24th, 1911.

My Dear ---,

I am grateful for the kind and hopeful wishes you expressed in your last letter to me. How I wish that I could respond positively to your sentiments. Unfortunately, I have no happy news. Nearly two months now have passed since the disappearance of Addie and Penny, and no new word has come through. I begin to lose hope- \*\*\*a few words have here been scribbled out by Lionel\*\*\*

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 2
text: Ambassador Buchanan has been most generous with me over the course of these past months. I feel as if I have hardly been a fit representative of our Empire in Russia. Even when I was yet present in St. Petersburg, my mind could not dwell upon the affairs of state. I have drawn up my resignation and sent it by post to the Ambassador, along with my own recommendation for replacements. I doubt the latter will have much sway, given my abrupt tenure.

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 3
text: As with Sir Buchanan, the German gendarmerie have been, by and large, helpful and accommodating. I have been in this village of Halbzwölf for three weeks have. I have searched and searched and searched - praying, beseeching God that some missed clue or hidden trail might lead me back to my wife and my daughter. I have read and reread the letter Addie left, and the gendarmes have even allowed me to search through the old schoolhouse, and the fort in the hills. I have hectored Wachtmeister Lutz Schiller, their officer in charge of the investigation, with ten thousand questions and ravings. He has answered all in a placid and honest manner. He would not allow me see the bodies of the women who were slain - they being already buried. For this, I cannot reproach him; I asked when I was yet desperately full of hope.

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 4
text:
I am sitting now in a single wooden chair, outside the exact same house of Herr Boeckmann's where my Addie and my Penny last slept. I can hear the distant *boom, boom, booming* of the coalmine machinery. I am looking out upon the hills where they dissapeared. The tops of the trees are green, but underneath - my friend, this truly is a Black Wood.

I shall return to England soon.

\-Lionel

name: Scene 6 - Paragraph 5
text: Post Script: Have just received word: from Ludwigsburg, 40 miles east. Addie still missing - but, praise God, Penny is alive! My daughter. She is with town doctor now. I am leaving to see her at once. She is very weak. Malnourished, and doctor says she is suffering from amnesia.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 2
text:
Like fingers gone numb, flexing and unflexing, prickling as the blood creeps back under the pale skin - in this way do your senses return.

**Touch:** You feel the bark of the tree, your spine scraping against the trunk.

**Sound:** Your lady fallen quiet; but in the succeeding silence is a sound you know. *Caw. Cawcaw.* A crow. Between each of it's croaks, somewhere in the boughs, you hear the subtle hiss of light rain.

**Taste and Smell:** Breathing deely, air swells your lungs. The taste and smell of the rain light up another part of your lost sense.

**Sight:** First, you see nothing. Then light, and color, and a vague rectangle of shape. Then, your Blue Lady. Her face is bent over yours, looking down at your eyes, you knew, behind the shadow of your visor. Around her neck she wears a silver necklace.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 3
text:
*The Necklace. Dulled as you are, yet still you understand her intention.* *Will you allow this? You must not. No matter that it is painful, shake your head.*

*Do not let her throw herself into that necromancer's debt once more.*

*Stand up. Stand.*

*It is no use. Your arms do not feel that wet grass. See how they fall limp at your sides.*

*To your Lady's left, to her right; the epitaphs on the Road of Graves. They blur in your eyes again.*

*Focus. Try harder. Was your will meant only for when you are at your best?*

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 4
text:
You still see enough to catch your Lady's smile.

"I see your eye's direction. I have touched the stone of Far Speaking that is in this necklace.

"I will call Othelmedir. The necromancer will know what afflicts you.

"My Cracked Cup Knight, always you are enduring. But rest now. I will see that you are made alright. The sun is still high, and few are travelers on this span of the road, where wend the hills west of Middlemoss. Rest.

"Othelmedir will know how to make you right."

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Paragraph 5
text:
The black tunnel sinks in around your vision. A shake of your head only sets it pounding.. Your neck and arms loll.

The smell of the rain vanishes like the raincloud that has spent its fury.

*Caw... Caw.......* The croaking becomes muffled, distant. Your Blue Lady speaks, but the words are garbled, as if his helmet were stuffed with wool.

The last sense you know is the warmth of your Lady's hands around your own.

\#

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 1
text:
No time for thought, you are in combat.

Immediately bring your sword sideways to parry a strike at your sternum.

Sword. Not familiar Steel.

You'll find yourself familiar with the weight. Good thing too, as your foe cuts with an overhead chop. Spring back and raise your sword to block. The tip of the blade will ring like a small silver bell.

By now you'll probably notice you have no armor, no helmet. You must rely on your quick maneuvering limbs - pliant like young saplings not yet hardened into trees - to stave the second, and the third, and the fifth and sixth blows from this foe that wants to kill.

This is a memory.

A memory of home.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 2
text:
A memory of your old weaponmaster.

Though it is only a memory, the bristling grey mustache and goatee - like three straw brooms covered in ashes - seem real.

So does the fight for your life seem real. So does that blow which would have gouged his scalp. *Crack. Crackcrack.* The sharp retorts of steel on steel ring through the courtyard.

If you glance right and left, you'll see the yellow, stucco walls of your Lady's keep. It leans like an outgrown tree, over the edge of a plateau. In the distance, The Lake of Tomatoes shines hot and red under the slate-colored sun.

*Thud.* 

No matter what you do, the weaponmaster's boot will catch your sternum. you fall. Your sword flies away. The weaponmaster tenses to finish the kill.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 3
text:
"Stop."

A voice like a gentle thunderclap rolls.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Paragraph 4
text:
Your Old Lord is there.

Your Lady is beside him.

She will catch your eye, and smile.

He will nod.

Then your old weaponmaster will tuck his sword away, bend, and pull you to your feet.

\#

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 1
text:
Another sudden conflict. You'll find yourself mid-swing. A reversal on a swing that had knocked his opponents guard wide.

To win, simply bring your fresh Polished Steel Longsword out in a diagonal upward arc.  An unblockable cut.

Your Steel opens a canyon through his enemy's tunic, through the torso. A river of blood and intestine spills onto the polished blue-and-white tiles of the castle's audience hall. Your foe - a different knight, a different clan - stares with bulging eyes. His sword falls. *Clatter.* Then he falls too.

A pool of red spreads from the dead man.

\#

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 2
text:
The castle employs seven men in the rite of the duel.

**The Four Burdened Ones:** Four to grasp the arms and legs. Four to bear the dripping body. Four to depart unknown.

**Scholar of the Arid Art:** He lives behind a slatted birch door, to the side of the dueling hall. He draws from a body its water, and returns the body to its clan.

**The Wringers Twain:** They soak the sacred foe's blood up from the floor, and wring it into a silver-gilt iron ewer.

\#

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 3
text:
You must complete the ceremony. In one fluid motion draw a silken handkerchief the color of the sun from the white velvet pouch on your left hip, run it along the bloodied edge of your Steel, tuck the cloth into the empty pouch of black velvet on your left hip, and sheath your sword slowly, counting out exactly three seconds from the moment you begin to stow to the blade, to the moment you hear it's pommel *shink* against your scabbards copper throat.

Then spin in place. Face the high table.

Your great Lord, your beautiful Lady, your weaponmaster, two stewards, and sixteen visiting member's of your dead opponent clan; all sit with expressionless faces at a long marble table, cast in a slight honey color under a ceiling of tinted glass. Your weaponmaster's face is as masked as any other - such is the rule of the ceremony - but you think you notice, under his breastplate, a swelling chest.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 4
text:
Your Lord rises from the central chair.

"Honored, this clan is, through the sword of her servant.

"Thou hast slain well, sir.

"Honored, should our foe be, through the valiant death of her servant.

"He hath died well, my lords."

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 5
text:
Two by two, starting with the two beside your Lord and Lady, moving outward in pairs along the marble table, the lords of their rival clan rise. Each wears a crimson robe covered in white hexagons. Each carries, at his side, a short blade. A one-foot knife. 

Each lord turns to your Lord.

Each sets his left hand on the pommel of his blade.

The grip of your Steel is tight in your hand. Your jaw strains. The same tension stiffens your weaponmaster.

For a moment, the mood holds.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Paragraph 6
text:
Your Lady's voice breaks the silence in the high hall.

"Rival guests, your company this day has pleased my lord.

"We grieve for your champion.

"He held a fine sword. He carried his armor shiningly. His step shook the stones of our castle. Fateful stars only could spill the blood of such as he.

"We shall return him to your house with all ceremony."

The nobles turned to the lady. They bowed, stiffly, with faces blank.

Then they leave.

\#

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 1
text:
You may try to hold your gaze motionless, his eyes fixed on the painting-like image of your Lord and your weaponmaster and your happy Lady at their high marble table. Try as you may, however, something in the movement at an antechamber door pulls your attention. As if your eye were a fish on the hook, and the movement were the reeling-in of the fishing line. You must look at the door.

Othelmedir.

The dark-skinned, grey-haired necromancer watches you. Dressed in his robes of black satin like onyx, towering like a redwood, he casts a long shadow of an arm as he raises its physical counterpart, and beckons.

You *feel* yourself ripped from the memory. Not a painful tearing, but like the peeling off of a huge and dead scab. In a moment the hall is gone.

You stand in a plain room of featureless stone, with a small felt table laying between yourself and the towering necromancer. You feel your sense turn instantly old again. Not slow, but stretched and strained.

You are once more the Cracked Cup Knight, hard-worn soul, damaged vessel.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 2
text:
Othelmedir's voice posesses a distinctly deep, distinctly resonant sound.

"Your soul-sense tether is broken, sir.

"Your cracked body can sustain it no more.

"Throat, slit. Limbs, severed. Disemboweled. You have died and returned too often, your body is a cracked cup. It can barely hold that soul of yours. It cannot hold your sense as well.

"Your soul is strong, this I grant - but your senses are old and weak."

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 3
text: *Not now.* *Not in the place where your body now lies. Not with your Blue Lady on The Road, alone. Why should your body surrender now?*

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 4
text:
"Your lovely Lady rang for me. You two have gone far from our gleaming lake, far along the Road of Graves. She pleaded that I rescue you once more, no matter the distance which separates us.

"I cannot.

"She would not understand. But you will, no matter that you do not wish to.

"Your soul-sense is gone."

\#

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 5
text:
**The Indigo Lodge:**

The Kustodevite sect of mancers present the image of a person's soul as a building. A painted house, with many floors, many rooms, many windows and doors. The house begins with the boy on the roof, waving a flag at the pigeons in the sky. It descends down, through various rooms and persons: the husband and wife dining on the balcony, the maid dusting the curtains, the three old friends playing cards at a table on the porch. The lodge ends at the bottom, with the coffin-maker in the cellar.

\#

Othelmedir stares at you without blinking.

"Your own sense is not sufficient. Still, you might steal another's.

"I shant give you my own, ha ha ha.

"But you lie near middlemoss, on a well-traveled stretch of The Road. Cast your soul there.

"It is not likely that you shall find any sense. But, stranger things have occurred."

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Paragraph 6
text:
*Would it be YOUR sense?* *If you steal another's sight and touch? There is no valor in such action.*

*And even were you to take it freely, would the virtue - and sin - that is YOU be the same? You am hardly the same knight, if all the senses which affected that virtue - that sin - belonged to someone else before.*

"I have given you what guidance I can.

"I go. Search the road or be lost.

"Know this: your lady called me, knowing the price. I rendered aid.

"I shall be repaid."

 Othelmedir vanishes.

\#

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 1
text:
You stand on the bottom of The Road of Graves.

The underside of the epitaphs run out - pale and unweathered - before and behind. There is no soil, no encompassing dirt surrounding your cracked body. It is like you stand behind a reflection, one without stars, or a slate-colored sun, or sky.

With only the Buried.

They floated above your head. One Buried for every Road flagstone. They lay horizontal to you, staring at the top of his head from empty sockets: skeletons, wrapped and embalmed corpses. Near one edge of the road, where four new stones must have been freshly appended, four Buried stare down from bodies only half-decomposed. A mother in a dress of wormholes. A father whose nose has been eaten away. Two children; boy, and girl.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 2
text:
You'll want to sprint. You'll know why shortly. Your platemail lacks its regular weight, nor makes its usual clamor. Each pounding step on the bottom of The Road produces only a faint and tinny *Tap. Tap. Tap.*

Take care which dead you pass under. The empty eyes may affix Baleful Judgements on you. Try keep under the skeleton, long-dead, empty bodies.

After five minutes and ten-thousand miles you will hear the reverberations of another set of steps. Barefooted steps. Behind you. Keeping time.

\#

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 3
text:
**Behind:** Where the Road of Graves ran there lies now only a bristling nothing, filled of static. At the center of this mouth there lurches a form. The tattered, bloody, ten-foot aspect of The Red King.

**Ahead:** The Road seems to have narrowed to only a few stones in width. The line of watching Buried overhead thins respectively. Only a narrow band of complete, floating skeletons remains to witness your passage.

\#

Two things of smoke and mist will drop just before you. Call on your trained instinct for succor. Bring your Polished Steel out and cutting.

You have time to strike only one of the mist things. Then the other throws itself at you.

Though it is only a vaporous thing, you feel your whole body stiffen as if bound. Though you may try to raise your sword, your arm heeds not your will.

Behind you, the bare-footed steps slow. Stalking in.

You'll probably want to shut your mouth. The cloud will creep up neck. Your face will go numb.

The soft footsteps become softer still. Muffled. 

Now you hear only the breath of the cloud. Rasping. Vicious.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Paragraph 4
text:
If you've reached this point, only one course remains to you. Open your mouth and - inhale.

\#

Fire creeps down your throat.

Your ears boom like drums in a cave.

Pressure fills your skull.

The Road dissolved.

\#

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 1
text:
Your eyes crack open.

The branches of the old oak tree spread like a thick and knobbly spiderweb. The livid sky - that sky of the Time of Dying - lies behind the web of the tree. It seems to your sight, however, that the sky is in the foreground, the tree behind. It is as if all the tiny fragments of the firmament lie just within reach of your fingertips, and behind them expands some tractless flat sky of wooden bark, only seen through the gaps in the blue. The sun is there, but no clouds.

You breathe.

The air that comes into your lungs does not burn, as did that poisonous cloud in his hallucination. But it seems like strange air. As if it were made of different gas. The air seems light, and has the slightly bitter taste of winter air. And yet now is the season of Summer. No visionary summer either, but the true, hot reality.

You sit up.

You see The Road of Graves. The tree under which you lay grows only a few yards from the nearest epitaphs.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 2
text:
*This wind has a taste you do not recognize. And a cold stranger's hand. Whatever ancient sense is in you, which now sees and touches your world, is unlike to your own. Your own lost senses...*

*The flesh of your hands looks the same; scarred and stitched and calloused. Hmmm. You still feel the sting as you press the nail of that thumb against your index.*

*Your face- ah. The touch of cold metal. Your helmet...*

*Your Blue Lady.*

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 3
text:
As you rise - your muscles returning the sensations of someone else's body - you'll immediately catch relieving sight of your Lady, approaching, then beside you.

"My Cracked Cup Knight. No, stay. You need not rise. All is safe.

"Can you see my face?

"Can you hear what I say?

"You have been lying for only a few hours. Rest a little longer. Here, I will hold your hands again."

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 4
text:
You will feel her fingers close around your own calloused, stitched, scarred hands. You may rest in this way, for a time. When you are ready to proceed, look up.

\#

The sky and the trees seem normal to you now. The same slate-colored sun has begun to dip towards evening, but there are yet some hours of daylight.

You'll find your armor, shield, and Polished Steel Longsword lying against a large root of the tree. As you lift your heavy scabbard and looped it around your waste, you touch the hilt. You can almost feel the tension of the Steel in its sheath.

The Steel *feels* ready.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Paragraph 5
text: *In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 1
text:
In march, the rain always comes down heavily on Khoroduhn. All the rest of the year, the mountain town matches the cool, arid climate around The Lake of Tomatoes. But Khoroduhn seemes to lie under a special affliction - invoked by one of the seven god-necromancers to commence with Spring.

Naturally the rain falls first upon the keep. For the keep perches like a stone owl on the summit of the mountain. The whitewashed homes of Khoroduhn, and the guarded stone wall, glisten wetly in the shadow of the keep. Rolling along the descending hills, the rain slides continually along the walls, over the cobbled stone roads, in rivers along the gutters - as if the elements fight to wash some persistent stain on the city. Always the water runs down, deeper, and deeper, and deeper still.

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 2
text:
**The Serpent Shelf:**

In a dry, stone-girt, dark central room of the keep at Khoroduhn, there is a library with one long, spiral shelf - and at the top of that shelf there is a book with yellow pages - and on the first page of that book, a pale, sliding fungus has gradually devoured the name of the man who laid the first stone of Khoroduhn. Whoever it was, his family, and twelve families like his, have won and lost Khoroduhn each in turn. At the bottom of the spiral shelf are three books, spanning three hundred years, and every first name in those three books bears the family name of Weige.

The Weige family of Khoroduhn is one of the greatest in the cities around The Lake of Tomatoes. Their Majordomo, Pigburne, has guiled the lawful assassination of thirteen children in rival families. The family are said to be secret worshippers of The Red King, conducting black rituals in the corridors under Khoroduhn keep.

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 3
text: As far as the windscarred stone of the keep rises over the summer of the mountain, still twice as far do the corridors run beneath it. In the larders live the wine and the rats. In the mausoleum, skull after skulls stares blindly and thoughtlessly from black sockets, or sockets glinting with sapphire and ruby and onyx. And deeper still, under the bones, are the sewers. The sewers that run with the rain of March. The sewers where the throwaways and the outcasts and the embarrassments of all the families on the spiral shelf wind up. The sewers, that roll down through city, stretching like a labyrinths under Khoroduhn and its mountain.

name: 1 - Prelude - Paragraph 4
text:
Back above, in the boulevard of Wrinkler's Way, a woman-crier gives the day's portents.

"His Grand Corpulency, Gallbladder, Lord of Lake, predicts for today.

"Mold shall grow on your first crust of bread.

"And the flood rises rise unto the street of- Eh?"

The old crier stops. She holds a hand over her face. Water streams along her wrinkles.

A man stands backlit on a balcony, on the high eastern parapet of the keep. *A Weige family retainer*, thinks the old woman.

\---

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 1
text:
**Pigburne:**

In a high chamber of the eastern wing of Khoroduhn, the shaved-headed, middle-aged, swarthy Majordomo of the Weige family leans over ha desk crammed with jars.

"This one is a Trumpet.

"See it?

"Look as I hold the jar the near the candle.

"You can know this one by it's brown color, and wavy edges - but not all mushrooms are as kind to the constitution as the Trumpet.

"Never have I given the incorrect mushroom for another to eat.

"Do know why?"

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 2
text: Now that Pigburne has turned to look in your direction, you know the moment has come to perform your unpleasant task. You should first shut the balcony door. Then go without hesitating to the soundproofed door of the chamber itself, and throw the bolt shut. It may not be an honorable method, but without your equipment, secrecy is your only chance of slaying this man.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 3
text:
Pigburne will stroke his chin and begin to speak. It's best to cut his monologue short, but listening to what he says may give some insight into the Weige clan's inner workings.

"Hmmm. Now let me see what this means.

"Doubtless you are not one of my usual guards."

To have reached my chamber and locked my door, you will know that this wing is barren. And I am trapped.

"You carry no visible weapon, and although I am not young, nor am I some cripple or feeble nonagenarian.

But where is it written that a family assassin must do his work with a knife?

"The right and bloody hands will do the job.

"Will you answer me; which family are your masters?

"I thought not.

Too professional.

What if I told you, assassin, that although this room is proofed against sound and spying, still I have means of alerting-"

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 4
text:
If you let him speak until he gets to the part about his secret alarm, it's already too late. Nevertheless, you'll want to finish the job you came here for.
