Grab the majordomo around the neck with your pale, scarred hands. Pull him away from the desk and out of his chair - he has a knife hidden under the desk.

The strangling, if not honorable, is quick. In a few minutes Pigburne will cease to thrash.

You'll want to keep the padded crimson armor and cloth mask disguise of a Weige Family retainer. It may aid your escape, and the mask hides your cracked face. You also want to retrieve the knife strapped to the underside of the Majordomo's desk. Use it to open his chest and cut out his heart. It will prevent common necromancy from resurrecting this evil man.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Paragraph 5
text:
As you rise from behind the desk, you can hear the rain, coming steadily down, hissing dully beyond the balcony door.

Then, from another part of the keep, a bell begins to ring.

\---

name: 3 - Mentor - Paragraph 1
text:
Before you go, be sure to grab the following loot from Pigburne's chamber:

**Song-Singing Fungus:** A mushroom with a hidden mouth under its cap. Sings in the presence of uncontrolled dead.

**Constellation Chart:** This chart, drawn on thick paper, shows in lines all the major constellations visible from The Lake of Tomatoes. One set of lines, the Amber Ox, has been circled in ink.

**Jeweled Dagger:** Ornamental, but worth a few coins at least.

When you hear fists hammering the chamber door, you'll know it's time to run. USe the rope you stowed on the balcony to scale down the castle bricks and the cliffs below.

**Street of the Oldest Ghost:**

Finally your feet touch cobbled stone. The bells roar from the keep that is now above you.

As you turn, you see guard in padded crimson, a real retainer. He stares at you. Fifteen paces. Too far. In his left hand, he holds a signal horn. He raises it to his lips.

Instead of reverberating peal,  you hear one wet crunch. The man crumbles forward. Embedded in his spine is a morningstar covered in horn-like spines. Another man - lean and with a dark face - rips it free by the handle.

name: 3 - Mentor - Paragraph 2
text:
**R the Killer:**

"Escaping?

"Same here. The name's R the Killer. Just bust out of their gibbets.

"I'm no sympathist, but two red hands are better than one.

"They'll be locking the gate soon.

"What say, shall we slay a way out together?"

\---

name: 4 - First Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
The streets of Khoroduhn can be a maze for strangers, and R and you both are. To navigate the city, take the following course: a left at Larchroad, straight on for thirteen intersection, right down a long and crooked stair (watch out, the rain runs like a cataract over the slick stones), right through Black Janus's Tunnel, Left into Squallor's Road, and finally, up the wooden scaffold on your left after the third bystreet. By following this path, you also avoid most of the patrolling faceless retainers of the Weige Family.

R the Killer complains when you turn away from the nearest exit of Gate of Spears, but follows you regardless.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 1
text:
After climbing the scaffold of what an opera house under older families, but has now become a common inn, you'll arrive back at the room you rented when you first snuck into Khoroduhn.

You enter an eight-foot by ten-foot box. The rafters run grey with the dust of centuries just overhead. Something - something which glints in the light of the open door - is stowed in the corner of one rafter. Sparse furniture occupies the floor of cracked wood boards: two straw mattresses, and a paper screen with holes. A plain but clean green wool cloak covered on of the mattresses.

There are no windows, but someone has at some time painted two false ones in blue on the wall beside the door. Evidently this was long ago; the paint flakes now so that the windows are a calico of false sky and whitewashed stucco. An iron coal lamp, near the back behind the screen, hisses and filled the box with amber light.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 2
text:
You have hardly stepped inside when, from behind the screen, you see movement.

Your Blue Lady.

Her cloak flutters as she rushes toward the Killer behind you, a dagger in her pale fingers. She stops when she your raised palm.

"Two strangers stepped into my chambers, where I dwelt a stranger also.

"My Cracked Cup Knight, this is no noble man at your heels.

"Oh, but tell me of our foe.

"Has your hand freed us from the Weige family shadow?

"Are the ghosts of my husband and my sons and my daughter by some small measure sated?"

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 3
text:
You'll want to pull down your equipment from the rafter - you are going to need it soon. Take your armor, your helmet, your Pinecone Crest Shield, and your Polished Steel Longsword behind the damaged screen, where you can remove your crimson disguise and equip your true gear without revealing your face.

R the Killer meanwhile will introduce himself to your lady, with remorseless honesty. You recognize distaste your Lady has for the Killer by her tight-lipped, monosyllabic replies. The sarcastic tone in R's voice makes you think the feeling is mutual between them.

Stay focused on your equipment; make sure the buckle on your shield-strap is tight, and that your rusted right pauldron isn't getting caught on your breastplate and restricting your motion.

Soon enough, you'll hear a sharp banging from outside. R the Killer goes to the door, opens in a creak's-worth, and sneaks a glance at the street below.

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 4
text:
"City militia. Driving people off the streets.

"Cursed luck

"They'll have shut the gates by now.

"Oh knightly assassin - we should've run out when we had the chance.

"Your delicate eggshell here has cost us our escape.

"Look at you now. A speechless, clanking tinman."

name: 6 - Second Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
Six ways lead to the sewer.

**The Haymarket Well:** A railless hole in market square.

**Greeg's House:** This way is closed to you at this time.

**Khoroduhn Keep's Wine Cellar:** Your worst bet.

**Deathless Catiph's Mausoleum:** You can find this marble sepulcher atop a grassless gravel hill in Khorodun's largest cemetery. Inside the sarcophagus with the name scratched out, there is a secret tunnel.

**Abandoned Shack:** This shanty, just one street left of the Gate of Axes, is well guarded bt Khoroduhn's black-market cabal - Ruddynails.

**Hasodeki's Crucible:** This seemingly innocuous clay-firing kiln, in the Weige's Family's most esteemed necromancer's tower, serves as a portal to a hidden sewer chamber. The activation word is 'Bindweed'.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 1
text:
With a hum and crackle, your lamp and R the Killer's burning torch lead the way into the sewer. The twin sources shed a huge light in a small space.

Having descended down a thin borehole with a slick iron ladder, the tunnel you find yourself in shines with thicker slickness still. Lumpy slime - some brown-white mold that bloomed with drooping magenta petals - festoons the blackened brick walls like ivy on a mansion.

The hiss of rain is gone. Instead, a river babbles; not just from farther down or up the tunnel, or from the little stream of greywater flowing around your steel boots, but through the slimy brickwork walls.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 2
text:
R the Killer holds his crackling torch toward each direction in turn.

"You've got the armor, tinman.

"You lead.

"I hope that lady of yours is willing to get her silk socks filthy."

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 3
text:
Finding your way through the veiny tangle of the sewers with be no easy task. The tunnels, all of differing sizes and grades and flows, merge and split in seemingly endless junctures. As you explore this greywater grotto, you're likely to to encounter some sentient flotsam.

**The Ratwall:** The sewer stream flows right under this living wall of ratskin and ratheads. Probably some necromancer's work. The gap is too narrow for you to squeeze through. If R the Killer still has his torch, you can hold it against the wall. The hair will burn before a moment, before the entire structure collapses with a loud, leathery, ripping sound. IF R doesn't have his torch, you'll have to backtrack. Don't try cutting a way through - the wall eats Steel, and arms holding it...

**Mermaid's Lair:** Best to avoid this bloated siren. If you do choose to fight, trying getting under her belly. Her fish eye's can't look down.

**The Penny Lizard:** This enormous salamander collects all the coins which slip through the grates in the city above. You run the risk of encountering it at any passage in the sewers. While it's lair (and hoard) cannot be found, killing the monster and cutting open it's stomach will net you the day's copper and silver catch.

**Stitched Soul Men:** This colony of grey-eyed men with stitched-together flesh of mixed colors live inside of eight stone lanterns, in a vast carved cavern at the intersection of two main channels. At a single gong-note from one lantern, they'll attack. You'll have no choice but to kill them

name: 7 - Wilderness - Paragraph 4
text:
Eventually, your lamp and R's torch will both burn out.

If you grabbed The Cat’s Eye Spear from The Hall of Diffusion when you retrieved Mool’s Spit, you may use it now to light your way. Holding it aloft, whispering the secret word, a giant glowing cat's eye will on the tip of the spear. R has a free hand to hold the weapon.

name: 8 - Third Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
After hours of wander, you'll find a rough stone tunnel. It runs for a score of paces, with the rushing sound of the sewer water swelling at each step. It emerges quickly back into the slimy brickwork of the regular sewer.

At the end, one of the stitched soul fanatics crouches with a sharpened femur-bone. R emerges first from the tunnel. When he does, the wild man leaps.

R will see the movement just in time. Bringing the spear arcing sideways, deflecting the club, in the same motion he swings his Horned Morningstar in an uppercut. The horns sank up through the madman's chin, into the mad matter of his brain.

R bent over the corpse. He pointed to a tattoo on the shoulder.

"The Red King's Brand.

"They are his devoted, wrathful dreamers."

You stop, listening Three paths run into this intersection, including the carved tunnel. Three lead from it. Down the central egress you hear the sewer stream roaring slightly louder.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 1
text:
The tunnel lifts up overhead, and spreads apart as you lead your lady splashing through the growing rush of sewer filth. The Cat's-Eye spear in R the Killer's hand shows trio after trio of lesser sewer pipes spilling their massed rainwater into your main thoroughfare. Soon the water reaches your knees.

As if passing through some mystic threshold, the three of you suddenly stand in the midst of an impracticably vast cistern. You stand upon a wide circling stone ledge which rings a pool of unknown depth. Twelve rivers of sewage splash over the ledge, into this pool. The water just below you whirls slowly. Instead of grey, the water shifts hues constantly in a kaledoscope of whirling deep-blue and emerald and bright cherry-red.

At the whirlpool's middle, a mound of sediment and rags forms an island over the resevoir drain. Atop this mound, you see an oversized mahogany loom, rolling and clicking in continuous motion.

A giant figure sitting in in a rocking char at the loom notices your entry, and rises.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 2
text:
**Gigan Uthok:**

One of the Silk Giants, or 'Gigan', Othok was once used as a powerful weapon in the Weige family arsenal. He followed without fail the commands of Buster Weige, until that member of the house was slain during one of the bread riots. The death of his master drove Gigan Othok to madness, and he turned upon the family. His sinewy flesh proved too tough for any blade to fell him, but the Weiges managed to drive him deep into the sewers. The giant was thought to have starved to death decades ago, and was forgotten.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 3
text:
When the fight begins you won't have time to do much else than draw your Steel. As a Silk Giant, Gigan Uthok is incredibly nimble. He will spring from his island and reach your position on the ledge in a single bound. In doing so he'll instantly knock either R or your Lady down, removing them for most of the fight. You'll only be able to defend one of your allies with your Pinecone Crest Shield.

Your best option with be to block Uthok from striking your lady. R the Killer can't influct much damage to the hard-skinned giant with his Morningstar, and the vision of a beautiful woman will leave the silk giant hesitant and frightened while your Lady still stands.

The first moments of the fight will be frustrating. Gigan Othok kicks with both feet or swings with his left arm to attack. These attacks are fast, and hit hard, but you can block them safely. Inflicting damage however is almost impossible. Every time you swipe at the giant he will leap backward or skitter up the wall like a spider. The most you can inflict are glancing blows, which are not enough to cut through the giants saddle-like skin.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Paragraph 4
text:
The key to this fight is light. You'll notice that Othok keeps his right arm in reserve through the fight - he uses it to hold his hand-woven tunic of rope and sewer rags over his face. He keeps his head turned slightly away from the Cat's-Eye.

To win, wait until Othok leaps away, and pick up the Cat's-Eye spear. When he leaps back in, hold the light forth. The giant will scream and then stop in place, swaying, stunned. Use this moment to ram your sword through his tough abdomen, slaying the monster.

\---

name: 10 - Fourth Transition - Paragraph 1
text:
As the giant's dying scream fades to a gurgle, then to silence, the roar of the rushing, swirling sewer water once more fills the space. The colors of the water slowly fade, first to grey, then to a surprising silvery clarity. The distant mahogany loom on the island of sediment and rags - *clicking, clicking, clicking* all this time - stops.

R the Killer rises from where the giant tossed him. He is battered and dizzy, but otherwise unharmed. He will live to kill again, it seems.

name: 10 - Fourth Transition - Paragraph 2
text:
You hear the light splash of your Lady's footstep, then her hand upon your right pauldron. She speaks.

"The breath heaves within you still, my Cracked Cup Knight.

"Be easy once more. Yours was the better arm.

"Look. Across the pool.

"Some slimy section of the wall has crumpled away. A false wall, a woven net of slimy silk.

"See that darkness beyond? Perhaps it is a passage out."

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 1
text:
**The Showering Gorge:**

The tunnel leads for a hundred - or perhaps ten thousand - paces. It seems an interminable length of perfect darkness, broken only by your little light. The crash of the draining sewers gradually disappears. You are left with only the sound of your group's wet footsteps on the flat, carved rock.

Then, hissing. It grows steadily as you press along the rock.

A tiny mote of light appears in the expansive black. At first you do not trust it, but the light grows until it is the size of a walnut. Then, an orange. Soon a new sound begins to mingle with your footsteps. Hissing.

You come out on a broad, grassy ledge. It leans out over a small gorge. You hear splashing. Looking over the ledge, you can see the other sewer pipes emptying into the stream below.

The rain sprinkles lightly down about you. The sky is dimly illumined in the pre-dawn glow of the Slate-Colored Sun.

To the east lies Khoroduhn. You have travelled far underground. The bells of the distant whitewashed town and keep upon the mountain - not even the faintest echo of their clamor can you hear.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 2
text:
You're eyes behind your visor glance upon your lady.

*Look how her face is raised to the sky. You can see how the rain washes the slime from her brow and her hands. Are you not ashamed, to have let her soil her graceful person in the sewer of your enemy?*

*No pride should you take in guiding her to this safety, and slaying the master of her tormenters. You have served. This time, the service was a foulness.*

*Still, perhaps you may be pleased with the way she looks now. For she seems, having followed you through that darkness uncomplaining, to have cemented her nobility, though her noble house is ruined. You may be honored to serve her.*

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 3
text: The rain comes steadily down - it always comes steadily, over Khoroduhn and its keep and its city and its mountain, in the month of March. The rain washes the slime from your armor. It cleanses your Blue Lady's face and hands. It washes the gore and filth even from the killer's Horned Morningstar.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 4
text:
The walk through the final tunnel seems to have broken the stunned state which Gigan Uthok inflicted upon R the Killer. He stretches his arms, and wags his head like a dog. He turns his dark face toward you and your Lady.

"No ceremony. I'm done here.

"I'm too well-known a Killer around The Lake of Tomatoes now. It's off to Middlemoss for me.

"You think you're safe here, eh?

"No.

"That family will just find another master killer. Might be me, hahaha, if I weren't cutting loose.

"Take my advice. Make the trip to Middlemoss.

"Alone though. I'm done travelling with Tinmen and Melancholic Dames."

With his words to ponder, R the Killer leaves you and your Blue Lady alone in the rain.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Paragraph 5
text: *In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 4 - First Transition - Paragraph 2
text:
*Why does this black hound shadow your step? For him, doubtless, the evil work of tonight is only too usual.*

*Perhaps you should have driven him off before coming here. Pray to the seven god-necromancers that he is not a secret foe.*

\---

name: 5 - New Lover - Paragraph 5
text:
R the Killer cracks the door open again, this time with his Horned Morningstar ready in his hand. He'll wait in that position for a few minutes, before leaving suddenly, and trying to escape on his own.

You'll want to make a choice before the Killer leaves - he'll follow you if pick a different escape route. His fear that the gates are closed now will prove all-too-true. Trying to escape from the city by a guarded way will get you slain and your lady captured. Rumors in castle and hovel around The Lake of Tomatoes, however, speak of at least one secret way in-to and out-of Khoroduhn.

\---

name: Setup - Blob 1
text:
*"Of the means and motives of necromancy much is written.*

*"Of the ends, none discerns."*

The Book of Winterdemon

\#

name: Setup - Blob 2
text:
Your Blue Lady tosses and turns, under the thin grey blanket of her bed, through the early night. You hardly need sleep these days; you watch her from the cobwebbed, red chair in the corner of your rented room. Your notice that your hand is tense on the hilt of your Polished Steel Longsword, sharp in its scabbard. Will it cut dreams?

At midnight your lady wakes. She says nothing for many hours. Then, as dawn's sky periwnkle whispers on the glass of the window, your Lady speaks in a quiet voice.

"My daughter...

"Oh my Cracked Cup Knight, I have seen my daughter tonight.

"Her ghost. Darkly white the bottom of packed snow.

"But on her cheeks, a hot red blush.

"My knight, why should my daughter haunt me?

"Why not my sons? My Husband?

"Why her blush?"

name: Setup - Blob 3
text:
Even if reply were possible, the opportunity is denied you. Four heavy knocks rattle the painted ash door of your room. The walls, the floor - even the iron oil lamp on the bedside table - quiver.

Opening the door, you behold a mass of body filling the entire frame.

**The Steward:**

Serving as clerk, caretaker, cook, and every other role for The Graveside Inn, this creature seems too large for a mortal. You cannot take his whole aspect in at once, no matter how far back you move; without craning your neck, only his bristling grey mustache is visible. You suspect he was a soldier once - a soldier's cudgel hangs like a chopstick from his continental hipbone.

The Steward asks that you come downstairs immediately. Something dreadful is in the dusty air.

Your room lies in a far corner on the highest floor of this ancient mansion-turned-inn. The descent is long, the stairs many. The boards make no sound under the massive Steward's feet, but each of your own steelclad steps summon a communal groan from every surface. You pass by one window on the stair. Outside, over all the trees and grass, a late March frost is master.

The steward leads you in silence. He bears a four-pronged candlestick to light your way. There are many mirrors in the hallways; each time you pass one, four ghost-candle flare to life, then die with your passing.

name: Setup - Blob 4
text:
You enter the dining hall. Seven other guests at the Inn sit around a long, felt-topped table.

**Timberwolfe:** White hair. White beard. White bearskin cloak. Eyes: black. He breathes lugubriously.

**The Late Poet:** This troubadour is a Living Bones; a walking, conversant skeleton. Speaks often, usually in rhymes.

**Miss Leatherworker:** The woman wrings her hands constantly, like she is trying to squeeze all the blood from them - yet her face is windworn and placid.

**Six-Cats-Hiding:** He wears a set of purple silk, many-folded, fraying robes; the pattern on the robes shows hundred of white cats.

**Frida:** A woman, with blonde hair parted down the middle and shaped like two huge golden cauldrons upon her head head. Her body is disproportionately small. She looks anxious.

**Doctor Hoof:** A short and bald doctor; wears two normal-looking boots.

**Melancholia:** She smells like burnt sugar.

name: Setup - Blob 5
text:
The steward clear his throat.

"Hrrrummm. You see that Madam Strock is absent.

"She is murdered.

"Hrummm.

"I went to give her - Hrrrum - tea. She did not stir.

"HrrrrRRRrrrruuuuuuMMMMmmmmm."

name: Setup - Blob 6
text:
Your visored vision reverts to your Blue Lady.

*This happening upsets her. You know that look of sorrow, where the lines of her faceseem to darken.*

*You will protect her.*

*These others seem unconcerned by murder. Even the steward, with his face so high, wears a brow more-troubled by wrath than surprise.*

name: Setup - Blob 7
text:
The Late Poet speaks up at that moment.

"The doors are locked; the windows barred.

"The steward's words surely sound true.

"Unless it it be he, (and it's surely not me!),

"Why, the killer must be one of you."

name: Conflict - Blob 1
text:
At this point, you can follow a few different paths. Trying leave through the front door seems like the most obvious choice. When you reach for the heavy iron handle, however, you find the enormous oak portal locked shut. The Steward refuses to let anyone out of the inn until the killer is found and punished. (A note: you are better off not picking a fight with The Steward, at least not while he is fired up. He is probably too strong for you to beat at this juncture).

The other guests will proceed to take a breakfast of eggs and black bread with honey. You may join them.

Your Lady also suggests that you two might examine the scene of the murder for some clue to the killer.

name: Conflict - Blob 2
text:
Madam Strock's room lies in the Yellow Wing. To reach it, search for a flight of stairs with a dead crow on it's third step. This goes to the sixth floor of the inn. From there, take three left turns, until you pass through a room of furniture covered in dusty sheets. Do not lift any of the sheets. On the other side of the room is a stair which will take you back down to the third floor. From there, follow the yellow chalk marks on the walls, until you come to a mahogany door hanging loose on one hinge.

Madam Strock's room lies in total disarray. The red velvet curtain hangs partially torn from it's rod - slate-colored morning streams through the barred window. The cabinets are turned over, and one chest of drawers has been cleft completely in two, the splintered halves digging into the old wooden boards. Madam Strock lies atop her bedspread, amidst a red pool.

If you perform a thorough search, you can find the following pieces of information:

**Loose Straw:** Five long pieces can be found stuck in the threads of the rug.

**Jasmine:** There is a faint scent of jasmine perfume in the air, but it is overpowered by a damp, musty, earthy odor.

**Scroll Shreds:** A pile of paper shredding shredding, yellow with age, and bearing fragments of necromancy's Deadspeech, have been swept under a tall cabinet in the corner.

name: Conflict - Blob 3
text:
Your lady steps over to the bedspread, but does not touch Madam Strock's body.

"Will her face live on?

"Will she hang from the misty wall of some beloved child or grandchild's thoughts?

"I am sorry for this sorrow, my Knight.

"Your faith succors.

"My daughter-dreams, however, remind me-"

name: Conflict - Blob 4
text:
A scream reverberates through the inn, drowning your lady's words, swirling about in every chamber like a breeze through windows.

Following the sound is simple enough. It's echo leads you on, an echo rolling out for an impossible length of time. You follow the sound to the second floor.

**Tenant Theater:**

Seven rows of small wooden chairs with leather cushions sit in circles around a solid stone stage. A path of broken legs and rent cushions leads from one of two spectator-doors, through the rows of chairs, to the great slab. Atop the slab, Melancholia lies. She stares at the ceiling. There is a knife sticking up from the center of her chest - the same knife which the steward used to cut the morning bacon.

Miss Leatherworker stands against the wall a little to the side of the stage. She wrings her hands. The echo of her scream still reverberates, but her own mouth is closer, her face is placid.

name: Conflict - Blob 5
text:
But as the other guests filter in through the two spectator doors, Miss Leatherworker raises one more a series of short, echoing shrieks.

"Strolling after breakfast!

"Me. The girl. Doctor Hoof.

"A trio. Safer.

Alas, separated! In the Room of Windows."

name: Conflict - Blob 6
text:
The word 'windows' will still be rattling in the legs of the chairs when The Steward arrives. At the sight of the body, he clears his throat, then draws his chopstick club and swears to bludgeon whoever is the killer.

If you say that the knife is his, The Steward will stamp one of the wooden chairs into splinters, point a huge round finger, and say that the murderer could just as easily be you. Or, your Blue Lady. (You should resist the temptation to draw your Steel at this second comment - it will start a fight).

The only sure info you know at this stage is that neither you nor your Lady killed Melancholia or Madam Strock. By interviewing guests, you can find the following clues as well.

**Unless Three Share a Lie:** Timberwolfe, The Late Poet, and Six-Cats-Hiding have all been together since breakfast.

**Unless Two Share a Lie:** Miss Leatherworker, Doctor Hoof, and the victim Melancholia were together until an hours ago - Miss Leatherworker and Doctor Hoof became separated only a quarter of an hour ago.

**Unless Three Share a Lie:** Frida and The Steward and The Late Poet all heard the chords of a crow-and-tincan throat singing from The Yellow Wing half-an-hour before the murder.

name: Conflict - Blob 7
text:
Frida, as she is staring at the body, wraps her long and skinny fingers around the two blonde cauldrons of her hair.

"I KNEW this would happen.

"A curse shadows these rooms, our bodies.

"Once a man lived in these rooms.

"Kriecher of Graveside.

"Kriecher loved and lost the heart of a harridan, and she cursed him under her moon.

"Now he is a vampire, living but not, frozen far away.

"And his house will kill any other who sleeps between its walls."

name: Conflict - Blob 8
text:
Morning wrings the dew from the grass.

The next death will always be Timerwolfe. You cannot save him. You will find him in Madam Strock's room, lying atop MAdam Strock's body. Their blood has dried together on the linen bedcover.

The Late Poet stands beside the bed when you enter. His skull wears no expression, but the bones of his thumb and index finger pinch his mandible in a pensive manner.

name: Conflict - Blob 9
text:
"Death's not so bad, I'm well abreast.

"This troubadour is unimpressed,

"With fleshy stock. They're overdressed,

"You must be very hot.

"This troubadour covets a star,

"A muse worthy his sad guitar,

"Someone befitting sung memoir.

"But you, sir knight, are not.

"This lady here, though, rings my bell!

"Your blue complexion, mademoiselle,

"Another face- Ah, vain to tell,

"For a name, I haven't got."

name: Conflict - Blob 10
text:
Your Blue Lady turns her eyes from The Late Poet. You guide her from the room.

It is while you are escorting your lady back to the first floor, in search of the other guest - in the middle of wide chamber of wooden colonnades thick with sheets and curtain of dense grey spidersilk - that something slams you. Your armor crashes against the floorboards. Unhuman fingers wrap your metal boot, and drag you away.

name: Drive - Blob 1
text:
"...I will hold your hands."

You jolt from the dream. No gentle sunrise of consciousness is yours, but a rush back into your cracked cup body.

Your jerking motion is checked, however, by a rope wrapping your torso and arms and legs. You are bound, tied in your plate armor to an old but fine, cornflower-blue velvet chair. Light like a campfire's, filtering through a tiger-colored glass window, dances slantingly in the dust of the space. It is afternoon.

The prodigious Steward has been pacing before you. He stops when he sees your sudden thrashing. He wears your sword like kitchen knife on his hip.

name: Drive - Blob 2
text:
"Did the - hrrrrruummm - thrill go to your head?

"I found you where you killed her, between the Shell Shelves, in the fossilarium.

"Hhhhhrrum.

"Why'd you cut her buns off?

"They shall be preserved. Hrum. In the museum.

"But once I find your false Lady - 

"-hrrrrruuummm-

"-the murderess, your comrade in slaughter -

"-HHHHRRRRUMMMMMM-

"-I'll roast you in that steel."

name: Drive - Blob 3
text:
Nothing you now say will convince The Steward to release you. He is convinced that you or your Blue Lady killed the other guests. Nor can you break free from your bonds; the rope he tied you with is woven from the silk of the Sewer Worm. Your binding is stringer than the iron on the window.

You will need to wait, tied to the velvet armchair, for at least one more death. When The Steward is told by The Late Poet about this fourth murder, point out to him that you could not be the killer. He we untie your bonds, but he withholds your Polished Steel.

name: Drive - Blob 4
text:
The fourth victim, and the two after, occur in a random order. You can discover a clue to the killer near each of the bodies - the specific clue you receive depends on where that victim died in the order:

**Miss Leatherworker:**

1st - A belt, perfectly stitched, hangs from the rafters beside her.

2nd - Written on the bottom of her shoe: "Corn crow. Corn crow. Corn crow."

3rd - Found near the Closet of Masks, where every wall bears faces made of plaster.

**Six-Cats-Hiding:**

1st - A bowl of curdled milk lies spilled at his feet.

2nd - Found nailed to the top of the dumbwaiter shaft.

3rd - More loose straw, and a wad is stuffed down his throat.

**Doctor Hoof:**

1st - The last line in his diary is cut off: "I should have locked the window anyway, knowing..."

2nd - There is one empty, labeled vial in his satchel - 'Ill Omen's Heart"

3rd - A hole-riddled, sun-bleached tophat is locked between the death's-vice of his fingers.

\#

name: Drive - Blob 5
text:
You march down a corridor of paintings. Every painting is a portrait, and no two faces look alike. One bald man with a bullbouse nose and rosy, poxmarked cheeks ogles you with a pair of soft brown eyes. A woman, half her face behind a veil of mouse skins, glances sidelong in your direction. A dark-skinned boy of ten watches you behind his painted eyelids.

You were separated from The Steward some hours ago, in the ballroom of the manor, where the floors and walls change configuration every moment. You have been left to wander without your Steel.

Dusk approaches.

name: Drive - Blob 6
text:
**The Sweetmeats Room:**

As the day reaches its close, you step into this chamber, high on the thirty-third floor of the mansion. A dozen potato sacks hang from railroad spikes that have been driven into the rafters. Each stack is swaying, as if there were a breeze, and is dripping some brownish fluid. Additionally, there are two huge, multi-level, silver displays. Each one is crammed with candles and bonbons.

You see The Late Poet. He is pointlessly masticating a bonbone beside one of the displays. The Steward lies dead - a veritable sea of viscera - at The Poet's bony feet. The Poet holds your Steel.

 When he sees you enter, The Lat Poet flourishes the blade in your direction, once, then laughs. He tucks the weapon into its scabbard, and hold it out for you to take.

name: Drive - Blob 7
text:
You'll need to act fast to survive this next attack. As you reach for your weapon, something heavy slams into your paudron. You'll crash against the floor. Whatever it is continues its momentum. It slams the display where The Late Poet stands. There is a enormous crash of metal and chocolate. The silver dishes send dazzling reflection of the candles spinning around the chamber, dazzling your sight.

Ignore the lights. Raise your shield in the direction of the initial charge. You'll feel another impact against the front of your guard - you still can't see *what's* attacking you. It will throw you against the other display. With another crash and hail-like thudding, the candles gutter out. The room goes dark.

In the aftermath, you hear the clucking sound of dry bones on floorboards. Then, a raspy, fading noise, like a broom sweeping slowly away.

name: Drive - Blob 8
text:
After many minutes, your firestriker finally conjures a small flame from the wick of a candle. You scan the room. Bonbons lie everywhere. Mixed with them are The Late Poet's scattered bones.

*You are alone, your Lady's fate as yet unknown.*

name: Finale - Blob 1
text:
By now you'll have probably realized that this killer is some creature. It's not one of the guests - unless they be one of necromancy's ends - and it isn't your lady. Take your Polished Steel in hand, knight. Hunt it.

One thing you can try is going back to the scene of each murder. IT may take many hours to navigate the creaking, silent halls. If you do, you'll find that each body, and even the blood and other signals of killing, have vanished. The inn feels tenantless.

name: Finale - Blob 2
text:
As you walk, that broom-sweeping sound sometimes reaches your ear. *Rasp. Rasp. Rasp.* You are never able to trace it, each time you follow it into a room or corridor, the sound has ceased by the time you enter.

The final confrontation will always happen in one of three rooms.

**The Belfrey:** The heavy bronze bell has snapped from its line. It now lies embedded in the splintered, uneven floorboard

**Octopus Atrium:** This central garden is full of brittle shrubwood and small but deep pools.

**The Roaming Room:** This room roams the halls on a hundred marble feet, like a centipede. It seems docile. You may enter through it's 'face'.

The specific room makes no difference. When you enter, you will se your lady lying atop a table within. She does not stir, but you see her breathing.

When you run toward her, it will trigger the final battle.

name: Finale - Blob 3
text:
**Gallowspook:**

This tall and lank wight may only be summoned under the light of a harridan's moon. It's  sackcloth skin bristles with the feather's of albino crows. It is heavy despite it skinny frame - the body is stuffed with hazelwood chips. The creature wears a huge cloak of woven straw, which swishes and sweeps the floor as it walks. In its left hand it bears a candelabra, with seven low-burning candles, though there are places for more.

name: Finale - Blob 4
text:
You don't have to slay the Gallowspook to win this fight, you just need to survive until the candles in its candelabra burn out, at dawn.

The Gallowspook moves fast. At the start it will try to envelop you up in its straw cloak. If it succeeds in catching you, you'll find the cloak to be airtight, and you'll suffocate. Don't let it catch you. Whenever you find the fiberous garb closing around you, cut a way through with your Polished Steel. The Cloak sews itself back together, but at least you won't be caught.

Occasionally the monster will try a more conventional punch with its free hand, or a heavy kick. Keep your Pinecone Crest Shield up and ready. Even one of these blows, aimed right, will crack your bones.

You'll also want to stay relatively close to your unconscious Lady. The Gallowspook will sometimes lose interest in you as a target, and rush to attack her. Be ready to intercede. Cut at the monster as often as possible. You probably won't kill it, but you can slow it down with repeated blows. Keep up this rhythm, and you'll triumph over this oversized scarecrow.

name: Finale - Blob 5
text:
From some deep cellar, an ancient grandfather-clock booms with the morning hour.

The Gallowspook vanishes in puff, it's white feathers floating down, disintigrating as they touch the floor.

Your Lady stirs. As you rush to her side she sits up, rubs her head, then blinks with remembrance of your surroundings. When she sees that you are unhurt, he face adopts it's customary aged sorrow.

You hear voices from without. A moment later, you see a crowd bustle through the door into your room. It is the seven fleshy guests, led by The Steward. The latter looks at you. He asks who you are, and if you would like rooms for the night. A few minutes later, The Late Poet wanders in, rubbing an olecranon. When he sees you, he shrugs.

name: Finale - Blob 6
text:
"Of the means and motives of necromancy much is written.

"Of the ends, none discerns.

"But to meet again this beautiful melancholy lady is fitting,

"That memory I might upturn.

"You once cast out some offspring, that I'm certain.

"His face I crossed

"In middlemoss,

"But that is your concern."

name: Finale - Blob 7
text: *In The Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 1
text:
"Old marble cellars trim the front,

"That Temple to the goddess dead,

"Into those vaults I'd never tread,

"Though treasure's one might hunt.

"Called also House of Emerald,

"In Middlemoss they're wealth abounds,

"The Temple's built on secret grounds,

"But high, they're price, I'm told."

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 2
text:
Your Blue Lady's cloak flickers in the low wind as she steps across a long stone Bridge. Her step is unsure, and even the small gust sways her to one side of the unguarded span. Middlemoss's long chasm opens its stone throat to accept any that would fall.

Your Lady places her hand around your vambrace. She lets you guide her across the walk. The other dwellers, crossing the bridge at that hour, in the chasm town at the heart of The Road of Graves - they pay her little mind. All but one. As your step nears the opposite wall of the chasm, your Lady's cloak flutters again a man going in the opposite direction. She draws her cape back. The man is hairless of scalp, with only a little mouth, almost invisible in a black circle of tattooed skin. He is one of the monks of silence. He gives your lady a withering look, before passing on.

Your Lady hardly notices. Her eyes are fixed on the marble vaults in the cliff wall across from you.

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 3
text:
*You hope that The Temple will give your Blue Lady the lost son she seeks. Few are the avenues she has not explored in this city. Even so, you know that the priestesses of the goddess-necromancer will take more than they give.*

*Greed lives in The Emerald House.*

*It's painted edifice seems almost soft beside the other mausoleums. Like a growth of ivy on the surface of this place. Your Steel must be ready to cut its way back out - but you should hope that does not come to pass.*

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 1
text:
Standing just inside the crack of the silent stone door, there stands a woman. Her robes are ivory muslin, with yawning, gold-trimmed cuffs, and two padded shoulder-shelves that catch her hiar like coils of rope. When she speaks, her voice is deep and strange.

"Polgroms, mournors, debtors, addocts?

"Thou need not answor.

"Evory body has a painting somewhore in it's post.

"The House has paints for evory color.

"Evory soul."

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 2
text:
You pass through a convoluted vestibule of lantern-shadowed marble. The walls are crooked, and heavy with portrait paintings. Your passage is heralded by song. The music is of human voices, rising from behind the portraits, but not human words. Each voice seems to mimic some instrument.

**Walrus Painting:** A man with a pointed steel cap and a long grey mustache. It produced a brassy, squeaky sound, like a hunting-horn rusting as it blows.

**Madam Painting:** This tall woman with a goose's neck and tight black hair trembles like the string of a violin.

**Wind Painting:** Sun-scarred and rain-worn, this olive-skinned elder produces only a halfhearted whisper. Like a voice speaking to itself, but right beside your ear.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 3
text:
You may choose to explore a little before you leave the vestibule of The Emerald House. 

The priestess will stop beside a flight of broad, shallow stone steps that slope gently downward. She will wait there, seemingly forever, and does not seem to mind if you wander again through the gallery you have just passed.

Feel free to stop at each of the portraits. There are dozens. Each produces a different sound. If you stop for a bit before one portrait of an apple-cheeked, full-flushed young woman, you may notice that the paint is shiny and new. There is a stone bench beside the portrait. If you look in the dust beneath, you will find an empty paint bottle. There is a paper tag tied to the blue-drooling lip of the bottle, with a name: Squide. This will come in handy later.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Blob 1
text:
**The Easel:**

The sanctorium of this temple to Lady Horsehair is a tall cylinder, with a floor like a stepped bowl. At the bottom of this bowl is hexagon of emerald tiles. A few statuettes, and a huge wax golem, stand upon the stairs leading down. Nothing stands on the emerald tiles.

There is also a semicircle balcony halfway up the cylindrical wall. Two tunnels like black eyes burrow into the wall at either end of the balcony. Six priestesses in white-and-gold robes with padded shoulders stand up there, looking down

As you step inside, another procession enters from a door on your right. Two more priestesses, carrying something tall and heavy between them, under a velvet tarp with tassels that drag loudly on the floor. They set their burden in the middle of the emerald. They pull the velvet tarp away.

An easel.

A canvas.

Paint.

name: Setup - Blob 1
text:
Edda let her fingers splay out on the lumpy stone. She always held her head askance in this performance, her other hand letting the paint-heavy tip of her brush waver above her grainy canvas surface. She imagined her splayed fingers looking impossibly long and shiny. Her plaited hair tickled her shoulder. She imagined it was artic blue, but probably it was an ordinary color.

One of the doctor's behind her coughed. Edda splayed her fingers wider, listening more intently, ignoring the shushing of the other doctors. Even when silent, she always knew they were watching her by the crackle of their lamps, and their breathing.

A rattle like a cicada vibrated up through the stone. Vibrated through the petrified tissue of the earth. Vibrated from the earth's bones. A rattle only Edda heard.

She exhaled sharply.

name: Setup - Blob 2
text:
"Will the blowing tunnel bring us medicine, Edda?" asked one of the doctors. She recognized the voice, recognized it at once as Lutz's voice. She did not move.

"Paint for us," another doctor commanded.

"Do the bones portend a quake?"

"Quiet. The seer's still listening."

"No," Edda heard her own voice say. "No, gentlemen. I have the image." She began to paint.

name: Setup - Blob 3
text:
Edda painted a vault where three bristling timbers held up a drooping ceiling of tissue, mottled with black decay. Under it she painted clear water, and at this the doctor's murmured excitedly. The Heart Well had not produced water for many beats.

It was Edda's first false painting. She dared not paint her true vision, not with Lutz in the room. When the day's wind had slowed, she would go to the private vein of Jakob, Lutz's brother.

Jakob had been the subject of today's vision. Jakob, thrashing, disintegrating in the Hissing Green Sea. And Lutz had been watching.

name: Conflict - Blob 1
text:
"Private veins." The old herdsman with the long grey mustache leaned against his crook, and scoffed. "What have those carvers and leechers of the father body done to deserve their private veins?"

The other herdsman standing in that long arterial tunnel set his lamp down, and shood away one of the bacterial sheep as it tried to sniff the light. He said, "I think they scrape the wind tunnel for fungus, sometimes."

"It's all stone anyway. What's a little mold matter?"

"You're probably right."

"Besides," the old man went on, twisting one end of his mustache, "god's got veins enough for everyone."

"Only if you want to live like a dweller."

"Hmm." The old man raised a hand. He squinted into the long, wide, dark plain of the tunnel.

The younger man said, "Well there's room in one of those veins now."

"One of them try to fix himself did he?" The old man laughed.

"Nooooo. Have you not heard?" The old man wagged his bristly chin. "What I heard was that that younger doctor, Jacob, fell into the Hissing Green Sea, and burned to death."

"Bad end."

"Yep," the young man agreed.

"Aren't they high on that special seer they have? The blind one?"

"Edda."

"Well, why didn't he have her predict his death then? Could've saved-"

At that moment, however, the pair saw a group of their sheep straying into one of the deadfall passages. They stopped speaking, and ran to head off the herd.

name: Conflict - Blob 2
text:
Edda took another heavy breath. She tried to focus on the air in her lungs, to calm herself. IT was no good, not with the dioxide of the jury surrounding her, scraping her throat. "Gentlemen," she finally went on, "I am telling you the truth."

"So you said before," came Lutz voice, sharp on her ear with sarcasm.

"Prophetess." The nasally but kinder voice of Doctor Mirie. "How can you be certain of this- new vision? Is the voice the same as what you heard last year? Can you paint it for us?"

"It's not the same voice. I've tried to tell you that."

"Once more, please, prophetess."

"Lutz killed Jakob. He pushed his brother into the sea. In the original vision-"

"False," Lutz said without an affected anger that only Edda could hear.

"-original vision," she went on. "He seemed to envy his brother's control over the medical wind."

"But you said the vision did not show Lut'z killing his brother," said Mirie. "Only watching."

"Yes, doctor."

"Then how do you know?"

"His brother's bone-"

"Enough!" This was not the voice of Lutz, but of Doctor Tillman Noske, who was Lobeman of the god's body. "Enough, prophetess. We see you feel strongly, but no heresy will we brook his the god father's sacred wind chamber. Only the god's bones may whisper to you. Even this, we only granted for the veracity of your paints."

Edda bit her lip.

name: Conflict - Blob 3
text:
Lutz said, "The prophetess thinks she knows the body  better than its doctors."

A silence swept the assembly, whisper-broken. And in the whispers, Edda's sharp ears caught the words, "-better serve the dwellers in the southern way's."

name: Drive - Blob 1
text:
Edda's fraying shoe caught a burl in the uneven tunnel. Though she knew the balance of walking without seeing, this time she failed to catch her fall. Her palms struck the acid-washed stone of the The Way Dark and Long.

"The other way! The other way!" Jakob's voice, the voice of his bone somewhere in the god's body, seemed to cry louder with each bygone hour.

The fall had rattled her teeth. Edda only rose carefully to her knees, not making a sound. She patted the floor, making sure that none of her precious mushroom, or curds of sheep cheese, had spilled. It mattered little. Only a small morsel of rations were left. Edda had held without seeing to the world for so long, however, that to let go of life now was not within her.

"Can't see in the dark?" Edda stiffened where she knelt at the sound of the voice before her. All her creeping had been for nothing. The voice was not Jakob's. It was a husky voice, from the only other dwellers in the god's body who might step as softly as Edda. From *the* Dwellers.

name: Drive - Blob 2
text:
"Blind." Another voice spoke from behind. Then others joined it.

"Not to the dark only. Look at her eyes."

"Thrown out by the Uppers, doubtless."

Edda managed to summon a little rasp from her dry throat. "Please," she said. "Water."

"Best we send her to the sea," said the first, husky voice. "Little enough comes our way from the uppers. Not food enough for the ones they throw away."

Edda could hardly hear the Dweller's. "The other way! The other way!" Jakob's voice screamed in her ear.

"Please," said Edda again. "The bone's so loud now."

There was a silence then. Once more she heard "The other way!" Then, nothing.

"She couldn't see it," said one of the Dwellers at last.

"How'd she know?"

"Maybe saw the one the bone belonged to, thrown in the lake.

"Girl." Husky voice. "How did you see that bone we found in the sea?"

Edda only shook her matted, uncolored hair. To say would bring skepticism at best. At worst...

"Well," the husky voice deliberated, "If she won't say, we can sell her to the burker."

name: Finale - Blob 1
text:
"I've don't want them back alive."

Edda recognized Lutz's voice. She felt her skin prickle, under her rope binding, to discover that he was the dwellers' 'burker'. But it wasn't Lutz's voice that kept her attention. It was that of Jakob's bone. "They must be golden!"

"Not our job to kill," said the husky leader of the dwellers. "You said, 'bring them back and I'll pay.'"

"Not alive."

"Not ours to kill, doctor."

"They must be golden! They must be golden!"

name: Finale - Blob 2
text:
"They must be golden," said Edda.

She had spoken under her breath, to herself. The dwellers musth ave been as sharp of ear as of eye, however. She heard them look at her as one. "What did yo usay?" asked the husky one.

Lutz spoke on, "I'll give a knife-"

"Be quiet." Said the dweller. Then Edda heard his face turn to her. "What did you say?"

"They must be golden," Edda repeated. "It is what his brother's bone repeats."

The dwellers made not a sound.

"If you don't have the spleen to finish-"

name: Finale - Blob 3
text:
Edda shuddered as she heard the wet gurgle that suddenly silenced Lutz's voice. She heard the stumble of the doctor's boots on the stone, then a heavy, final thump.

"Why?" Edda asked simply, facing the dweller.

"'They must be golden.'" said the speaker for the dwellers. "It is the key to another world. It is the answer to the riddle of the god body. 'How can his eyes look on a light that never fades?' They must be golden eyes."

"The seer will live among us," said another.

"She can speak the secrets of the gods' bones. Truly."

Despite her weariness, Edda could not withhold a little smile. She said, "I could say more with a brush. And, paint."

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 1
text:
Your Blue Lady looks down upon the easel, then at the balcony, then turns her face to the priestess who led you in. She twines her fingers before her.

"I would know to what far curve of the road my lost Stepson has gone.

"Does he even breathe?

"He was only a tenling, born to my lord's first wife.

"Before my nights at my lord's bedside.

"Long before the adobe of our castle wept with red.

"A tenling, fair-haired, and with perhaps this pinecone broach I wear, upon his chest."

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 2
text:
On the balcony, the six priestesses speak in turns. Each of their voices is differently strange.

"Sonsssss and girlssssss - otherssssss sssssought."

"Ne. Ver. A. Step. Son."

"WE RECOGNIZE THIS BOY."

"He he. We have traded for deeper-buried secrets than your yearning, sad lady. He he he. Secrets whispered into cups. He he. Secrets written on paper and hidden under rocks. He."

"Butshehastopaintforit."

"Climb down, small spider.

"Reflect in the emeralds.

Paint the face you see."

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 3
text:
You notice then that the priestess who led you into the chamber must have slipped away. She now reappears. She carries a robe that is the color and texture of decaying hedgeapples. She bids your Lady put it on.

**Lady Horsehair - The Porcelain Girl Doll:**

By now you will likely have started to realize that The Emerald House is not some mere Middlemoss moneylender. Ostensibly, the temple is an art gallery. A visitor might ask any favor of the priestesses. Their only price? A self-portrait.

The reality is more-befitting the necromancer-goddess of greed.

The priestesses of Lady Horsehair truck in souls.

Under the direction of their Grand Daughter, the temple gathers the souls of their supplicants. They store these souls in phylacteries, a tool more common among true necromancers, scraping each soul for the knowledge it had in life. After a soul is scraped, they serve it to their goddess.

How do the priestesses acquire these souls? A contract, binding on death.

They make you paint your face.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Blob 1
text:
Your lady's first strokes leave delicate, wet, midnight trails over the upper quarter of the canvas; they dribble some, before fabric swallows the paint. She paints over these with black. Then, below, circles of wet eggshell.

In your Lady's hand the brush moves delicately and with care. Hers is a noble, practiced hand at the arts. The features of face begin to take shape. Her mouth, her downcast eyes, and the shading.

You notice, from your seat on the stone steps, that beads of sweat have started to form on your lady's brow. The portrait is acquiring all her sorrowful vitality, but her own face looks wan.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Blob 2
text:
The moment you rise, preparing to stop your Lady's hand, the six priestesses on the balcony will raise a chorus.

"Ssssstop."

"Sac. Re. Lig. Ous. Knight."

"THE NECROMANCER GODDESS WOULD LAY A CURSE UPON YOUR SPINE FOREVER."

"He he. This is no light art. He he. This is a blessed rite of the seven. He he."

"Holdtoyourvowsstupidmale."

"Let your lady paint,

"The longhaired brush, caressing,

"Like dewy grass blades."

name: 6. The Master Appears - Blob 1
text:
*Gong. Gong. Gong. Gong.*

A gong crashes from some remote deepness of The Emerald House. You hear two feet tap on the stone simultaneously, and turn your visor to the door.

Two priestesses descend. They take measured steps, together, each stepping first with her left foot, then bringing the right to the same step.

Between them they carry a fresh palate. The paint splotches gleam, like a tray of differently-colored eyes.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Blob 1
text:
You must intervene, sacrilege or not. If you don't stop the ceremony, your Lady's soul will be forfeit.

dRaw your sword and cleave through the palate.

Six shrieking voices will erupt from the balcony above you. Your Lady's stroking brush will falter - she'll look up and around with a vague, confused expression.

The two palate-bearers will leap back from you and clap their hands together. Resist the urge to cut them. They are too quick, and trying will mark you as a target of their avenging ghosts.

You'll see a flash in the corner of your visor, a shiny surface. If you're quick enough with your shield, you can avoid some broken ribs as the wax golem wallops you.

The monster bears you to the stone. Your Steel clatters down the steps. The two priestesses grab your lady. They pull her through a doorway, and you are dragged off by the stomping automaton of wax.

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 1
text:
**The Exhibition of Wasted Paint:**

By the time you touch the floor again, your cracked body is bruised and rattled from the handling of the wax golem. Your armor is sticky with wax. The golem trots away from you, returning to the stone door by which you entered, and slamming it shut.

You find yourself in a large room, with a low, low cieling, and hexagon pillars. On each face of each pillar hangs a faded self-portrait.

A skeleton leans against a near pillar. As you rise, so does it. It is The Late Poet.

"I thought to find my star, within the temple deep

"The priestesses saw through me, and left me here to steep.

"The paintings are phyacteries, where dead souls shall return,

"Your cracked soul they must think is cheap; your lady's, they would earn.

"She's struck her bargain already? Her painting's sealed her fate,

"Her soul will go to them in death. In death it allocates,

"Into this shrine,

"Where no thought mine,

"Can  free her from the state."

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 2
text:
One of the first thing's you'll notice about the room is the two additional wax golems. They patrol in a complicated but set pattern between the pillars. Each carries a key to the single stone door of the exhibition, but you haven't a hope of besting either without your steel.\
You'll want to start examining the space - take care. One of the golems is berserk. If you get to close to it, it will kill you. To look at the paintings and search the room, keep an ear out for their footsteps.

If you promised in The Ghost Factory that you would retrieve and bury A Brother's Skull, you should retrieve it while you are here. It is behind a bookshelf, next to the painting of the clown paled by moonlight.

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 3
text:
Each painting has glowing stone below its frame. All the paintings are peeling and faded. The souls are gone. Some appear almost to have returned to blank canvas. A few catch your eye.

**Man with Sheep's Wool:** A coarse beard of curly white wool cover's half of this cursed man's face.

**The Broom Woman:** She holds one. If you touch the fading handle, some of the flaking paint will slip into your skin.

**Time-Eyes:** The paint of his eyes alone has fallen away, leaving two pupils like the hands of clocks. No matter how long you stare, the hands refuse to move.

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 4
text:
*How can you take the keys from the golem? If you had your Polished Steel-*

*Wishing will not free Her.*

*The Living Bones is garrulous, perhaps a curious ally. You could ask him for his leg bone. A club, though not sturdy. No substitute for a cutter against wax.*

*Will those priestesses leave you here to starve?*

*You MUST save Her.*

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 1
text:
*What is that which your eyes just saw moving in that painting, on that face of the pillar? That was no shadow. You know that men left to brood in ill-lit places will fall prey to ill-lit imaginings. But no, that young man you see on that painting is younger of paint too. Something is alive about that one.*

*Your old master once spoke of a monster he cut low. A wearer of object disguises. Your old master... There was one of deadly skill. He, without longsword, was a match for all comers.*

*There. You saw the painted face move. Will you wait for hunger?*

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 2
text:
As you rise, resolving to try this moving painting, a voice catches your attention. It is not the poet's.

**Squide:**

from a recessed shelf in the stone, a human slips. His face has both the vitality of young man, and the wasting deep eyes and wrinkles of hard aging. He looks at once twenty and a hundred, with a head full of stark white, long, sway hair. He has to look up at you when he stands - his spine is bent like a letter C. His shirt is covered in paint stains.

"I- I- I hh-h-heard what the b-b-bones ssssaid.

"you're in a f- f-. A f- f-. Hahaha. A quandary.

"T-t- too late n-now. If-f-f-f your L- L- L- Lady painted, they h-h-have h-h-her.

"I was the chemist. I know.

"But I made one s-s-special p-p-.... Acid.

"The Unhue.

"That could b-b-break the bond."

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 3
text:
The chemist Squide will say no more, and look no more at you. He crawls back into his stone shelf.

Armed with his knowledge (and scant else) you'll have no real choice but to return to the moving painting. As you approach, it's wooden frame bends subtly. As though in expectation.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Blob 1
text:
**Eleven Strokes with The Old Man Below :**

You round the six-faced stone, and the mimic strikes. The painting swathes you like a leaden tarp. Dense, crushing, hard - the monster squeezes.

Let it squeeze, but keep your arms high. Raise your pinecone escutcheon. As your breastplate crimps, strike. Pound your aegis down. The art will hiss. Pound again. It shrieks. Pound.

Like a cloak loosened at the clasp, the false painting slides from around your shoulders. It forms a puddle at your feet.

As you expected, there is a tunnel in the pillar which had been concealed by the mimic. Taking the glowing stone from the shelf beneath the black hole, raising it inside, you will see a curving, narrow, ascending stair.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Blob 1
text:
Many steps later, you emerge from the narrow tunnel and drop down a short stone ledge. You feet thud softly onto grass.

A garden. The ceiling is open to the sky, open to the high heath above middlemoss. Night songs float down to your ear. The mixed silver of moon and star limb a hundred bulbs of chrysanthemums. There is a trellis against one wall, a possible escape.

Escape, after your Lady is saved. You see a cracked door behind one bush. It leads back down.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 1
text: Your objectives are three in number. First, retrieve the Unhue acid. Second, free your Lady from her binding to the phylactery. Finally, escape with your Lady from The Emerald House. You may accomplish the first of these in one of two ways. Openly, or by stealth.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 2
text:
If your goal is to sneak through the temple without raising the alarm, you'll want to collect the spare silk robes that can be found atop the decorative glass statue in the garden. Ripping the robe into rags and wrapping your boots will muffle your normally-clanking passage through the halls.

From there, you'll just have to take it slow. Check each corner before you round it. The upper halls are mostly devoted to the private quarters of the priestesses. Most rooms, if you peek inside, are closed, windowless chambers, well-furnished but small. The high priestess's room is locked behind a door gilt in purple and gold. There is a key on a shelf in a room beneath the easel room, where only yellow light may shine. If you take the key and use it to get inside the high priestess's room, you will find that it is empty but for a single tripod, on which sits a spherical mirror. It is called the **Left Eye of an Old Ent**. It's function is yet unclear to you.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 3
text:
If you fight, you'll find the priestesses and their servants to be no soft foes. Aside from the wax golems, which can crack your bones through your armor if you don't block their fists with your Pinecone Crest Shield, the priestesses each have one of a certain set of violence talents.

**Wailing Priestess:** Her rituals of grief can fill even a stout heart with despair, and stop a weak one.

**Tonguebinder Priestess:** Not especially effective against you. Her prayers to the goddess can stymie the mind of a necromancer.

**Gargoyle Priestess:** This kind have covered their skin in grey, painted, leering, fanged faces. Their flesh is stone. They fight with quick knives.

**Living Hair Priestess:** Keep your Steel swinging with this kind.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 4
text: By stealth or slaying, you'll find the chamber of paints on the third layer, between a doll-statue and the temple loomery. The Unhue is in a small, plain clay jar, bottom shelf, third row on your left.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 1
text:
The easel, your lady, and her completed portrait await you in the sanctorium. The easel stands still on the hexagon of emerald. Your lady's portrains leans on it, full of life. Your Lady sits, slumped, motionless, head-hung, on the stool. Her face is a drift of pure white snow.

You have hardly taken three steps down the stairs, when onto the balcony file the same six priestesses.

"Godlesssss sssswine."

"You . Have. Bought. Your. Doom."

"THE PROCELAIN DOLL WILL SUCK YOUR MARROw."

"He he. We'll give your lady what she wants. He he."

"Shegaveuswhatwewantafterall."

"But your heresy,

"Purchases a roadless grave,

"Worm food, unmarkered."

**The Painted Twins:**

You hear their thundering footsteps before you see them. A moment later, the doors on either side of the room swing inward, banging on the stone.

Two Wax Golems. Both painted a hundred colors. The one nearest you has a bat-face. The other is featureless and yellow. Both are nine feet tall, a thousand pounds apiece, and immediately start marching toward you.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 2
text:
You're in for a bone-rattling, no mistake. The paint covering these two golems makes their wax as hard as stone. It's not a full coat, but it'll make anything other than precise cuts and stabs useless.

The Painted Twins will ignore your lady. The moment they enter they make a beeline towards you over the circling, bowl-like steps of the tall chamber.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 3
text:
The key to this fight is keeping distance. Fortunately, the six priestesses wil stay on the balcony. Their screams rings in your ears through the battle, but does no immediate harm.

Start by going straight for the golem which entered through the nearest door, the one with the bat-face. Attack with your Steel. Use your Shield to block.

Once the other one closes in, run. You can't fight both at once.

The way to split them up is by clanking down and up the bow-steps, through a diagonal of the room. One or both of the golems will frequently stumble and fall when they follow you. This is the only way to make space between them.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 4
text:
There's no secret to destroying The Painted Twins. It's a battle of division and attrition. Block their blows and keep whittling at the wax.

When the last golem crumbles, the priestess will fall silent.

Walk to the easel. Pour the Unhue over your Lady's portrait. The face will fade, and her own color will return. Raise her on your shoulder, carry her to the garden, and leave.

And pray. Pray the goddess never saw your face or heard your name.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 5
text: *In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 1
text:
The thicket wriggles and squeezes. From the left. From the right. Above. Under your sabatons, the bark of the archtree buckles and squeaks.

Pick your path up the sloping trunk:

**Right:** The slope, ever climbing in buckles gnarls, rises gradually like a great cragland of wooden hills and wooden cliffs. Branches, like trees in their own right, burst up from the rugged terrain like volcanic mushrooms. There is no easy path up the slope; the tunnels through the thicket shrink and shirnk

**Left:** Your branch opens onto a wide, flat ledge. The light looks brighter this way. A place for your Blue Lady to catch her breath?

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 2
text:
On the wide branche-cliff of the mountain-tree, the old wood swells away like the sea. Far below the Soulwood foams and flows in slow but heavy winds.

You see white fog rising, in that lower wood.

The canopy of your archtree spreads high over you. It darkens the sky, you cannot see the sun. It is as if you like under some vast sweep of stormclouds. But there is no rain. Only that strangely pale fog. It moves fast. You see it touch near the base of your archtree, then climb. It rises up the bearded sides like fast-growing ivy, spiraling in tendrils and helixes. Reaching. Scaling.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 3
text:
Your Lady turns her face and coughs into the shoulder of her furred cloak.

"It's the same mist as I saw in the Soulwood.

"We haven't left its forest yet.

"Even so high, I taste it's sting in my throat.

"We should hurry."

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 4
text:
You'll want to return to the crossroads and take the Right path next - this wooden ledge tapers into a vertical wall of trunk.

The thicket wriggles and squeezes. From the left. From the right. Above. 

If you haven't drawn your sword yet, you'll have a hard time with the climb ahead. The narrowing tunnels up the rugged wooden slope are packed with thorny limbs. Your armor protects you, but your Lady's cloak and dress offer no defense. You can use your Pinecone Crest Shield to hold back the pressing, branches where they cut off a tunnel, allowing your lady to climb through. The wood creaks and groans against you.

At some point the limbs turn so thick and tangled that you'll have no choice. Draw your Steel. Start hacking. Each cut summons a shriek of protest from the living cloudpiercer of wood, but you have no choice.

The thicket wriggles and squeezes. From the left. From the right. Above. 

*It seems of no use. These branches grow too thick for any edge. Where has the light gone?*

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 5
text:
In the dark, you hear your Lady's coughing voice.

"It is here with us, my Knight.

"I can't see that white mist.

"Hurry.

"I feel it licking at my heels. I hear it hissing up around my waist.

"Oh, hurry. Please."

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 6
text:
Swing. Catch. Yank. Cleave. Thud. Thud. Thud. Thud.

With one last shriek of splitting timber, the heaviest of the branches before splinters apart. Like the heavy central support pillar of a temple, bring down the roof as it crumbles, the branches writhe apart as the timber falls. Down a single hole obve you at an angle, a lone spear of the sun crashes. It shines on a pool of fog around your waists.

Waste no time. Sheath your sword. Take your Lady's arm. Pull her up the tunnel.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Blob 1
text:
Warm light gleams against your helmet's crest. You pull yourself from the black, knotted hole in the tree canopy.

Standing atop the ancient oak, you find the wide leaves and woven branches bear your weight. Springy, but stable. You pull your lady up beside you.

You stand above the clouds. Beneath the drifting sheep, far away and far below your place in the sky, the sprawling Soulwood lies still and mistless. The yellow sun paints everything up here in gold. The mist has not followed you. It cannot live in the sun.

Other great trees spread behind you, eastward. Dozens. They sway, more than any normal trees, and twine their beam-like branches with one-another.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Blob 2
text:
The Ents are the oldest trees left in The Time of Dying. In the songs of men, they speak. They communicate with the other ents directly, through the long wooden fingers of their branches. Rarely, a smaller beast or plant is blessed with an ear for their language, and may understand what they want through the creaking and groaning of their limbs.

The Ents dwell only in the remotest parts of the great Soulwood. Up on the forested mountain peaks. Or, hidden in ravines that run so deeply into the earth, their canopies lie lower even than that of the regular trees. They sway, but do not move from the place of their roots.

You have heard, in The Third Song of Splywater, that Ents dislike all men who touch their bark. This one, though it gave you an arduous climbs, seems not to have held a grudge.

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Blob 3
text:
Your Lady raises a hand to shield her eyes from the sun, and looks east.

"Summer. But these old growths wear their autumn gold and rust.

"Look. See that one beside us?\
"Why are its leaves like stone?

"Drooping. Grey.

"But I am glad to have come out of that dark growth.

"I'm glad we see this, my knight."

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Blob 4
text:
Your Lady's words give you a moment of thought.

*Was it wrong of you to cut your way up to this place?*

*You held back your Steel for as long as you were able. But that matters nothing, now. In the end your bure your cutting edge against this old, living thing. Surely, though you are far from The Road, this is a sacred place. To have chopped one branch is to have sinned against a thing older than yourself.*

*But your oath to your Lady - that is more important.*

*Her words are happier ones now. Here, under the sun. Would that you could remove your helm, and feel it on your face.*

*Her words are happier... But it seems that her speech, her voice, her words... They are still not the same.*

*Would that you had never been to Middlemoss.*

name: 2 - Rest (Venus) - Blob 5
text:
You may choose to stand with your Lady for many hours, on that canopy of the ancient Ent. In this place, you will not be attacked by monsters of the woods, or by your enemies. The great tree will sway underneath you, perhaps more than any normal tree would. It's movements are the movements of the heavy beasts, however - the movements of the whales and the elephants, and of the great canvases of wing on ten-thousand year old owls. They are slow undulations. They will not shake you from your place, nor parts the foliage beneath you.

This is a peaceful stop.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Blob 1
text:
As dusk spreads, the Ent quakes and trembles. The verdure underfoot moves restlessly.

Your time of rest has ended. You'll want to get off the top of this tree before the fifth stars blinks into place in the sky above. Two possible routes lie open to you.

**Right:** One long branch swings out in wild arcs. Down and sideways. Up and sideways. Whenever this horizontal pendulum reaches the end of its arc, it draws near the bristling, swinging limb of a nearby juniper-Ent. If you take this path, and make the jump to the opposite tree, you will find the nest of a golden crow amidst the immediate bristles. Taking one egg from the nest will bring you ill-luck. If you take two instead, they will later hatch, and be your faithful followers.

**Left:** One branch of your towering oak wriggles back and forth like the tail of a snake. At its end, it almost touches the still branch of the strange, grey-leafed tree you noticed before.

name: 3 - Capers (Mercury) - Blob 2
text:
You pull your Lady, rushing for the Ent that is mottled grey. A pine. The grey-shapes are not leaves, you now see, but calcified pinecones, with some of their grey bleeding into the wood.

The oak-limb bucks underfoot. You feel as if the thing is trying to throw you from it's top, down down down to the forest below. Each step is a stumble, like walking over a suspension bridge in a castle-city during an earthquake.

Your lady's fingers slip from your gauntlet. You turn. You see her slide down the curving, writhing side of your branch. She reaches for a knob, she misses.

You throw yourself. Your armor clangs on wood. Jarring teeth. Reaching, you wrap your fingers around her forearm just in time. With your shield, you hook a limb.

You climb back. Run. A gap - you leap it. Your lady leaps; you catch her. You stand, at last, on stable footing.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Blob 1
text:
The branch of this pane is like stone under your step. No motion. No softness. Tapping. It feels as if you are stepping down the nave of a vaulting, silent cathedral.

The branches quickly weave together around you. They form a wide tunnel. The weave is tight, but not solid. It allows some of the purple dusk light to filter through. Even with its gaps, yours and your lady's footsteps still beat a muted echo.

the tunnel smells of old pine, like timber in a house.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Blob 2
text:
"My knight, this tree is-

"How has it died, when all the rest of these old growths thrive?

"It must be below. In the roots.

"Yet... it's voice is tranquil.

"Serene.

"I knew a woman who spoke with such a voice once.

"A Sister of the Yellow Smile.

"She too stood at peace."

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Blob 3
text:
**The Grove Within a Grove:**

A place of peace and moonlight waits in the center of the elder pine. The trunk is wide, open, and hollow. One lone, inward-growing branch twists up through the flat floor of creamy-yellow rings.

The sky is open above, with the rays of the silver moonlight slanting down to gild the twisted root. Your Lady's hair shines like ice reflected in onyx as she steps into the light.

On one side of the hollow space the outer wall has crumbled away. You step near the edge. You glance over. This dead tree stands at the edge of the Ent growth. High trees sway to your right and your left, but the forest that stretches away from you is neither the towering growth of Ents, nor the deepness of the Soulwood. It is an ordinary forest.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Blob 4
text:
Something rustles behind you. Instantly your fingers are wrapping the hilt of your Polished Steel Longsword, but a familiar voice relaxes your hand.

"Where, oh where, hides the font of my light?

"Where, oh where, can it be?

"My day's rather long, neverending, my song.

"Here I've come, over roads, over sea.

"But my star, like a thief, still evades me,

"Oer these hours stretching forever on.

"Broken bones, broken dreams, hopeless heart,

"My star is gone."

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Blob 5
text:
This is your last chance to alter the fate of this Living Bones, the Late Poet. His green cap droops. His voice is melancholy. Your thoughts determine his end.

**He has wasted his time:** The Late Poet realizes there are no stars on The Road of Graves. Later, you find his bones in a defiled coffin, near Alsoburg. They appear to have been buried there for centuries.

**Remember your lord:** The Late Poet will compose a song about your fallen lord. The song wins him fame. He wanders The Road, singing it between the towns, long after you are gone.

**Remember your Lady:** The Late Poet will compose a song about your Blue Lady.

name: 4 - Prestige (Jupiter) - Blob 6
text:
You notice then that your Lady has not moved from the ledge.

"Look.

"The white mist has rolled back from the woods.

"I see the silver-gilt tops of the trees.

"This hollow-

"But we have crossed that wide ravine now, on the branches of these towers.

"Let us descend."

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Blob 1
text:
Nothing you do now matters.

\#

There is a noise. At first you mistake it for the reverberation of your lady's feet on the floor of the narrow, winding tunnel down which you walk. But it is too loud for footsteps. It sounds like the ticking of a clock buried under earth.

The tunnel widens.
