Small trees, *within* this great hollow tree, also dead, shoot up on your left and right. The skeletons of their branches brush your pauldrons.

*How have they grown without sunlight?*

The blighting grey washes more and more of the color from the wood the deeper you go. Many are the afflictions which might strangle an oak or pine in such a way.

**Petrification**

**Red King's Curse**

**Stone Curse**

**Slumber**

**Wilting Disease**

**Millipedes**

**Curse of Soft Roots**

**Pulping**

**Shakes**

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Blob 2
text:
Something catches your thinking.

*Why are your hands backwards? No, no this is no trick of the light. Look at them here, against this crack in the curving vein. Look at how the moon in shining through them.*

*Your hands, indeed, are bent wrong.*

*Stranger still. They seem to twist and crack back into shape. Painlessly. But there are smoky splotches now where your fingernails once grew.*

*This is a punishment.*

While you have stood at the crack in the wall, examining your hands, your lady has said nothing. She stopped, and stood without looking at you, waiting only to continue walking down.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Blob 3
text:
Tick.

Tick.

Tick.

Tick.

Ticktick.

Ticktick.

Tick.

Ticktick.

Tickthump.

Thumpthump.

Thump.

Thump.

Thumpthump.

Thump.

THUMP.

THUMP.

THUMPTHUMPTHUMPTHUMPTHUMPTHUMPTHUMPTHUMP.

THUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Blob 4
text:
**Dread:**

An inexporable, choking sensation. Your throat is full of it. Your lady keeps walking - forward, curving, down - so you follow. But you cannot breath.

One minute...

Two minutes...

As you pass under one low-hanging, red, stringy root, your breath returns.

**The Red Root:**

It seems to your eye as you gasp for air, that there is a friendly air to the strange red root. A familiar face. Your lady keeps walking - forward, curving, down - so you follow. But you look back at the root once. It seems so familiar - if only you had been able to study it...

name: 5 - Atrophy (Saturn) - Blob 5
text:
Descending deeper and deeper and deeper, the tunnel constricts once more. The cracks in the exterior are few this deep within the vein of the great tree. The silver moonlight is gone. You must rely upon your Cat's Eye Spear for light. The wooden walls are dry and splintered.

This texture changes. Your spear gleams a blue streak along the tunnel's surface. Resin-amber runs like ore veins through the wood.

The thumping drums through the steel of your helmet, and rings in the bones of your ears. If The Late Poet was still singing when you left him, his voice slowly fades out of the range of your hearing. Then the voice is gone. You are left with only the swelling thumps.

THUMP.

THUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

THUMPTHUMP.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Blob 1
text:
**The Four-Pronged Heart:**

In a tetrahedron-shaped room in the center of the Ent lies its heart. Erratically beating, it pounds inside your skull as step into the vaulted chamber. Veiny roots loop through floor and wall. Every seventh root undulates with sappy red light. The room is tangled like a mangrove.

At the top of the dome you see suspended the giant head of a stag. It's long pink tongue lolls out between flat yellow teeth.

As you spearlight shines in the space, one shadow moves obliquely. What is this shape you see? Too big for bug, too chitinous for man. It moves with a pen-on-parchment sound. The left side of where his face should be is dominated by one large, rectangular eye.

Your light awakens the stag-heart. It lows at the apex of the ceiling. It's opens one eye, which it turns on you like a yellow spotlight.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Blob 2
text:
The Four-Pronged Heart begins by closing off all three entrances to the room with walls of roots. Then it will fix it's beaming eye upon you.

The corrupted right eye of this Ent will petrify you if you stand in it's light for too many moments. You can delay the onset of petrification by ducking under the huge looping roots, but the Heart controls the roots and will retract them one-by-one.

However, you have a way to divert the eye beam. By taking The Left Eye of an Ent which you found in The Emerald House, and by holding it aloft, you can turn aside the gaze of the elk-head.

Doing so comes at a price. You will draw the attention of the chitinous-man, with the other corrupted heart-eye on his head. He cannot be slain, his whole body apart from his hands is covered in chitin. He will try to steal your eye.

Who is this figure dwelling in the dead Ent heart?

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Blob 3
text:
**The Thief of the House of Maze:**

A once-man. The Eye of an Ent is a valuable prize. He tried to steal this one's eye for The House. He forgot to bring a clotting slave for the wound, and so the heart, in vengeance, trapped and consumed him. Whatever was human in him is gone. He has become the heart-mite, the insect-servant of the Ent's thrumming organ.

name: 6 - Spellcasting (Uranus) - Blob 4
text:
To win, you must cut the thief-gauntlets from the hands of servant. With your Steel, lop them as he reaches for your protecting eye. Done the gauntlets while he is writhing on the ground. Then, using the roots, climb to the apex of the heart-chamber, and plug the stag's other eye.

The heart screams. A mix of dying stag and the ripping limb of a tree. The chamber throbs irregularly.

Then the thrumming stops. The tree shudders, and crumbles.

You and your Lady fall in a ruin of sawdust.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Blob 1
text:
Soft...

Dry...

Cold...

You awaken. You are on the floor of a forest, in a massive pile of sawdust. Your lady's lies beside you, stirring just as you rise.

All about, this forest is quiet, though not silent. Grasshoppers churr under a rooted stone a few yards ahead. In an oak to your right a cuckoo presents its two-note song. The forest is eerily ordinary. Nowhere do you see the towering Ents, nor the deep and writhing tangle of the Soulwood. All that remains in the huge sawdust pile.

As your Lady rises, she brushes the dust from her hair and cloak. Then she dusts your armor.

\#

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Blob 2
text:
The air blows freely through the trunks as you walk, mustless and brisk.

You will meet with a trio of sap miners. They are painting rings of lambent gold color around the shaggy trunk of a hemlock. The paint is made of glowing moss. If you touch it the moss will adhere to your gauntlet.

The miners are curiously unaware of your presence. Perhaps it is their heavy, bubble-like, pewter platemail suits. You can attract the attention of any one of the trio by tapping their globe-helmet. The miner will still refuse to speak, but will point with a thick leather glove in a direction through the trees.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Blob 3
text:
*Why does she say nothing, nor look upon you? In that corrupted archtree your Lady seemed to turn inward.*

*She took shelter while you fought that stag with the gleaming eye. And, the insect-man. The stag never looked upon her. The man never chased her. That she was safe - for that you should be satisfied. But it was as if neither of those monsters saw her. As if she were dead, or the tree was a figment.*

*She has of late been much changed. Her words...*

*Not for you to question. For you, to serve.*

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Blob 4
text:
You emerge from between the trunks. A coarse and rocky tundra stretches away and away and further away. Far in the distance, white teeth. Mountains.

The cold open space tears your breath out in a mist through the visor of your helmet. The sky seems almost dizzily high - as if you stood in a holy cathedral for giants, with a painted ceiling.

The thin woods behind you feel like the last ragged edge of the age. Until you spot it. The Road. It is almost invisible against the washed-out, grey-brown grass, but you spy a thin line of paving stones winding through the land. And each stone has its epithet.

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Blob 5
text:
Your Lady speaks into the wind.

"I wonder...

"Did that tall tree outlive its seeds?

"Its line, perhaps, ended in that dust pile.

"Thank you, my knight.

"I felt that tree's ill health while we walked among its sickly branches.

"A fever.

"I seemed to see my pale daughter again. Or, her ghost.

"Come, my knight. Let us go on."

name: 7 - Mystery/Resolution (Neptune) - Blob 6
text: *In The Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.*

name: 1 - Prelude - Blob 1
text:
**The Cocoon:** A symbol of love on The Road of Graves, in The Time of Dying.

Three thousand years ago, this pasture shows a different aspect. Before the graves and the dying, the land is ruled by an uncounted number of kings - masters of a countless demon throng. Blood mattes the dust of this field, and paints the faces of those far mountains. Men do not live.

Then; necromancy. Despair so fills the bones and the skins and the souls of the dying, that they do not die, but rise to hate again and again.

"I shall come to the finger of my sire and my brother and my neighbor.

"I, a ghost."

name: 1 - Prelude - Blob 2
text:
Seven god-necromancers were the first and greatest masters.

**The Thin Man:** Who from his cradle, brought wasting sickness down upon the demon.

**The Old Man Below:** Who with a smiling whisper, made their hearts grow timid.

**The Scratcher:** Who dealt in curses.

**The Mother of Worms:** Who, naked, looked on the hoard - and all their bones did rot.

**Gallbladder:** Who summoned the hungry among the dead.

**Lady Horsehair:** Who bought her power.

**The Red King:** Who, with red conscience and nails, tore the throats from his peers.

Seven god-necromancers were the ones who left ruins of stone where once had been the palaces of the demon kings.

name: 1 - Prelude - Blob 3
text:
On this pasture, now, there is only one lonely hill. Smoke pours constantly from a hot spring beneath it.

The demon palace - of the demon king who once matted this plain - is gone from the hill. Only the bones of the great king himself remain. They have grown for three millenniums after his death. The giant skeleton sits, half buried in the hill, leaning against it like a stump, with one tower demon-horn rising up over the plain like a black spire.

name: 1 - Prelude - Blob 4
text:
This pasture is barren now. Little life dwells here. Yet, in memory of its old mamsters, it has a habit of hallucinations. Dangerous, hateful visions. False visions.

False desires.

This figments manifest during the frequent snowstorms that blow over the region. The snow masses into huge hummocks, incongruously on the flat plain. It white-blinds and deceives the eye of any travelling pair misfortunate enough to wander over the ancient soil...

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Blob 1
text:
"White like milk spilled on a porcelain dish.

"Somehow, though, almost t-t-translucent.

"Hear how my teeth chatter.

"I feel almost as if I see her face again; her pale face amidst all this blowing white snow.

"My daughter...

"Oh my knight, how glad I am that you are here. Beside me.

"Together..."

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Blob 2
text:
*This is a rare pleasure. To guide the lady to whom you are sworn, through this ice storm, is a chance to fulfill your oath. Visions: she seems ensorcelled by them today. YOU know the things she sees are not there. You are given the chance to be strong, for both of you, despite your cracked cup body.*

*So why do you hesitate to take her hand?*

*Hark.  There is speech in the wind. Doubtless it is another strange illusion. You should ignore it, yet there is something so familiar in the deep voice...*

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Blob 3
text:
If you listen to the voice for too long, a wasting sickness will sink into your skin.

**Sage Chutes:** These tiny fungal growths rise from your pores in long tendrils that burn like molten lead against your skin.

The hallucinations are more dangerous to your Blue Lady than to you. Most of the sensory phenomenon, you only see out of the corner of your visor, or catch at hearing's bring.

The exception is a pair of separate sensations, manifesting simultaneously.

**Approach the Vision of Your Old Lord:** You lead your Lady deeper into the blinding, blowing whiteness. She cannot see your Lord, and it seems you never come closer, no matter how quickly you march. *Crunch. Crunch. Crunch*. Your sabatons crush a long path in the snow. Eventually, the vision disappears. You will die in the cold.

**Follow the Smell of Old Wet Stones:** The snow seems pockmarked in places. You skirt wide of these depressions in the plain.

\#

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Blob 4
text:
Wandering, you may come across one enormous drift of snow. There is a vacant cavity in the side of the drift. Hesitating, you reach out and feel around inside. It is not an illusion, but there is nothing within to account for the strange hole. It seems as if the snow has piled around nothing.

The key is to close your eyes, then climb inside the drifting. You will find yourself in a long, warm, humid tunnel. It smell like the breath of a cow. If you crawl a little along this passage, eyes-closed, you will find The Demon King's Claymore.

**The Demon King's Claymore:** This weapon can still even the strongest of the sleepless dead. It is, of course, deeply cursed.

name: 2 - Opening Conflict - Blob 5
text:
As the snow continues to whirl about you, you just catch your Lady's tremulous voice.

"Why does each of them have her face?

"Those marching girls, they cannot all be her.

"It is s-s-so c-c-cold.

"How I wish we had brought the blankets through The Soulwood.

"Something to swaddle us, my knight. You and I.

"A cocoon.

"But, look. Are those the shadows of some giant bones?"

name: 3 - Mentor - Blob 1
text:
*CRUNCH.*

*CRUNCH.*

*CRUNCH.*

Not your footsteps, but heavier ones, sound somewhere ahead in the blinding white sheets. You keep your Lady's cold arm in the grip of your shield-hand, and draw your Polished Steel Longsword.

The blowing snow breaks in one open space. It is as if some giant, invisible squats in the space. The empty air floats adjacent to a high hill. You can barely discern a pair of arcing, black rib-bones, gigantic as trees, looping like stitches in the snow on the hill.

name: 3 - Mentor - Blob 2
text:
A voice vibrates in the air.

"Hummmmmm-the king surely never dreamed how large his remains would loom. Not in those last hours.

"He was handsome too, before all his blood was spilled. Beneath this snow.

"Now look at him.

"Flesh, gone.

"Even the beloved worms have nothing to taste here.

"Just buried bones hugging round the ruins of his home."

name: 4 - First Transition - Blob 1
text:
A moment later a solid blackness surface sprawls against the hill. Not a bone, though it lies under the toothed arch of a cracked jaw - it is a cave.

The cold is sharp on your neck. It seems to cut through your armor. Your Blue Lady still stands, but her face and hands now match her name.

As you approach the cave, you will see one of four hallucinations guarding the entrance.

**The Grandmother Snake:** It's face is old and human. Its illusory poison can paralyze.

**Sittingsmoking:** A harmless woodsman sits and puffs on his pipe.

**Ruddy Living Bones:** A living bones covered in viscera.

**The Spider:** Take care with the webs this one spins.

If you do not trust these, they pose no threat; your Steel evaporates each in a single cut.

name: 5 - New Lover - Blob 1
text:
An odor of sulfur breathes from the mouth of the cave. Snowflakes melt as they pass before the opening. You must climb a tract of ice to reach the cave.

Once, this space beneath the ground were the dungeon's of the demon king's keep. The upper keep is gone now. All that remains of the fortress are a few walls of loose stone and mortar, moldering in the fumes, beyond the black opening.

Still some of the demon blood must ferment beneath the ground. The fumes come from a hot spring, somewhere deep in the cavern. It is said, in the stories of the barren plain, that a lone warm grotto, surrounded by fangs of ice and frozen darkness, might give a traveler shelter from the storm.

If they pass the visions.

name: 5 - New Lover - Blob 2
text:
*How she clings to you... You must stop her - your armor is frozen from that storm, and her hands are already blue. There. She has understood what your waving hand suggests. She looks so Blue there on the ground, blending with the ice.*

*This reek has mired her mind, surely. Her affections are brought on by cold and poisonous fumes. You must be strong for the both of you. Remember your vows.*

*And you must find a fire, fast. Pray that the reeking gases of this freezing cavern do not-*

*What? That is no illusion.*

name: 5 - New Lover - Blob 3
text:
A man in threadbare grey furs hobbles out of the fumigated deepness of the tunnel. The winter light of the entrance barely clarifies a poxmarked, scarred, hairy face. Sewn to his collar is a worn symbol, which you nevertheless recognize. A Walker of Whiteeye.

"Hahahaha! Looking for shelter from the storm, eh?

"Bad pick.

"I've been wandering this cave all my life, or, has it only been some days?

"One loses track of time.

"Its the mutants, you see. The beasts of whiteye. They live here too, even though I thought only the Waste housed horrors like I've seen.

"But, what am I saying. Think you can fool me? Fool me?!"

name: 5 - New Lover - Blob 4
text:
The mad Walker of Whiteeye will pull a long, sturdy iron needle from within his rags. He attacks.

This fight is not too difficult. The mad Walker strikes with the speed of crackling glass, but his body is rotten with starvation. Leap back from his initial lunge. Keep yourself between him and your lady - he WILL attack her if you give him the chance, and his needle inflicts suprisingly-grevious wounds. Don't bother with ripostes or parries. Use your Pinecone Crest Shield her to block his quick stabs. He will wear himself out in only a few minutes. You can then either kill him or knock him unconscious - it makes no difference in The Time of Dying.

name: 5 - New Lover - Blob 5
text:
As the walker's body falls still, you hear the chatter of your Lady's teeth.

"Oh, my knight.

"Ever and across this r-r-road, since we left the r-r-ruin of my home, you have k-k-kept me s-s-safe.

"Are you not cold yourself?

"Come to me, let us warm one another.

"I can ignore these phantoms for a time if I fill my eyes with you.

"I could forget them. My-

"Even my lord..."

name: 6 - Second Transition - Blob 1
text:
On the icicle pillars that line this corridor of the cave, you kindled fire finally reflects. It rises like burning cathedrals in the icy glass. You pull your lady near it. After a moment, she stops shaking.

You think it is best not to leave the bloody body of the madman so near. You haven't dragged him a hundred feet deeper into the tunnel when you feel something soft underfoot. You bend down.

A carpet. Woven silk. Colored too, it shows a river winding through a field of chrysanthemums.

You hear a shout. Look up. See your Lady, running. Fleeing.

You run after.

\#

name: 7 - Wilderness - Blob 1
text:
For some moments you follow the echoes of your lady's footsteps. The light from you tiny fire seems to rebound ahead of you, dimly reflecting on the frozen cave walls.

Soon, however, the echoes writhe and crisscross, and other echoes mingle with your Lady's soft feet. You are soon hopelessly lost.

As you wander, you may enter several unique chambers.

**The Old Wall Room:** Some sort of paper or thin fabric fills the cracks between the crumbling brick walls. If you chip away some of the ice coating the walls, and pull on a piece of paper, it whispers in an unknown tongue.

**The Glowbug Hive:** There are small bubbles in the ice-walls of this room. Glow bugs shine dimly from each bubble, frozen in light.

**Emerald's:** Your sabatons crunch over fragmented ice and glass in this long corridor. If you take the time to sift through the crystalline gravel, you can find an emerald with a soul trapped inside.

**The Casting Chambers:** A cauldron for pouring metal hangs over your head, from two chains in the ceiling. Something sputters inside.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Blob 2
text:
You'll have to fight your way through a variety of hallucinations as you search the frozen ruins and frozen caves in search of your Lady. The hallucinations have different strengths and weaknesses.

**Laminated Goblin Knight:** Attacks with spear. Destroyed by cracking the ice in which he scintillates.

**Millingmist:** Grates away your armor and skin. Breath deeply, and realize it is just cold air.

**Voidingmist:** Suffocates. Close your eyes and imagine a wide, sunny space; it will vanish.

**The Knife Mother:** Stabs you. Stab her first, and she vanishes.

**Unshadowed Wraith:** A black ghost which surfaces in any source of light. Drains your life. Cannot survive in the dark

**Demonheads:** Disembodied deadheads with wing-ears. Their screams will deafen you. Plug your ears with whispering cloth from The Old Wall Room.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Blob 3
text:
Your Lady huddles in the transiept of a small chapel. Strong granite walls, and a red-stained skyglass in the configuration of a rose - the illusion seems so vivid and real.

Of course you know the walls. Your Lady knows them too. She looks up and around with eyes slicked. She doesn't see you, her gaze is on the the three rows of stepped oak pews, the red reflection of the rose on the dust of the floor, the shadows under the alchitraves.

You both recognize home.

Then a white-faced ghost catches both your gazes.

name: 7 - Wilderness - Blob 4
text:
"MOTHER, WHY SO MANY TEARS? NOT FOR YOUR DAUGHTER CAN THEY BE.

"NOT FOR YOUR DAUGHTER, SHUNNED AND SPURNED IN LIFE, CAN YOU WEEP. FOR HER, YOU CARED NOTHING.

"ALL YOUR LOVE EVER FELL FOR THOSE GOLDEN SONS AND PERFECT FATHER.

"WHERE ARE THEY NOW?

"GHOSTS.

"I SUMMONED THE WEIGE FAMILY.

"LET THEM IN.

"RUINED OUR HOUSE.

"ALL, FOR YOU."

name: 7 - Wilderness - Blob 5
text:
You should stop this ghost. Draw your Polished Steel and cut her. Destroy this figment and stop her torment of your Lady.

As the spectre evaporates, pull your lady up, between the pews, through the door on your left.

name: 8 - Third Transition - Blob 1
text:
"My knight, stop! I can see only the horrible dead in this dark cave.

"I thought, for a moment, that we had stood in our keep.

"Oh my knight, how could you cut her down? My poor daughter.

"I cannot understand why she spoke at me thus. Love her less than my sons? How could I?

"Her ghost says her body let the killers through our strong castle door...

"...

"What is that squeaking underfoot?"

name: 8 - Third Transition - Blob 2
text:
A light that is the color of the ocean rises around your legs. Your Lady is limned in blue. You find that you and she have wandered into a rough rock tunnel. The floor, is a carpet of mushroom, which brighten at your step.

The light shines in your Lady's eye as she looks your way. You see it shine further down the tunnel too. A reflection. The air seems to drift warmly from that direction. You set a hand on your Lady's shoulder, and lead her along.

Your Lady lets you guide her. She is silent now. You feel her eyes upon you. You try not to meet her eye, but you cannot help feeling the warmth of her fingers as they find your arm.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Blob 1
text:
The blue jumps across the carpet before your feet. From fungus to fungus it leaps. Then all at once it spreads scintillatingly out in a fairie ring.

A pool. The mushroom carpet rounds a small pond, here in the depths of the cave. The water sputters and bubble - not like a cauldron, but with small staticky bubbles like a hot spring.

Stringy, shimmering, webby fungi glisten with condensation across the walls and ceiling, dangling like strands, dripping with soft *plink, plink, plink* sounds back into the bubbles. The moisture in the air clings, warm and damp, beneath your armor.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Blob 2
text:
Your Lady steps toward the pool. She looks at her reflection in the ripples at it's edge. Then, she turns to you and stares.

"How warm and comfortable this place is. Like a cocoon in the middle of the ice and the rocks.

"I have almost forgotten the storm that drove us into this place.

"Forgotten the visions... my daughter... sons... lord...

"Come closer to the pool, My knight. It is warmer this way.

"We have walked the road together long enough, you and I. You need not be my servant.

"Come. Remove your helmet and your gauntlets.

"I will hold your hands."

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Blob 3
text:
*You cannot take those two white hands of hers in your own. You know what is happening. She is settling; for you. For you, her cracked and broken defender. Certainly, no woman should love a weapon as she think she loves you.*

*It is these illusions. It is that daughter's ghost in her mind's corner. She no longer believes she is worthy of finding her son.*

*Push her back. You know it is right. Would you break your sacred vows? Would you violate the tenets of The Road, The Time, The Seven to which you are sworn?*

*Think of that killer, R.*

*Would you follow his path?*

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Blob 4
text:
In many ways this next moment will be your hardest battle thus far - greater than any living death you felled by Polished Steel.

You must resist the warm touch of your Blue Lady.

Your only course is to push her back. Refuse her advance. No matter what you choose to do, you will gain no surety of the reasons for her sudden longing. If you choose to submit to her, know that you will sever the last thread of hope for your cracked soul. And hers. From then on, you live a doomed journey.

Now is the moment to follow your oath, sworn over the Graves.

name: 9 - Mysterious Master Foe - Blob 5
text:
As she finally steps back from you, your lady places her warm hands over her eyes. For a moment, her voice is strangled in sobs.

"Turn away. Look back toward that opening we came through.

"Turn away. I do not want your eyes me, knight.

"Perform your service, then, if that is all you are. A servant.

"I am tired now. I'll sleep beside this pool.

"I see now that I will find no comfort in waking."

name: 10 - Fourth Transition - Blob 1
text:
For a long time your Lady stands at the lip of the hissing spring. She watches the faintly rippling glass of the water's edge, limned in the twilight blue of the mushrooms.

After a moment, a face appears on the surface. Both you and  your Lady see the face. It is the face of your Lady's daughter. The daughter opens her mouth and smiles. Her teeth are black, then blood reddens them as her mouth floods with it.

Your Lady passes a hand over her eyes. Then she steps back from the pool, throws out her green cloak over the mushrooms, and lies down to sleep.

You watch as the changeless hours pass in the mushroom grotto. You need little sleep these days. The steam of the pool whirls around you. It seems sometimes to you that, in the blue glow, the mist takes the shape of a hundred eels. They wriggles in the air all around you as the hours slip.

As the hours slip, the pool ceases to hiss. The water falls still. The air within the cave deadens to a chill like the air without.

Your Lady wakes.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Blob 1
text:
To escape The Stone Cocoon, you'll need to navigate back through the ruins and tunnels. The visions will harass you no further - a new danger has replaced them.

Unless your very lucky in the corners you round, you're eventually going to come up against one of the new Ice-Walls. A semi-transparent wall of fast-frozen vapor, this wall is nonetheless strong. Your Steel shall not crack it. You shall have to find another way.

Your escape from the tunnels is a battle against time. Each minute that passes, a new wall of ice forms in a random location. You'll need to move quickly to escape. IF you don't, you'll be trapped inside the tunnels, where the hallucinations will eventually drive you made. As you run, keep an eye open for depe, black cracks in the floor. (It is a small wonder you never stumbled into these during your entry).

The most efficient sequence of turns to escape the tunnels is: left, left, straight, left, straight, right, right, straight, up, left, exit.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Blob 2
text:
*There is the daylight. You have led your Lady from this darkness.*

*Did you act rightly?*

*She will not meet your eye. Her own seem flat and dull as you spy them through this heavy helmet.*

*Perhaps it had been better to forgo your sacred vow; welcome her embrace. You have broken other vows in your travels.*

*Are no tenets to be upheld?*

*Is your soul to fill with cracks like your body?*

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Blob 3
text:
You emerge from the freezing humidity of the cave. The fresh wind tastes sharp and clear beneath your helmet. All about, the land is a heavy, flat blanket of white snow. The sky is cloudy, but the storm is gone.

As you step out from under the jaw-roof of the cave, you see better the black bones of the demon king. They sick partway from tthe snow-covered hill. The sockets of the skull are empty, beneath the lone horn, yet you think you spy an amused gleam in the hollow blackness therein.

A small pack of spider-hounds has left tracks by the cave mouth. They are old tracks. The hounds never entered the cave itself (animals smell the evil in the place).

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Blob 4
text:
"Oh knight, my behavior in that place hurts me like a needle through my chest. I only thank the Mother of Worms that you held righteous in the face of it.

"I recall a glass orchid my lord once gave to me. I did not bring it with us. Shame. I would like to look upon it now...

"But all that is lost. All because of my daughter. All for my failure to love as a mother must.

"What is there to hope for from the stepson I drove away?"

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Blob 5
text:
No matter which direction you take after leaving The Stone Cocoon, you will eventually come to a small shrine in the midst of the tundra. It is made of piled straw, held together by dried mud. The villages of the plains keep it. Embedded among the straw, you will find bits of paper. Each bears the name of a village nearby where you might go for shelter.

Pick a town. Start walking in that direction. You will eventually return to the Road.

name: 11 - Future and Finale - Blob 6
text: In The Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.

name: Setup - Blob 1
text:
The dew on the grassy moor serves as a bristling mirror to the face of the midnight sky. The moon looks down at its hircine reflection on the the flat expanse. It's face is poxy, perfectly-round, and bright, brilliant yellow. A Harridan's Moon.

Distantly, a spider-wolf howls.

As you pull her across the moor, your Lady's foot slips in the dewy grass. She collapses to her knees. Her face is bent and pale. You waste no time; you take her up in your arms and carry her. She is light in your arms. As you run, you take her hand in your yours. Her fingers are wet from the dew, and freezing.

name: Setup - Blob 2
text:
"My knight, charging onto the moor is the hope of a blind man searching for a door back from death.

"Stop- stop tiring yourself. I am not worth carrying.

"Ah! This venom. Like tiny slivers of ice, sliding along my arms, slipping under each of my fingers.

"You have served as faithfully as any retainer, my knight. Rest now. The service is finished."

name: Setup - Blob 3
text:
*You saw the insect that bit your Lady's skin. A Nickel Beetle. You know what she says is true. Why do you plod through this moor in the night? You know she will die.*

*You plod because it is your duty. Serve to your end. Not hers.*

*You knew there was no hope on The Road. What chance had you of coming upon any stranger, in that desolate stretch? Few men, armed and armored, make the long journey from Middlemoss; which you have your Lady on. No, you would have met no stranger on the road.*

*But is there any more help to be found in this wild?*

*You should have shaken her blanket. If she dies...*

*The Nickel Beetle. The same insect killed that Walker of Whiteeye. Wrathmaige.*

*But he died. Your Lady must live.*

*Serve to your end.*

name: Setup - Blob 4
text:
Having come this far onto the moor, under the Harridan's Moon, it is useless to turn back. Your Lady might reach the road alive, but there is a 0% chance that you will encounter another wanderer before she expires. (And a less than 0% chance that said wanderer happened to carry some cure for the venom that is killing her.)

Your only hope lies on the wild, lawless moor.

As you carry your Lady, keep an eye on the scattered scrub trees, the ones with the tuberous leaves shining black in the moonlight. You have a 50% chance of finding one of three plants growing by the bush, which might aid your Lady.

**Arming Flower:** Small yellow flower with long stem. It's scent dulls the mind.

**Mildmoss:** Pale green moss growing on bark. Slows toxins, venoms, and poisons.

**Shield-o'-Fahud:** A button-like weed with red petals. Can be chewed for pain relief.

name: Setup - Blob 5
text:
Out of the night a lone light shines. At first you think it is a reflection of the moon on a body of water. Or, a gleam in an eye. You peer through your visor.

The gleam is not a reflection, nor an eye, but a window in a large, round, dark shape. A hut on the moor.

As you stumble nearer the dwelling, you see a small fenced garden, and a nearby pond. Then a lighted seam opens in the side of the hut. The crack of a door. A second later something passes over the crack, blocking the glow cast on the grass.

name: Setup - Blob 6
text:
"Gah! Gah. Gah.

"Two hornless kids stumbling through the bush?

"Oh my bones. Oh this poor poisoned girlie.

"Bring her in, sir. I have cures.

"Hehehehehe!"

name: Conflict - Blob 1
text:
When you step across the threshhold, the first thing you notice is the slimy air. The inside of the hut is scented like spring berries, and sticks like spiderwebs on your skin. Elk's Ears plants hang in a drying row from one drooping rafter. A lone lantern, hanging behind the Ears, shedding all the buttery light in the dark hut, casts the shadow of the Ears over your chestplate as you carry your lady inside.

A few other items attract your eye.

**Silver Beetle Relief:** Projected on a flat sheet of shining steel, the horn of this beetle follows you wherever you walk.

**Moss Basket:** A basket full of various mosses. You recognize at least one helpful and one poisonous cultivar.

**Dreamcatcher:** Woven of yarn; hangs above the window; changes shape whenever you look at it.

**Mangrove Table:** The kitchen table appears to be the stump of a large mangrove tree, turned upside down, and with all the roots woven into a roughly flat tangle.

**Stone Cauldron:** Sits above the firepit, in a far corner of the hut. The bottom is blackened with soot. If you rub some of the soot away, you find a symbol you do not recognize - a star with eleven drooping points.

name: Conflict - Blob 2
text:
The hut's lone occupant - a woman with more wrinkles than the crust of the earth - speaks.

"How white is your woman. She's not long for this Time. Minutes of life left in her bones.

"I've seen that color on skin before. Hehehehehe. You wayfarers never do take care where you lay your bedroll, never throw the haunch of meat that keeps the bugs distracted.

"Lay her on the table, knight.

"And lay your scabbard and that shining blade there too. No sword's borne in my home. Nooooo, too wily for you, aren't I? Hehehehehe."

name: Conflict - Blob 3
text:
The old woman watches you warily.

*Can you trust this remote dweller on the moor? Don't attempt to fool yourself. You know only wild men and witches live in places like this - where the wind means forever, and rain and snow and spider wolves are one's sole company. And the wild men keep no huts...*

*Feel how your Lady breathes in your arms. Shallow. Slow. The woman, witch or not, says truthfully that your Lady will not live for long.*

*Your must serve your Lady. Which is the best way?*

name: Conflict - Blob 4
text:
You must choose either to give up your Steel to this old woman, or with it cut her down. Ultimately, if you don't lay your Lady and weapon on the table, your Lady will perish.  You can try to search the old woman's hut for healing medicines yourself, but it is hopeless. You need her assistance.

While the old woman has insisted that you lay your Polished Steel Longsword on the table, she has not mention your Pinecone Crest Shield. Keep it strapped to your arm. You'll need it soon.

name: Conflict - Blob 5
text:
Your scabbard seems almost to leap from your hand and adhere to the rooty table as you lay it beside your lady. The old woman chuckles whispily.

"One should not heed strangers without care.

"Hehehehehe.

"I will cure your sickly woman. But, how uncomfortably you shuffle there in your steel plates. She's not your woman, but your lady, eh?

"She's a pretty woman. Not much pink in her cheeks, and not young, but young enough."

name: Conflict - Blob 6
text:
At this point the old hut dweller will present you with one of three tasks.

**The Sandbook:** She asks you to retrieve a book bound in white leather, which is buried in the earth, in the crawl space under the hut. You will have to maneuver around three venomous rattlesnakes that nest there.

**Chimney Sweep:** Something lives in the chimney of the hut. You will need to climb up, hunt it down, and kill it. The chimney is vast and cavernous on the inside, and no light can shine there; you will have to rely on senses other than sight to slay whatever is lurking there. Even if you succeed, you are unable to drag your quarry back into the light.

**A Pattern in the Stars:** A bowl-shaped, crystalline ornament sits on the sill of the hut's lone, rounded window. You will be asked to capture a reflection of a shooting star in the facets. A tip - don't look at the bowl while you are capturing the star. It's light will blind you.

You have no choice but to perform the task you receive in order to progress the healing of your Lady.

name: Conflict - Blob 7
text:
Upon completion of your task you find the old woman bent crookedly over your Blue Lady, her thin grey hair tickling your sleeping Lady's face. When the old woman notices you, she scowls.

"Done already? Impossible. But then, you valiants were always capable at such set tasks? And not a mark on your sight or your skin?

"I should have taken the advice of that hypnotist on the yellow moor. Never a task *inside* the-

"Your Lady will soon be well.

"She will be thirty on waking. There is a pool, outside, beneath the heavy and leafless elm. You cannot miss it. Here. Take this bucket and fetch your parched Lady the silver water to wet her lips."

name: Conflict - Blob 8
text:
The door groans against the wind as the old woman shut it behind you. You stepp toward the shining reflection of the moon on the pool, held between the black branch-fingers of the tree.

As you bend over, you see your own reflection. Your armor, your helmet. Not your face. It is too hideous.

You have just bent to collect the water when you hear a shuyffling from behind.

The hut charges you. It towers over your head now, running on two giant goat-legs. You raise your shield just in time to catch the hoof on your Pinecone Crest. The kick sends you hurtling into the pool.

You sink.

name: Drive - Blob 1
text:
**Witches:**

Witches, cursewives, harridans; the creatures who dwell in the uninhabited corners of the wilderness have many names in The Time of Dying. They are a peculiarity of nature. They are neither human, nor undead, nor animal, but sometimes wear the features of all three. Sometimes they appear as old women. Sometimes, as great black half-wolves. Sometimes with dripping grey flesh, breathless, bloodless.

Their magic is profane - not necromancy. Old. It is said their spells can cure ills beyond the means of godly power. Even so, few are those who willingly deal with them. A deal with a witch always ends badly in the stories.

name: Drive - Blob 2
text:
Gasping, you crawl from the pond. The wet mud of the pond's shore swallows your gountlets as you grasp and pull yourself forward. At last you reach a root of the leafless tree. You find solid ground. You spend a moment bent, sucking in the cold night air.

As you stand, you scan the space. The witch's hut is nowhere in sight. Witch, hut, and your Blue Lady with them, have vanished onto the moor. The bright yellow Harridan's Moon lets you see far, but there is only the vast plain of tall grass.

Then something catches your eye. You march against the wind to a patch of flattened heather. Looking about, you quickly spot a trail of similar patches. Footprints, you realize.

They lead north.

name: Drive - Blob 3
text:
*You let yourself believe the witch. That beetle poisoned your Lady because you lacked care in your service. Now this monster has her, for the same reason.*

*How could you let yourself fall for such a storied trick? The old woman in the lonely place.*

*If your Blue Lady dies, it marks the certain failure of an oath. Your greatest oath.*

*Have you not failed enough?*

name: Drive - Blob 4
text:
as you begin your march, following the trail of giant goat-prints in the tall grass, the cold will be your first peril. You can use your Pinecone Crest Shield to block the biting wind, but the air still slips through the creases in your armor. Your wet wool is like a frozen tongue against your skin.

Keep your shield raised anyway. You'll need it.

Your first warning of second danger is lone howl. It whistles like birdsong over the moaning wind. You know the sound, though you wish you did not.

Spider Wolves.

**The Wolfpack Fight:**

This will be one of the more difficult fight's in your adventure, mostly because you have only your Shield to fight with. The wolves skitter in through the rustling grass. They attack in duos and trios, coming in at crossed sides with each attack. Their greyblack fur, thin limbs, and long, low bodies make them hard to spot when they lunge from the grass.

Lacking your Polished Steel Longsword, your best strategy will be shield bashing. Your platemail should spare you from the pincer bites of the wolves when they attack from varied angles. Brace your shield in the direction of one of the lunging wolves. After it rebounds off your Pinecone Crest, and after you recover from the stagger of the bites at your back and flanks, bring the edge of your shield down on one of your attacker's bulbous, chitinous heads. You can usually kill each wolf in one blow.

After you kill six of the Spider Wolves, the rest will flee.

name: Drive - Blob 5
text:
*Ahhhh.*

*One of the beasts nipped through the chain behind your knee. You feel the sting, worse than cold. It is bleeding.*

*But your relationship with pain is different. Since your body cracked. You can march through it. You will.*

*Will you die here on the moor? You would have no burial under a stone on The Road. An unknown grave.*

*So be it. If your lady has no stone over her bones, should you?*

name: Drive - Blob 6
text:
You wander north across the windchilled, moonlit moor for what feels like days. The moon remains fixed in its place among the stars, as if that golden face were painted on the blackly purple sky. A nightjar follows you, trilling in your wake.

Your fingers and toes fall numb. You press on.

At last, a black mass rises ahead of you. It is a mound, topped in the silhouettes of pines. Among them, you see a familiar, cone-topped round shadow. The witch's hut. It has squatted down. It's windowlight matches the yellow of the moon.

You raise your shield and proceed up the hill.

name: Finale - Blob 1
text:
As you reach for the knobby handle of the hut door, you hear a deep, growling voice within.

"Not young. Not youthful as I might have wished. But young enough is she.

"Never have I worn skin so white with hair so bluish-black.

"A pretty one I shall be. Hehehehehe. Pretty in a way.

"At least she was easily had."

name: Finale - Blob 2
text:
The knob of the door refuses to turn.

Your steelclad foot opens the way.

As you step inside you see that the entire hut has altered. The soot-blacked hearth looks as if it exploded outward; the fire-poker is embedded in the opposite wall. Though the round window seemed small on the outside, it now dominates the wall to your left like a huge glassy eye. The yellow harridan's moon streams through like a hot sun.

Where the mangrove-root once made a table, there now stands a tall upright stone. It is covered in chalky, rose-colored runes.

Your blue lady lies on soft moss at the base of the stone. She is in some kind of stasis. Her eyes are open, and she breaths, but she shows no reaction to your bursting entry.

name: Finale - Blob 3
text:
**The Witch-Beast of Windy Moor:**

The old woman of the hut, or a creature that is her monstrous reflection, stands over your Blue Lady. She turns, snarling at you. Her eyes are small and black like a bug's. Her wide nose flares with wroth.

Like an ancient hound, her body is now covered in shaggy grey hair. She has tripled, perhaps quadrupled in size. Her hunched posture and longer forearms make her look like she crawled from the first dawns of the world. All about her there is a musk of the shadowed undergrowth of old forests.

In her left claw, she waves your Polished Steel Longsword.

name: Finale - Blob 4
text:
The fight begins with the witch-beast lunging. She'll immediately lay into you with your own Steel. (Now is a rare moment when you'll regret keeping the weapon oiled and sharp.)

You'll have to block or dodge her strikes. Her power is astounding. Even strikes you block with your shield send lightning pain through your cold-numbed fingers.

Your goal in the first part of this fight is to simply keep moving. Take as few hits as possible, and let your body warm up.

name: Finale - Blob 5
text:
The witch will eventually stop to catch her breath. Take that as the signal to go on the offense.

Your best bet is retrieving the fire-poker embedded in the wall. It's not much of a weapon, but it's something. Keep dodging the hag's swipes (they're slower and easy to avoid now). Whenever she opens up her ribcage as she follows through a swing, capitalize.

name: Finale - Blob 6
text:
As her ribs bleed and crack, the witch becomes desperate and dangerous. She will speak words that are not necromancy.

Curses.

The witch will try to lay her magic on your head. Strike fast to interrupt her curses. A single one getting through will likely spell your end (petrification, sleep, skin rot, etc).

While she curses, the witch's long tongue will loll out. This is her most vital point. It's a narrow target, but you'll need to spear her tongue with the fire poker.

When her tongue is speared the witch will howl. She'll drop your sword and try to pull the poker out.

From here, the battle is won. Quickly retrieve your sword, raise it, and sever her head.

As the witch-beast falls, your lady's trance breaks. She blinks, rising.

She is healthy once more.

name: Finale - Blob 7
text: In the Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 1
text:
*The city wakes already, though the light of the slate-colored sun has yet to drop over the ravine's crooked lip. All the white marble roofs are still in shadow. The pedestrians though, they carry their lamps in the early hours. How much easier they are to follow in the hours of dark.*

*Is one of those lamps your Lady?*

*You should not have let her step out alone. No matter that she asked you to stay in this house. By now, you may be certain of danger in Middlemoss.*

*Certain too, that she will not find her stepson here.*

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 2
text:
The sky above the Middlemoss ravine is wet with lightning. From your place at the window, if you look down past spackled window of your mausoleum house, you might spot any of the following passing on the road.

**Half-Nose:** The man you see in fact has no nose. Only a tall, thin hole below his left eye.

**Girl Dragging Canoe:** She walks with the front half of the vessel held aloft in her skinny arms. The stern rattled on the paving stones behind her. The bow, like an arching beak, protects her from the rain.

**The Coffee Seller:** He hold his lamp high in one arm, and shakes a big jar of dark beans in the other.

**Living Bones:** At you first you think the upright walking skeleton might be The Late Poet. On second glance, however, you notice that the hate is purple and gold, not blue and orange.

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 3
text:
As you watch Middlemoss's citizens shuffle by below, one young woman in a rainsoaked blouse of stone-colored cotton catches your attention. She shouts from cupped hands as she passes by.

"They who live in in the silence of the coffin approach. Clear the road, all!

"Make way for they from The Tower of Silence.

"Make way for The Monks of The Lidless. They make observance out of their Tower.

"Pull those pigs aside. You behind the cairn; wipe off that grave soil from your scalp, you louse!"

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 4
text:
*Day must be well underway, if the monks are out. You should go-*

*No, there she is on the bridge. Your Blue Lady. You cannot see her face, through the rain, beneath her cloak. But you do not need to. You know her expression is sorrowful.*

*If by strength and faith you could make her smile, you would.*

*Strength and faith - they cannot conjure her stepson.*

*He is not here.*

name: 1. Title Card - Blob 5
text:
By the time you notice the threat, it will be too late.

As your Lady approaches the end of the bridge, the line of Monks comes into view. They are four in number, hidden under oilskin cloaks.

Your Lady stops and waits. The monks, however, suddenly turn in her direction.

Before you can react, your lady is grabbed and taken.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 1
text:
Retrieving your Polished Steel Longsword, emerging from the quiet mausoleum onto the busy, wet Middlemoss thoroughfare, you find that that the Monks of the Lidless have disappeared.

Your first step should be to the nearest woodplank bridge spanning the Middlemoss chasm. (Reminder: never look down when crossing the chasm.) You don't know where The Tower of Silence lies, but you saw the monks crossing the bridge themselves.

After you cross, you'll want to plunge into the streets burrowing into the ravine's rugged face. The only lights are the lamps on passing pedestrians, and the occasional small yellow candle lightning a lintel above a vault. You can either use the Light of your Cat's Eye Spear, or stick to the pedestrians.

Regardless of how you enter, you'll soon become lost in Middlemoss's dense suburbs. Don't try to find the tower alone. Ask for direction.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 2
text:
**Valance the Rat:**

Only a little way into the warren, you will come abruptly helm-to-face with a whiskered man. He is tall, but crooked, so that you see eye-to-eye. He wears a great velvet robe that draped his long spine like a cloth over a table.

"Scratching in my turf, knight?

"Ah! Keep that sharp weapon sheathed, please. Nothing meant by it. I promise.

"I know what you're after, after all. The Tower of Silence. But how can you find a Tower in here?

"There's shiny treasures buried here, in Deep Compost. Bring me a treasure. I'll tell you where to find The Tower."

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 3
text:
You can find a few different treasures within the Deep Compost burg that will satisfy Valance.

**Steel Gargoyle:** Hidden beneath a tangle of dead brown roots over an abandoned chrypt house. No one cares if you take it.

**Glowbug in Amber:** The shopkeeper who sells this curio tells you that a necromancer imbued the bug inside with un-life before freezing it in amber. Unliving or not, the bug glows brightly.

**Old Blacksmith's Cinder:** In a tomb in a far corner of Deep Compost, you will find a dry, crumbling mummy, wrapped around a glowing piece of slag. Despite it's glow, the metal is quite cool.

Alternatively, instead of bringing Valance a treasure, you can consult with the Haruspex, Joole. Her shop in in Deep Compost. The price she demands is steep; Three stamped squares of Lady Horsehair's gold. If you choose to pay, she will consult the ghosts of her prophetic organs, and give you The Tower's Location.

name: 2. Arrival of the Foe - Blob 4
text:
Once you have your directions, run. You may be approached by a fallen knight seeking a duel as you march through the dark burg. Ignore him. You may pass a curious well with no lip, and a familiar lordly face in the water below. Ignore it. A stream of knock-kneed girls may beg for your Steel's aid. Ignore them.

Your Lady is in danger.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Blob 1
text:
You emerge into a monumental cavern. You never never knew a space of this size existed beyond the main Middlemoss ravine.

The room is a landscape and a skyscape. Three layers of hill-like rock rise until they meet the cavern walls. On the ceiling, wispy, bioluminescent red vines hang like glowing clouds. Like tassels of braided hair, they sway in a warm breeze blowing from a high cross-tunnel. 

The red light shines down on pale mausoleums. Homes and shops, these marble structures dot the hills. One is a wainwright, and from it you hear the *tap, tap, tap* of a wooden peg being hammered into place. On another hill there is a small ampitheater. You see a trio of ballet dancers performing for a crowd.

At the back of the cavern, however, on a hill with only one building, there is total silence.

name: 3. Enter the Arena - Blob 2
text:
**The Tower of Silence:**

Shining brightly black like oil, reflecting the scarlet ceiling vines; the Tower of Silence waits. It rises from the farthest hill in the cavern. It stands alone. It punctures the stony roof.

You spot the tower just in time to watch a pair of monks enter. They push your lady before them. The steel tower door slams. With a soundless click, somehow, you know that door is locked.

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 1
text:
You might consider charging the front door.

You won't consider long, however, before a familiar voice reaches you from the shade beneath a nearby pediment.

**R the Killer:**

"I knew you'd not be far behind. When I saw those monks dragger that daydream-lady of yours behind them, I said, 'The Steelclad'll come any minute now.'

"The front's no good. I've seen. Hundred of those tattooed silent freaks inside. Hundred, at least.

"Don't sulk though, I've found another way inside. There an underground. A Wine Chrypt. Old. Silent, *maybe* empty.

"Me? Hehe, don't worry your shiny helm about it. I just need that shiny sword of yours. In case it's *not* empty.

"Well? Team?"

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 2
text:
Follow R the Killer and you'll eventually reach a web-packed alleyway deep in the Middlemoss catacombs. From there, the path is dusty and dim. Bring a candle.

**The Wine Chrypt:**

The Mailed Flayers and other high servants of the seven god-necromancers spoke words to summon spirits down in these dark vaults, long before the modern Time of Dying. These ancients needed their food (and drink) the same as modern occupants, however, and they kept their liquor in this vault.

Nowadays, The Monks of the Lidless send to this Chrypt their outcasts; monks gone blind. Wrapped in blankets of owl-feathers, bellies distended from the ancient wine that is their only comfort, these blind monks pound and lumber through the corridors under The Towers of Silence.

A word of caution: don't open any of the barrels. They are filled with a stinking, sour, putrid grey fluid. You expect the wine serves the order in other ways than drink.

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 3
text:
The best way to progress through the tomb is to dart between the bulging casks of banded oak. Let R lead, he is quieter. You can slip past several of the stomping monks in this way, and avoid several stamina-draining battles.

You'll eventually reach a long and wide hall, with three rows of pillars studded in iron hooks. Curtains of varicolored silk line the black space. Sleeping space. No matter how stealthy you've been up to this point, the sharp ears of the blind monks will now catch your tread. Be ready for serious fight. Anywhere from two to eight monks may be in the room at the time. There are three kinds of blind Monk of The Lidless, with their eye tattoos identify the different kinds.

**Tuskhands:** Watch of for these ones. You'll know them by the bristling, many-lashed, yellow eye-tattoos over their own eyelids. Their fleshy haymaker punches can put solid dent in your rusted platemail.

**Man of Dogs:** The eye-tattoos on this one's body seem to glisten, as if each is beneath a shiny droplet of saliva.

**Leaping Monk:** Former wrestlers of the order, probably, these monks will charge you headlong before jumping and drying to crush you under their flabby mass.

name: 4. False Hopes - Blob 4
text:
As the last monk falls, R the Killer flick a string of crimson pulp from his horned morningstar. He looks angry.

"It's that silly scraping steel you're wearing, tinman.

"You'd stick out in the middle of a cymbal concerto. Forget a colony of freaks with high-strung hearing.

"Well, it's done. Let's hope no one else heard.

"Come on. We must be nearly under the tower now."

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Blob 1
text:
From the room of curtains and pillars you'll enter a wide vault-like chamber, divided by broad stone arches carved with the faces of staring owls.

Despite R's desire, your battle has drawn the attention of other outcast monks. You'll find yourselves facing a charging quintet of rotund, wordless attackers. Their footsteps shake the stones. This group has had time to gather weapons; two carry sickles, and the rest bear carpenter's hammers. Though blind, they're able to pick out your movements with surprising accuracy.

Five against two would be hard odds. Fortunately, both you and R are better equipped (and better trained) for melee. Your best strategy will be to retreat to just behond one of the arching supports. This narrows the avenue of attack. The quintet will only be able to attack in duos and trios. It's best if you take the right flank; you can then use your shield to block blows aimed at either yourself or R. R's signature move is to crush and tear the kneecaps of his foes with his horned morningstar. Whether they lower their guard to block, or take the hit and buckle, then is your chance to finish each monk off with a clean head strike.

With the monks dead, proceed to the next room in the Chrypt.

name: 5. Trapped, Battle Dance - Blob 2
text:
**The Senseless King and Senseless Queen:**

By the light you carry, you can see that the floor of this room is tiled in alternating squares of dandelion-yellow and ruby-red. At first, the room seems empty.

Then two of the tiles slowly rise.

name: 6. The Master Appears - Blob 1
text:
From the two black pits in the floor two "monks" emerge. You aren't entirely certain that they are of the same order.

It is a man and woman. Their flesh is thin, their limbs skinny. Inked eyes of owls, goats, snakes, spiders, and a hundred other creatures coat their faces. They each wear a silver crown, and a loose white robe. Each holds a scepter topped in an iron hook.

Though their real eyes are shut, both their faces turn toward you. Their lips peal back like snarling dogs; their yellow, square teeth show; yet their snarl is silent.

name: 7. The Enemy Triumphs - Blob 1
text:
The two false monarchs will raise their scepters in unison. Then, they slowly level them at you.

Then, they attack.

You will need all your skill beat this blind pair. Each has a different attack pattern. The queen uses lightning-fast jabs, two-to-four in sequence, followed by a single swipe of her hooked instrument. Watch out for the hook; it can easily rip your shield away. The king only uses swings (also fast) but from a variety of different angles.

Your best tactic is to parry their blows while stepping back. Blocking wors, but they recover so quickly that it gives you no time to strike back.

When your Steel first bites papery skin, it has little effect. Despite their skinny frames, these two are unliving, and surprisingly sturdy.

R can distract one of the two monarchs, but his horned morningstar is less effective than your sword. Additionally, the pair slide about, as if on ice-skates, and you'll find yourself switching opponents often.

The key to the fight is to knock the scepter from the hand of the king or queen. When you do, the deathless monk monarch will bend to retrieve their symbol. From there, it's a simple beheading.

Take care. Killing one is half the battle. Even outnumbered, the king and queen are still fierce fighters.

...

As the last monarch falls, full silence resumes.

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 1
text:
Finding a circling stair at the back of the checkered chamber, you ascend.

*How dangerous must the Monks of the Lidless be if these are their outcasts? These ones in this Chrypt have gorged on sickening wine. Half alive; yet they fought like rhinos.*

*It is not a life you should ever aspire to, though it grants some hidden strength. Blind and drunk; and, existing in a silence that is like deafness.*

*What must be their thoughts? Abstract and spare; a psychological inner space; a place of no oaths, no "next duty".*

*Where do they store their dead? Surely, you have never seen the epitaph 'Monk of the Lidless' on the stones-*

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 2
text:
R the Killer's hiss breaks your contemplation.

"Don't make so much clamor as we're getting into the Tower proper.

"It's your fault. Their blood's on your conscience, Steelclad.... Fairly enough; it is dripping from my Horned Star.

"We'll fetch your girl yet from this secret Tower. These mum ones up here can't have noticed us yet. They'd have thrown the bolt on that heavy oak door we stepped through just now.

"Recall I have my own goads. These monks have a mirror, it is said, that will quiet the thoughts. Worth a penny to man with night terrors and too-full pockets. I have the buyer; I need the mirror."

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 3
text:
*This killer tells you has a patron. Should you trust his word? You have seen him fight. He lays men out for the grave with no care for the wholeness of their soul.*

*That is what you have heard. That is what the man himself says, that he is a killer. Yet never has he wronged you. Your grail and his have been of the same metal. You have taken upon your Pinecone Crest Shield a blow or two that would have slain the killer, and he has killed men who meant to kill you.*

*Is it right for you, with your shaky soul in your cracked-cup body, to judge the man?*

*The way he speaks about your Lady; that alone is cause enough to doubt this killer. His weapon as well. Only heartless men carry the horned morningstars.*

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 4
text:
As you reach the three-hundred and thirtieth step of the spiral staircase, you emerge from a floor well. Peeking carefully up, you find an intersection of four halls.

Pick a path and start sneaking.

**The Silk Carpet Path:**

Here your footsteps make little sound. A ribbon-pattern of blues and whites stretches around the curving corners of these corridors. Most of the rooms are private dormitories for the monks. Each room has a strange podium in it, with a steel statue of a different bird perched on each pedastal. You can take exactly one statue; the owl is the most valuable. Taking more then one will attract a curse from the ancient Starshadow which ensures the sanctity of the tower.

From the moment you first tread on the silken carpet, the correct sequence of turns to take is: right, left, straight, straight, up the first stairs on your left, right, through the door with bronze bands (past the first door, with horizontal copper bands), straight.

It is worth following R's directive to make less sound. Pad your armor with a bolt of cloth from one of the monks' chambers if you can. Drawing the attention of the monks is sure to be fatal. You will have to dodge a few patrols, but the halls are only dimly lit, and there are alcoves with benches that you can hide in.

name: 8. Introduction - Blob 5
text:
**The Tranquil Theater:**

The silken carpets end abruptly at a flat stone wall and a T intersection. Take either path, they go to the same place.

You find yourself in a huge, ovular room. The cieling is black above you. There are balconies lining the walls.

Rows and rows of stepped chairs fall away before you. All the chairs face a stage, hidden behind a thing, greying curtain.

The chairs all sport pairs of shackles. In one, your Blue Lady is bound.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 1
text:
Your Lady raises her head and turns her neck toward you.

"I hoped you'd seen them take me. I hoped you would a way inside.

"Such a burden am I to you, my knight. I've drawn the both of us into this shaded town, for what? For that crack I see on your vambrace?

"I see. You came with another. That man we met before. Is blood on his morningstar for my sake?

"These metal clasps hold my wrists against this chair. I heard the monks step behind that curtain before the locks clicked shut. The mechanism must be back there, though I cannot say what else you will find.

"Please, release me. It is past time we set our feet on The Road."

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 2
text:
You'll need to go behind the thin grey curtain that hangs before the stage. There is a small gap between it and the wall, by the short stairs on the left. The space behind is unlit, so bring a candle. R will refuse to follow you.

Behind the grey cloth, the stage is stacked with objects precious to the Monks of the Lidless. A blanket of dust tells you that few feet tread here. Watch out for the rats with quills bristling across their backs, which crawl among the items. They are harmless, if not provoked.

A lever which unlocks the clasps in the chairs will release your lady. The lever is hidden under a shroud. Take care; each covering pulls away in a cloud of dust, and each cloud of dust has a fifty-percent chance of inflicting you with one of the following diseases.

**Dustheels:** Every step is agony.

**The Red King's Curse:** You know this bloody doom too well.

**Meerie's Weeping Affliction:** A torrent of endless tears blurs your vision.

**Sccattles:** Shaking and shivering, your numb extremities move as if in slow motion. Last a year minus one day.

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 3
text:
When you step out from behind the curtain, your Lady stands. She dusts her blouse. R applauds, silently.

"I'd never set my toes in the dark and sacred place of a monastic stage. But you seem living still.

"Well done, steelclad.

"I heard a resurrection man in one of the drinkhouses say that this tower's got an owlery. They say it crosses a skybridge, and opens onto the heath.

"My square coins say our safest route outs up, not back through those deformed freaks in the cellar. Before we step too far, though, I'll recall to your-

name: 9. Foes Revel, Heroes Plot - Blob 4
text:
R continues to speak for a second, by the motion of his lips - but no sound emerges.

From the two doors of the T intersection that leads into the theater, a slow light spreads, like lime-colored flower petals creeping over the stone. You, your Lady, and R, duck at the seats.

The lime-colored light broadens on the left passage. Its petals spread over the walls of the theater. You see now that the walls are shiny, covered in a sap-like slime.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Blob 1
text:
**Eleven Strokes with The Old Man Below :**

A floating lambent orb slides into the room. Your skin prickles, you recognize the sphere.

R the Killer knows too, and he runs.

The glowing orb zips like a bolt. You pull your Lady down low. Soundless, the light passes.

You sprint from the row. Between the chairs.

Again; zip.

You turn.

Block.

name: 10. The Second Conflict - Blob 2
text:
**Will-O-Wisp:**

The sense-killing Will-O-Wisp is one of the deadliest of the dead. Small wonder The Monks of the Lidless bind them to guard their Silent Tower.

Abandon stealth for the present. Your first priority must be escape. The Will-O-Wisp moves like a shot arrow, darting brightly. Never look directly at the orb; anything longer than a glance will blind you.

On the positive side, the muffling nature of this glowing hot soul means you're unlikely to make much noise until you escape. If you escape. Your only chance it to block it's frequent charges, whenever you "feel" that vibration in the air. Your sword is worthless here; the Wisp is incorporeal, the only way to destroy one is through necromancy.

Fortunately, Wisps wear themselves down quickly, and dim as they do. This one's charges will becomes infrequent as its energy is expended. If you can manage to block six or seven shots, you should survive.

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Blob 1
text:
You are forced to stop at catch your breath. Between gasps, your Lady whispers.

"You were quick with my family crest, and on your feet, my valorant knight.

"But luck also blessed us.

"Othelmedir once told me only a fool or a master tries to subjugate a Will-O-Wisp; their light makes them too dangerous.

"I've seen mutes no fools among these Monks."

name: 11. The Relieved Breath - Blob 2
text:
R, swallowing past his own labor, gives your Lady a cunning look.

"By my soul, this can't be the same Lady you serve, steelclad.

"This one's not so dreamy in her brain, not so senseless in what she says.

"She's right. Something worse than tongue-tied celibates must be cpataining that glowing spirit.

"There could be more too. Try to clomp softly this time..."

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 1
text:
There are three different routes you can take to reach The Owlery in The Tower of Silence.

**Chain Lift:** You step out onto the corner-landing of a stairway. It creeps round the wall of a massive square room. The floor and ceiling are hidden in blackness. There is a single chair, too far out to reach, hanging in the central space. If you choose to follow this rout, you will want to first head down three flights, then back up seven. The door on the tenth flight up will always lead to The Owlery. (Warning: watch out for frenzied bat swarms here).

**Landscape Wing:** This long series of corridors is lined in broad windows, with thick green glass and heavy satin drapes. You know that you are underground, yet you see upon entering that each green glass window shines with light, and that each light is a different shade. Each window looks out on a different landscape: mountains, rovers, woods. Take care here. Looking out of certain windows will draw the attention of the Will-O-Wisps that live therein.

**The Scriptorium:** You find a T-inserction. The floor to your left and right both curve up and away. You soon realize two things. First, the hall is a circle. Second; gravity keeps pace with you as you walk the hall. The walls are lined in shelves of books, each book penned in the language of silence. Each door in the hall connects to another random door. You will have to try a few doors before finding the path to The Owlery.

No matter which route you take, you yourself should take the front. Your Pinecone Crest Shield and rusted armor give you the best chance of withstanding a surprise attack, should you meet any of the monks or their guardian light wisps. Let R walk behind your Lady, guarding the rear, and listening for following footsteps.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 2
text:
At some point as you traverse the Tower, you are likely to stumble into the monks' room of relics. This room hold the mirror R desires.

You step into an ovular chamber flooded in four inches of water. Waist-high marble pillars stick up from the shallow pool, each supporting a relic.

When you enter you'll find the room guarded by a heavily-eye-tattooed monk, wielding a man-length clever, and dressed in a cook's apron. He's not too difficult a battle, but he hits like a battering ram. R can dispatch this foe, or you can do so yourself.

Besides the mirror, which R gravitates toward, the room contains: a Sturdy Gambeson, a Scroll of Centipedes, and a Green Potion.

R will hesitate before retrieving his mirror. He inspects all around that pillar first. Finally he will reach out and take the item. He examines his reflection for only a moment, before frowning and tucking the item away.

name: 12. The Hero and the Master - Blob 3
text:
However you arrive, when you finally reach the Owlery door at the tower's summit, R will check your hand as you reach for the beak-like knob.

"Test your steel's swing first, knight.

"And you, *dear lady* should stand back.

"These monks have their callouses, their tough skin. But any fool with sense puts his fists up where he's most vulnerable.

"My square coins say this'll be well-guarded. Get ready."

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 1
text:
You step through the door.

Your Lady's cloak whips out behind her. The room is high and dusty. There is light; you see smoke swirling up from four piles of clumped candles, little flames flickering like cluster's of yellow spider-eyes on the floor.

Your sabatons squelch into a floor littered with huge droppings. The stink pollutes your helmet.

Above, catwalks and beams and rafters for a spiderweb of wood. You see a single portal, like a barn window, shining with a different light. The light of day.

A moving shadow catches your attention.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 2
text:
**The Lidless:**

Massive, never-blinking, like a heavy cloud in the dark; The Lidless swoops from above. It is a spotted owl demented and mutated, huge and stretched, with a face half-human and half-bird. But the eyes - all those eyes across its feathers, the two shining orbs on its face - they are the hunter's eyes.

The Lidless are malevolent winged female half-demon, born from two half-souls. They usually haunt churches, towers, and barns.

This one is unusually gigantic.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 3
text:
The fight begins with the lidless spinning as it enters its first dive. It turns its back to oyu, and you see the light-up spots across its broad wings. Keep your eyes off of this display; like the Will-O-Wisp, it will blind you. The Lidless will try this maneuver throughout the fight.

Dodging through the space with characterize the first stage of this battle. The owl droppings slow your tread. Keep away from the stone coffins scattered about the room. These are the graves of powerful blind necromancers, and they will afflict you with weakness and sickness.

Your goal here is to endure. After a few minutes, The Lidless will rise suddenly and screech. It is only at that moment that you will realize how silent was the fight until now. Your head rings, and the second stage begins.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 4
text:
You'll want to head ofr the nearest stairs up into the catwalks. Keep a look out for huge, falling splinters. The Lidless will stop swooping, wary of your sword, and try to use the environment against With its jaw-like beak of square teeth it tears chunks from the huge wooden beams. IT can hurl these with startling accuracy.

Once you and R have risen to the catwalks, you can start positioning yourself to strike at the swooping half-demon. Neither your Steel nor R's horned morningstar inflict meaningful damage. Something heavier is called for.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 5
text:
The key to beating The Lidless is to use its own weapons against it. Either remain in the catwalks yourself, or have R do so. This will encourage the monster to stay lower, near the floor. One of you must remain below, and draw The Lidless's attacks. Specifically, lure it near one of the Blind Man Coffins.

Once The Lidless is afflected with illness, it will be forced to land. You can strike more easily now, although the feathers are surprisingly scale-like and resistant. It's possible to kill the monster with weapons, but not likely. You will probably fail and die.

The better course from here is to draw The Lidless beneath one of hte beams it has lossened. When it is in place, either you or R can crack the huge piece of wood loose, sending it plummeting.

When the huge beams drops The Lidless attempts a last swoop.

Too late.

The broad beam punctures the owl-demon like a ballista bolt. It emits one last half-screech. Then the silence is complete.

With The Lidless dead, you are free ot proceed to the outer skywalk.

Leave The Tower of Silence.

name: 13. The Finale - Blob 6
text: In The Time of Dying lies a Road of Graves.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 1
text:
Roots curl like the nails of a hundred ancient hags - or, perhaps, like a newborn clutch of snakes - between the manifold oaks of The Buried forest. The roots have pried apart the epitaph stones of The Road of Graves where it wends the woods. The cobbled surface is broken. The epitaphs face the sky at crooked angles.

Somewhere in the woods comes a steady *shink, shink, shink*. A shovel in earth.

Two men in sweat-stained leather stand at the edges of a fresh hole in the earth. One carries a six-foot pole with a hook at the end. The other has a club. Guards.

A third man stands waste-high in the hole, his arms working a steady labor with his shovel. Each swing sprays with earth. An epitaph stone lies facedown, its mossy bottom open to the forest canopy, a few feet from the hole.

The dig man stops. He raises a calloused, dirty hand to his ear.

From the trees; *shinking* Steel.

...

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 2
text:
The hilt or your Polished Steel Longsword feels mortared in your palm as you step from the screen of a warty oak. The three stonecats have seen you.

Though your Blue Lady, alone back at The Scattered Shrine, might trouble your mind, you must now push those cares aside. These men pry stones from the Road. Your chivalry swears you to cut them down.

Their sentence is passed.

Thre three will attack together. None are particularly skilled fighters. If you sever the hook from the one man's pole, then kill the stonecat with the shovel (who can ring your helmet) you'll swell the advantage given by your skill.

This should be an easy first fight; for one strong and dauntless.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 3
text:
**The Gonging Hearse:**

A desperate tolling clamors sharply over the uneven Road. Sliding your Steel from the breast of your last foe, you wheel. You're shield rises.

Atop ten articulated legs struts a carriage of greying wood. The legs are woven with living sinew; the box, armored in bone-plate. Beneath the box hangs a bell of weathered bronze. It clangs with each step.

The necromancer atop the box steers his towering construct toward you. It moved like a hunting spider. You know it will crush you. You step to the trees, but the carriage is faster. You cannot escape.

From behind a trunk another figure steps. A horned morningstar swings out. Catches a leg.

Crack.

The Hearse stumbles.

name: 1 - Battle (Mars) - Blob 4
text:
With R the Killer's help you stand a chance. His Horned Star is more effective than your Steel against the undead machine.
